Options A and C are incorrect because hosting the visualization tool on premises forces the full 50 MB result sets to leave AWS for every query, generating the highest data egress; over the internet (A) this is the most expensive, and even over Direct Connect (C) it transfers far more billable data than option D. Option B is incorrect because, although the result sets stay in-Region, delivering the webpages to users over the public internet incurs standard internet egress rates, which are higher than the Direct Connect rates used in option D.

152.text:
Option C is correct because the multivalue answer routing policy lets Amazon Route 53 return multiple healthy records, up to eight, in response to a single DNS query. Route 53 associates health checks with each record and returns only the values for healthy resources, so the IP addresses of all healthy EC2 instances are returned, allowing the client to choose among them. This provides simple DNS-based load distribution combined with health checking.

Option A is incorrect because a simple routing policy can hold multiple values but does not support health checks, so it could return the address of an unhealthy instance. Option B is incorrect because a latency routing policy directs traffic to the Region with the lowest latency and returns a single record, not all healthy instances. Option D is incorrect because a geolocation routing policy routes based on the user's geographic location and likewise returns a single record per query, neither of which satisfies returning all healthy EC2 instance IP addresses.

153.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Storage Gateway file gateway, deployed as a virtual machine at each clinic, presents the Amazon S3 bucket data through standard NFS or SMB file protocols that the on-premises file-based applications can use directly. The file gateway maintains a local cache of frequently accessed data, providing low-latency access to the S3-backed files while preserving the bucket's read-only permissions for each clinic.

Option C is incorrect because a volume gateway presents block storage (iSCSI volumes), not file shares backed by an existing S3 bucket, so the file-based applications could not directly read the study files. Option B is incorrect because AWS DataSync performs one-time or scheduled bulk data transfers; it copies files rather than providing ongoing low-latency file access, and it would not keep clinics current with bucket updates. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EFS file systems can only be mounted from within AWS (or through Direct Connect/VPN), are not exposed by simply attaching to remote on-premises servers, and do not surface the S3 bucket data.

154.text:
Option C is correct because it makes both tiers highly available across two Availability Zones. Moving the database to Amazon Aurora with a read replica in another Availability Zone provides database resilience and read scaling, while creating an AMI of the EC2 instance and launching it through an Auto Scaling group behind an Application Load Balancer spanning two Availability Zones lets the web tier scale automatically to meet demand and survive an Availability Zone failure.

Option A is incorrect because a single manually launched second instance and an ALB confined to one Availability Zone provide no cross-zone fault tolerance and no automatic scaling. Option B is incorrect for the same reasons: the Aurora read replica and the second EC2 instance are both in the same Availability Zone, so a zone failure takes down the application, and there is no Auto Scaling. Option D is incorrect because running the database on a separate single EC2 instance with S3 backups keeps the database as an unmanaged single point of failure rather than a highly available managed service.

155.text:
Option D is correct because decoupling the architecture with Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) durably buffers incoming job requests so that no data is lost when the EC2 instances are saturated. Configuring an EC2 Auto Scaling group to scale on the SQS queue depth adds capacity when the backlog grows and removes it when demand falls, directly tying compute to user load. Updating the software to read from the queue ensures every submitted job is eventually processed.

Option A is incorrect because simply cloning an instance behind an Application Load Balancer adds fixed capacity without queuing or demand-based scaling, so spikes can still drop work. Option C is incorrect because vertical scaling to a larger instance type is a fixed-size fix that does not scale with load and still loses requests during bursts. Option B is incorrect because an S3 VPC endpoint only changes the network path to S3; it does nothing to relieve the 100% CPU bottleneck or prevent unprocessed data.

156.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server is a fully managed native Windows file system that supports the Server Message Block (SMB) protocol. Clients can mount the file share over SMB and AWS handles all administration, including hardware, software patching, and backups, satisfying both the SMB access requirement and the fully managed requirement for the media application.

Option A is incorrect because a Storage Gateway volume gateway provides block-level iSCSI volumes, not SMB file shares. Option B is incorrect because a tape gateway presents a virtual tape library (iSCSI VTL) for backup and archival to S3 and Glacier, not interactive SMB file access. Option C is incorrect because manually deploying an Amazon EC2 Windows instance with the File and Storage Services role is self-managed; the company would be responsible for patching, scaling, high availability, and backups, which contradicts the requirement for a fully managed solution.

157.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon S3 is a supported destination for VPC Flow Logs and is the most cost-effective choice for the described access pattern. An S3 Lifecycle policy can automatically transition the log objects to S3 Standard-Infrequent Access (S3 Standard-IA) after 90 days, lowering storage cost for the period when the logs are accessed only intermittently while keeping them immediately retrievable when needed.

Option A is incorrect because setting a CloudWatch Logs retention of 90 days deletes the logs after 90 days, but the requirement is to keep accessing them intermittently afterward, not to expire them. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Kinesis is not a native VPC Flow Logs destination and is designed for streaming ingestion, not cost-optimized long-term retention. Option C is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail records AWS API activity and is not a destination for VPC Flow Logs; you cannot send flow logs to CloudTrail.

158.text:
Option A is correct because the requirement specifies that data must be encrypted at rest before it is uploaded to the S3 buckets. Client-side encryption encrypts the data on the client before it leaves the source, so it is already encrypted in transit and arrives in S3 already encrypted, uniquely satisfying the before-upload condition. Uploads occur over HTTPS, providing encryption in transit as well.

Options B, C, and D are incorrect because they all rely on server-side encryption, which encrypts data only after it reaches Amazon S3, not before the upload as required. Option B describes server-side encryption generically, option C enforces SSE-S3 through a bucket policy, and option D uses a default AWS KMS key, but in every case S3 performs the encryption on receipt rather than the client encrypting the data beforehand. Only client-side encryption guarantees the data is encrypted at rest prior to upload.

159.text:
Option C is correct because AD Connector is a directory proxy that redirects authentication requests to the existing on-premises Active Directory without replicating, synchronizing, or hosting a separate managed directory, which minimizes operational overhead. Integrating AD Connector with AWS IAM Identity Center lets the company map existing AD groups to permission sets that grant access across the multiple AWS Organizations accounts, delivering centralized identity management and access control with the least effort.

Option A is incorrect because creating an AWS Managed Microsoft AD and establishing a two-way trust stands up and maintains an additional directory, adding more operational overhead than a simple proxy. Option B is incorrect because creating and manually managing individual IAM users and permissions for a globally distributed team is highly operationally intensive and does not integrate with the existing Active Directory. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Cognito is designed for customer-facing application user pools, not for federating workforce Active Directory identities into AWS account access, and would require significant custom integration.

160.text:
Option B is correct because an Amazon API Gateway resource policy can explicitly control which source IP addresses may invoke the API. By writing a resource policy that allows the trusted internal IP ranges and denies all other addresses, the company restricts access to its critical HTTP API to only the trusted set of IPs, meeting the requirement directly at the API Gateway layer.

Option A is incorrect because an API Gateway private integration controls how API Gateway connects to backend resources (for example, through a VPC link to an NLB); it does not restrict client source IP addresses. Option C is incorrect because you do not deploy a public API Gateway endpoint directly into a private subnet, and network ACLs operate on subnets, not on the API Gateway service. Option D is incorrect because API Gateway is a managed service that does not have a customer-attachable security group for inbound client traffic, so security group rules cannot be used to filter API callers by IP.

161.text:
Option C is correct because an Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) queue durably buffers the incoming tracker coordinates, decoupling ingestion from processing so that unexpected surges are absorbed rather than overwhelming the EC2 instances. Because messages remain in the queue until processed and can be retained, no events are lost and they can effectively be replayed by reprocessing, solving the data-loss problem. SQS is fully managed, providing this resilience with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because writing each reading to Amazon S3 and scanning the bucket is an inefficient polling pattern that is not designed for high-throughput message buffering and adds processing complexity. Option B is incorrect because invoking AWS Lambda directly from API Gateway for each item provides no durable buffer, so a sudden spike can throttle or drop work and still lose events without a replay mechanism. Option D is incorrect because using DynamoDB as a queue and polling it for new items adds design complexity, and the TTL-based removal can delete records before they are reliably processed.

162.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Glue is a serverless ETL service that extracts from the relational database, applies complex transformation rules, and loads results to Amazon S3 with no infrastructure to manage, giving the least operational effort. Attaching a Glue security configuration that uses client-side encryption with AWS KMS managed keys (CSE-KMS) encrypts the data while it is being processed before it is stored, and per-customer KMS keys satisfy the requirement for customer-specific encryption keys.

Option A is incorrect because SSE-S3 uses Amazon S3 managed keys, not customer-specific keys, and encrypts only after the data reaches S3, not while it is processed. Options B and D are incorrect because Amazon EMR requires provisioning and managing clusters, which is far more operationally intensive than serverless Glue. Option D additionally uses SSE-KMS, which encrypts at rest on the server side rather than client-side during processing as required, and option B's CSE-Custom relies on a custom root key rather than the simpler AWS KMS managed customer keys.

163.text:
Option B is correct because IAM Roles Anywhere lets workloads running outside AWS, such as the on-premises virtual machines, use X.509 certificates to obtain temporary AWS security credentials by assuming an IAM role. This grants the application secure, short-lived, automatically rotating access to the S3 bucket without distributing long-term access keys, satisfying both the temporary access and secure access requirements.

Option A is incorrect because allowing access based on the data center's public IP range is not a secure or reliable mechanism; IP addresses can change or be spoofed and it grants standing rather than temporary access. Options C and D are incorrect because they rely on long-term IAM user access keys, which are not temporary and increase the risk of credential exposure. Storing the keys in AWS Secrets Manager (option D) protects the secret but still distributes static long-lived credentials, which is contrary to the temporary, least-privilege approach that IAM Roles Anywhere provides.

164.text:
Option B is correct because defining IAM roles with fine-grained permissions based on the principle of least privilege grants each developer only the access required for their tasks, minimizing the blast radius and preventing unauthorized access to sensitive data. Assigning roles rather than sharing static credentials provides temporary, auditable, and centrally managed access, which is the AWS security best practice for granting people access to resources.

Option A is incorrect because sharing IAM user credentials among team members eliminates individual accountability, cannot enforce least privilege, and is an explicit security anti-pattern. Option C is incorrect because long-term IAM access keys are static credentials prone to leakage and do not by themselves enforce least-privilege boundaries that protect sensitive data. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Cognito user pools are intended to authenticate end users of customer-facing applications, not to provide internal developers fine-grained, least-privilege access control to a company's AWS resources.

165.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon ElastiCache for Redis provides in-memory data storage that delivers the sub-millisecond latency needed for near real-time player reviews and rankings, and it natively supports leaderboard use cases through sorted sets. Unlike Memcached, Redis supports data persistence (snapshots and append-only file), so the player data survives an application restart, and as a managed service it meets the requirement with the least operational overhead.

Option D is incorrect because Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached is a pure in-memory cache that does not persist data to disk, so a restart would lose the player data, violating the persistence requirement. Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 fronted by CloudFront is object storage optimized for static content delivery, not the fast, frequently updated read/write access pattern required for live rankings. Option B is incorrect because storing data on self-managed EC2 instances across Regions adds substantial operational overhead and provides no managed persistence or low-latency caching layer.

166.text:
Option B is correct because the read replica's CPU usage dropped to a constant 25% after end-of-year reporting ended, indicating it is now oversized for the remaining read workload. Resizing only the read replica to a smaller instance class rightsizes capacity and reduces cost while still leaving headroom for future growth. The primary instance remains unchanged because its CPU stays at a constant 60%, which is an appropriate utilization level that should not be reduced.

Option A is incorrect because deleting the read replica removes the offloading of read-only reporting queries, which would push that load back onto the primary and harm performance. Option C is incorrect because the read replica is underutilized and should be made smaller, not larger, and shrinking the busy 60% primary would risk insufficient performance. Option D is incorrect because deleting the replica eliminates needed read capacity and enlarging the already adequately utilized primary adds unnecessary cost rather than rightsizing.

167.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon Athena is a serverless, pay-per-query service that can run SQL directly against data in Amazon S3, and it natively integrates with AWS KMS to read files protected with client-side encryption using KMS managed keys (CSE-KMS). Because the queries are only occasional, paying per query with no infrastructure to provision is the most cost-effective approach, and partitioning by the per-day prefixes lets Athena scan only the relevant calendar day.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon S3 Select queries a single object at a time and does not support CSE-KMS client-side encrypted Parquet data the way Athena does, making it unsuitable here. Options C and D are incorrect because both Amazon Redshift Spectrum (which involves a Redshift cluster) and Amazon EMR Serverless carry more setup and cost than serverless Athena for infrequent ad hoc queries; provisioning these analytics platforms is overkill for occasional moving-average calculations on a single day's data.

168.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon EMR runtime roles let you specify an IAM role for a job submission, so each team's big data workload assumes only the role with permissions for the specific AWS services that team needs. This enforces least-privilege isolation at the job level and, importantly, decouples workload permissions from the EC2 instance profile, preventing the jobs from using the underlying instances' IMDS credentials, exactly as required.

Option A is incorrect because interface VPC endpoints control private network connectivity to services but do not scope per-team IAM permissions or block IMDS access. Option C is incorrect because a single EC2 IAM instance profile applies cluster-wide to all jobs and is reachable through the Instance Metadata Service, so every workload would share those permissions and could access IMDS, which the company explicitly wants to avoid. Option D is incorrect because setting EnableApplicationScopedIAMRole to false disables the scoped runtime role behavior, doing the opposite of isolating each team's permissions.

169.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon ECS with AWS Fargate provides a serverless container platform that delivers the modular service architecture and scalability the company needs without managing EC2 hosts, patching, or cluster scaling. Amazon RDS with a Multi-AZ deployment preserves the existing structured relational schema while providing automated failover and high availability, and Amazon S3 is the durable, scalable object store purpose-built for product images.

Option A is incorrect because storing product images in an RDS DB instance is inappropriate and costly; relational databases are not designed for binary object storage, and managing EC2 in an Auto Scaling group adds operational overhead. Option B is incorrect because re-engineering a monolith into Lambda functions is a major effort, DynamoDB is a NoSQL store that abandons the required structured schema, and it cannot store image files efficiently. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EKS on EC2 requires managing nodes and Kubernetes complexity, and S3 Glacier Deep Archive is an archival tier with retrieval times of hours, making it unsuitable for serving live product images.

170.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Site-to-Site VPN establishes IPsec tunnels that encrypt all traffic between the corporate network and the VPC at the network and session layers, satisfying the encryption requirement. Configuring route table entries directs on-premises traffic to the VPC, while instance security groups and network ACLs enforce least-privilege access, preventing unrestricted connectivity between AWS and on-premises systems.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect by itself does not encrypt traffic; it is a private but unencrypted link, so it fails the encryption requirement. Option B is incorrect because IAM policies and roles control access to the AWS Management Console and API actions, not network-layer connectivity between the corporate network and the VPC, and they provide no traffic encryption. Option D is incorrect because AWS Transit Gateway is a network transit hub for connecting multiple VPCs and on-premises networks; it does not itself provide the encrypted tunnel that Site-to-Site VPN delivers and is unnecessary complexity for a single VPC connection.

171.text:
Option A is correct because the S3 Requester Pays feature shifts the cost of data requests and data transfer (egress) from the bucket owner to the requester. When the marketing firm downloads data, its own AWS account is billed for the request and transfer charges, directly minimizing the surveying company's data transfer costs while retaining the existing cross-account access grant.

Option B is incorrect because S3 Cross-Region Replication copies all objects to the marketing firm's bucket, which incurs replication transfer and additional storage costs for the company rather than reducing them. Option C is incorrect because AWS Resource Access Manager does not support sharing S3 buckets, and it would not change who pays for data transfer. Option D is incorrect because S3 Intelligent-Tiering optimizes storage costs based on access patterns, not data transfer costs, and the described sync to another bucket would add transfer and storage charges instead of reducing egress costs.

172.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Storage Gateway Amazon S3 File Gateway presents an NFS (and SMB) interface to on-premises applications while storing data durably as objects in Amazon S3, allowing the social media workloads to scale elastically without changing the NFS access pattern. Pairing it with an S3 Lifecycle policy automatically transitions data to lower-cost storage classes, making it the most cost-effective migration path.

Option A is incorrect because Volume Gateway exposes iSCSI block volumes rather than the NFS file interface the workloads use, and its primary backing is block storage with EBS snapshots, which is less cost-effective for this file-based use case. Options C and D are incorrect because Amazon EFS is a fully managed AWS file system, not a hybrid bridge for the existing on-premises NFS workloads; using EFS would require migrating and re-pointing the storage, and EFS storage costs more per gigabyte than S3, so neither the Standard-IA nor One Zone-IA class is the most cost-effective solution here.

173.text:
Option D is correct because S3 Multi-Region Access Points provide a single global endpoint that automatically routes requests to the nearest underlying S3 bucket over the AWS global network, avoiding public internet congestion. In an active-active configuration paired with S3 Cross-Region Replication, data is kept synchronized across Regions and the access point handles failover automatically, minimizing the management of Amazon S3.

Option A is incorrect because configuring the application to choose regional endpoints requires client-side logic and does not use the optimized AWS global network or provide automatic, low-management failover. Option B is incorrect because an active-passive design does not send users to the nearest bucket for both Regions simultaneously, so it fails the requirement to write to the closest endpoint. Option C is incorrect because manually sending data to regional endpoints plus cross-account replication increases management overhead and does not provide a single global endpoint that routes traffic over the private AWS backbone.

174.text:
Option A is correct because a gateway VPC endpoint for Amazon S3 enables instances in the VPC to reach S3 privately over the AWS network without traversing the public internet or an internet gateway. After attaching the endpoint policy and adding a route to the endpoint in the subnet route table, traffic to S3 uses private AWS routing instead of the public S3 endpoint, exactly meeting the requirement at no additional cost.

Option B is incorrect because S3 is a regional service and cannot be moved inside a VPC; buckets are not assigned VPC private IP addresses. Option C is incorrect because an S3 access point simplifies managing access policies but, on its own, still resolves to S3 over the public endpoint unless combined with a VPC endpoint, so it does not by itself keep traffic off the public path. Option D is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect provides a dedicated link from on-premises to AWS and is not used to privately connect an in-VPC EC2 instance to S3.

175.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Secrets Manager provides a native, fully managed solution for storing and automatically rotating database credentials. It integrates directly with Amazon Aurora and supports custom rotation schedules via built-in Lambda templates, requiring minimal operational overhead.

Options B, C, and D are incorrect because they require significant "undifferentiated heavy lifting." They force you to manually build, maintain, and secure custom rotation logic using AWS Lambda, Parameter Store, EFS, or S3. These approaches increase operational complexity, introduce potential security gaps in credential handling, and lack the seamless, automated integration that Secrets Manager offers for RDS and Aurora databases. Always prefer managed services over custom-coded infrastructure for security tasks.

176.text:
Option C is correct because data transferred between Amazon EC2 instances within the same Availability Zone using private IP addresses is free, whereas traffic between instances in different Availability Zones incurs inter-AZ data transfer charges. Since the batch application moves large volumes of data between multiple EC2 instances, colocating them in a single Availability Zone eliminates those inter-AZ charges and directly reduces overall data transfer costs.

Option A is incorrect because placing instances in an Auto Scaling group governs capacity and elasticity, not the data transfer pricing between instances. Option B is incorrect because instances in the same Region but in different Availability Zones still incur inter-AZ transfer fees, so being in the same Region alone does not minimize cost. Option D is incorrect because deploying instances across multiple Availability Zones specifically introduces inter-AZ data transfer charges, increasing cost rather than reducing it, even though it improves resilience.

177.text:
Options A and D are correct because they combine the two AWS Organizations governance tools needed for this requirement. A tag policy (Option A) standardizes the data sensitivity tag key and enforces the allowed values of sensitive and nonsensitive on EC2 instances across the targeted OU. A service control policy (Option D) provides the preventive guardrail: an SCP can deny the ec2:RunInstances action when the required tag key is absent and deny tag deletion, which a tag policy alone cannot enforce.

Option B is incorrect because SCPs do not define or enforce specific tag values; that capability belongs to tag policies. Option C is incorrect because tag policies cannot deny API actions such as launching instances or deleting tags; they only report and standardize tags. Option E is incorrect because using AWS Config with a Lambda function to delete noncompliant instances is a reactive, destructive approach with higher operational overhead, and it does not preventively block untagged instance creation or tag deletion as required.

178.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon CloudFront is a global content delivery network that caches and accelerates both static and dynamic content from edge locations close to users worldwide. Configuring the existing Application Load Balancer as the CloudFront origin offloads traffic, reduces latency for millions of global customers, and lowers data transfer costs, all without re-architecting the existing Auto Scaling and ALB backend.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon Route 53 geolocation routing only directs DNS queries to endpoints; with a single Region it cannot bring content closer to users or provide edge caching, so it does not improve global dynamic content delivery cost-effectively. Option C is incorrect because an S3 static website hosts only static files and cannot run the dynamic application logic, and enabling public read access is an unnecessary security risk. Option D is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect provides dedicated private connectivity from specific on-premises locations to AWS; it is not a mechanism for serving web content globally to end customers.

179.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon EFS is a fully managed, elastic file system that supports concurrent access from many EC2 instances across multiple Availability Zones using the standard NFS protocol. Mounting EFS requires no application code changes, and its data is stored redundantly across multiple Availability Zones, providing the high availability and resilience the Auto Scaling group needs while every instance shares the same data.

Option A is incorrect because a self-managed NFS server on a single EC2 instance with one EBS volume is a single point of failure and adds operational overhead, undermining high availability. Option B is incorrect because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server uses the SMB protocol suited to Windows workloads, and deploying it in a single Availability Zone is not highly available for a Linux application. Option C is incorrect because EBS volumes generally attach to a single instance in one Availability Zone and cannot be shared as-is across an Auto Scaling group of instances, so it does not meet the shared-access requirement.

180.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS) is a fully managed, elastic file system that natively supports the NFS protocol. Because the legacy application cannot be modified to use any protocol other than NFS, EFS lets the application continue communicating over NFS unchanged while providing scalable, highly available shared file storage across multiple Availability Zones with minimal operational effort.

Option A is incorrect because AWS DataSync is a data transfer and migration service used to move data between storage systems; it is not a persistent storage solution that the application mounts over NFS. Option B is incorrect because Amazon EBS provides block storage attached to a single instance and is accessed as a raw block device, not through the NFS file protocol. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EMRFS is an implementation that lets Amazon EMR access data in S3 using S3 APIs; it is specific to the Hadoop/EMR ecosystem and does not provide an NFS interface for a general legacy application.

181.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Aurora Serverless provides a fully managed relational (structured) database that automatically scales capacity up and down to match application traffic, so you pay only for the capacity consumed. This serverless scaling makes it cost-effective for variable workloads, and Aurora includes automated, continuous backups, satisfying the structured-schema, scalability, and backup requirements with minimal administration.

Option A is incorrect because a self-managed open source database on EC2 adds significant operational overhead for patching, scaling, and backups, and using Spot Instances risks interruption that can disrupt a persistent transactional database. Option B is incorrect because while Amazon RDS is a valid managed relational option, an on-demand fixed-capacity instance does not scale as granularly or cost-effectively with fluctuating traffic as Aurora Serverless does. Option D is incorrect because the requirement is for a structured (relational) database, and a self-managed NoSQL database on EC2 abandons that model while also introducing substantial management overhead.

182.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Storage Gateway Volume Gateway in cached volumes mode stores the primary copy of data in Amazon S3 while keeping a local cache of frequently (recently) accessed data on premises. This lets the company stop scaling its on-premises iSCSI storage, since the full dataset lives in AWS and only the hot, recently accessed data is retained locally, exactly matching the requirements.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 File Gateway presents an NFS/SMB file interface, not the iSCSI block protocol the company uses. Option B is incorrect because Tape Gateway provides a virtual tape library for backup and archival workflows over iSCSI VTL, not primary block storage with a local cache of active data. Option C is incorrect because Volume Gateway stored volumes keep the entire dataset on premises (with asynchronous backups to S3), which does not reduce the need to scale local storage and therefore fails the core requirement.

183.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between frequent-access and infrequent-access tiers based on changing access patterns, with no retrieval fees and no performance impact. This makes it cost-effective for data that is used continuously during business hours yet sometimes goes unused for months, while preserving S3's eleven-nines durability and high availability across multiple Availability Zones.

Option A is incorrect because S3 Standard maintains the same durability and availability but does not automatically optimize cost for objects that are not accessed for months, so it is more expensive for this mixed pattern. Option C is incorrect because S3 Glacier Deep Archive is an archival tier with multi-hour retrieval times, which cannot serve data that is used continuously during business hours. Option D is incorrect because S3 One Zone-IA stores data in only a single Availability Zone, providing lower availability and resilience, which fails the requirement to maintain the highest durability and high availability.

184.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon ElastiCache for Redis caches frequently read, rarely changing data such as the product catalog in memory, serving repeated searches without hitting the database. Using lazy loading populates the cache only when a requested item is not already cached, which reduces redundant queries and offloads the high CPU load from the RDS for MySQL instance, directly improving search response time.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Redshift is a data warehouse optimized for analytical OLAP queries, not low-latency operational catalog lookups, and migrating the catalog adds unnecessary complexity. Option C is incorrect because adding more EC2 web servers does not relieve the database, which is the actual bottleneck experiencing high CPU during searches. Option D is incorrect because Multi-AZ improves availability through a standby replica but does not offload read traffic or reduce CPU, and throttling queries would degrade the user experience rather than improve search performance.

185.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Transit Gateway acts as a central hub that connects multiple VPCs and accounts, and the existing Direct Connect connection can be associated with the transit gateway. New accounts simply attach their VPCs to the transit gateway to gain quick, consistent, and cost-effective access to the on-premises directory and DNS services, avoiding duplicated connectivity and minimizing operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because provisioning a separate Direct Connect connection in each new account is expensive, slow to provision, and operationally heavy. Option B is incorrect because VPC endpoints provide private connectivity to AWS services or PrivateLink-enabled services, not to on-premises directory and DNS servers reached over Direct Connect, so they do not solve the requirement. Option C is incorrect because creating individual VPN connections between every new account and the DX VPC does not scale well, adds management complexity per account, and does not centralize routing the way a transit gateway does.

186.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Fargate supplies serverless compute for Amazon EKS, meaning AWS provisions and manages the underlying instances automatically. By creating a Fargate profile that maps pods to Fargate, the company runs its Kubernetes application without provisioning, patching, or scaling any EC2 nodes, delivering the least operational overhead while still scaling to handle millions of new customers.

Option A is incorrect because self-managed nodes require the company to provision, patch, scale, and maintain the EC2 instances itself, which is the highest operational burden. Option B is incorrect because managed node groups still expose and require management of EC2 instances, including capacity and upgrades, so the company continues to manage compute infrastructure. Option D is incorrect because managed node groups with Karpenter automate scaling decisions but the company still owns and operates the underlying EC2 nodes, so it does not eliminate compute infrastructure management the way Fargate does.

187.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon RDS automated backups perform daily snapshots and continuously capture transaction logs, enabling point-in-time restore to any second within the retention window. Setting the retention period to the maximum supported value (up to 35 days) easily covers the required 14-day point-in-time restore, and this fully managed, built-in feature requires the least operational overhead to satisfy the retention need within RDS.

Option B is incorrect because manually creating and deleting daily snapshots adds operational overhead and does not, on its own, provide continuous point-in-time restore between snapshots. Option C is incorrect because the Aurora Clone feature applies to Amazon Aurora, not Amazon RDS for Oracle, and cloning is not a point-in-time backup mechanism. Option D is incorrect because, while AWS Backup can centrally manage RDS backups, configuring a separate backup plan introduces additional setup and management compared with simply enabling the native RDS automated backups already available for the instance.

188.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is the AWS managed service purpose-built to securely store, encrypt, and retrieve credentials such as usernames and passwords, and it supports automatic rotation. Using AWS CloudFormation with the BatchGetSecretValue API retrieves multiple secrets in a single call, providing a streamlined, low-overhead way for the internal application to obtain the employee credentials securely.

Option A is incorrect because BatchGetSecretValue is a Secrets Manager API and does not retrieve values from Systems Manager Parameter Store, so the service and API are mismatched. Option C has the same flaw: it points to Parameter Store while invoking a Secrets Manager API, and adds AWS Batch unnecessarily. Option B is incorrect because, although it correctly uses Secrets Manager, introducing AWS Batch to run the retrieval adds needless compute orchestration and operational overhead compared with calling BatchGetSecretValue directly, making it less efficient than Option D.

189.text:
Option B is correct because the AWS Glue DynamoDB export connector uses DynamoDB's native point-in-time export to Amazon S3, reading data without consuming the table's provisioned read capacity. The Glue ETL job can then calculate the daily performance metrics on a schedule, ensuring minimal effect on the customer-facing table's provisioned read and write throughput.

Option A is incorrect because the Amazon Athena DynamoDB connector queries the table directly through the DynamoDB API, which consumes provisioned read capacity and can degrade performance for the live website. Option C is incorrect because a Redshift COPY command cannot read directly from a DynamoDB table without consuming its read capacity, and it adds a data warehouse to manage. Option D is incorrect because an Amazon EMR job using an Apache Hive external table over DynamoDB also reads through the table's provisioned throughput, impacting the table's capacity and adding the operational overhead of an EMR cluster.

190.text:
Option D is correct because AWS PrivateLink creates a private connection between the application's VPC and the third-party SaaS provider through interface VPC endpoints, keeping traffic on the AWS network. This provides secure, private connectivity to the SaaS APIs without exposing the internal service components to the public internet, exactly meeting the requirement for minimal public exposure.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Site-to-Site VPN is designed to connect on-premises networks to a VPC over IPsec, not to privately consume a SaaS application hosted on AWS, and it does not provide the endpoint-based private service access. Option B is incorrect because AWS Transit Gateway routes traffic between VPCs and networks but does not by itself provide private, one-way access to a third-party SaaS endpoint. Option C is incorrect because, while it references PrivateLink, its description is garbled and incomplete; Option D states the correct and complete PrivateLink solution for private VPC-to-SaaS connectivity.

191.text:
Option A is correct because an Amazon SQS FIFO queue guarantees that messages are processed in order and provides exactly-once processing through deduplication, ensuring each registration form is handled precisely one time. Decoupling the web tier from the worker tier with a durable queue also ensures no form data is lost if a worker is busy or temporarily unavailable, satisfying all stated requirements.

Option B is incorrect because an API Gateway HTTP API is a synchronous request/response interface, not a durable store-and-forward buffer, so it does not guarantee exactly-once processing or prevent data loss if the worker tier is unavailable. Option C is incorrect because an SQS standard queue provides at-least-once delivery and best-effort ordering, which can deliver duplicates and therefore cannot guarantee each form is processed exactly once. Option D is incorrect because a synchronous Step Functions workflow couples the tiers tightly and does not provide the durable buffering of a queue, risking data loss and lacking the exactly-once form-processing guarantee.

192.text:
Option D is correct because enabling S3 Inventory produces a manifest of all objects, and an AWS Lambda function can filter that report to identify objects older than three years while excluding the data to be retained. Invoking the Lambda function through S3 Batch Operations performs the deletions at scale across millions of objects, providing a fully serverless solution with no servers to provision or manage.

Option A is incorrect because running a deletion script on an Amazon EC2 instance is not serverless and adds the operational overhead of provisioning and managing the instance. Option B is incorrect because AWS Batch runs jobs on managed compute (such as EC2 or Fargate) and is oriented toward batch compute workloads rather than serverless S3 object deletion, and it is more complex than S3 Batch Operations for this task. Option C is incorrect because an AWS Glue crawler catalogs data schemas for analytics and is not designed to enumerate and delete S3 objects, and the approach still relies on a custom script rather than a native serverless deletion mechanism.

193.text:
Option C is correct because transferring 5 PB over a 1 Gbps link would take far longer than the six-month window, so the data must be shipped physically. AWS Snowball devices equipped with Tape Gateway let the company copy physical tapes to virtual tapes, ship the devices to AWS, and then use a lifecycle policy to move the virtual tapes into Amazon S3 Glacier Deep Archive, the lowest-cost archival tier, meeting the cost and timeline requirements for 10-year retention.

Option A is incorrect because using AWS DataSync over the 1 Gbps internet connection cannot move 5 PB within six months, and S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval is more expensive than Deep Archive for long-term compliance storage. Option B is incorrect because writing directly to S3 Glacier Deep Archive still depends on the bandwidth-constrained 1 Gbps uplink, making the transfer infeasible in the timeframe. Option D is incorrect because an on-premises Tape Gateway also transfers virtual tapes to AWS over the limited internet link, so it cannot meet the six-month migration deadline for such a large dataset.

194.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built to store database credentials and provides native, fully managed automatic rotation for Amazon RDS for MySQL through a Lambda rotation function. The application retrieves credentials at runtime by calling the Secrets Manager API, removing embedded credentials from the code with minimal programming effort, and rotation occurs without changing the application.

Option A is incorrect because AWS KMS manages encryption keys, not secrets; it cannot store credential values or rotate database passwords, and KMS key rotation rotates cryptographic keys rather than the database credentials. Option B is incorrect because encrypted local storage plus a cron job is a manual, self-managed approach that increases operational overhead and is error-prone and insecure compared to a managed service. Option D is incorrect because Systems Manager Parameter Store does not provide built-in, automatic credential rotation for RDS; achieving rotation requires custom Lambda code, which is more programming effort than the native rotation that Secrets Manager offers.

195.text:
Options D and E are correct because IAM roles for service accounts (IRSA) work by associating an IAM role with a Kubernetes service account through an annotation containing the role ARN (Option D), and by establishing a trust relationship between the IAM role and the EKS cluster's OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider (Option E). Together these let individual pods assume scoped IAM roles via web identity federation, delivering secure, granular, least-privilege access to specific AWS resources.

Option A is incorrect because attaching a policy to the EKS node IAM role grants permissions to every pod on the node, violating the granular per-service-account requirement. Option B is incorrect because Kubernetes network policies control pod-to-pod network traffic within the cluster, not IAM authorization for AWS API calls. Option C is incorrect because modifying the cluster IAM role to embed permissions for each service account does not provide per-pod credential isolation; IRSA, not a one-to-one IAM-to-Kubernetes role mapping on the cluster role, is the supported mechanism for granular access.

196.text:
Option B is correct because the company already uses AWS IAM Identity Center, so enabling it with permission sets is the lowest-overhead path. Permission sets bundle IAM policies that define granular, least-privilege access to Amazon RDS and Amazon S3, and assigning teams to groups mapped to those permission sets centralizes access management and scales without per-user maintenance.

Option A is incorrect because manually creating and assigning IAM roles with custom policies per team duplicates effort that IAM Identity Center already provides and increases ongoing operational overhead. Option C is incorrect because creating individual IAM users for every team member is the highest-maintenance approach, conflicts with the centralized Identity Center model, and adds long-term credential management burden. Option D is incorrect because splitting each team into separate AWS Organizations accounts with cross-account roles is heavyweight account-level isolation that introduces significant operational complexity and is unnecessary when permission sets can enforce least privilege within the existing setup.

197.text:
Option A is correct because enabling encryption at rest for Amazon RDS for MySQL with AWS KMS managed keys is a built-in, checkbox-level feature that requires no application changes, and using AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) SSL/TLS certificates secures data in transit through standard, fully managed certificate handling. This combination satisfies both encryption mandates with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because configuring IPsec tunnels for in-transit encryption adds significant networking complexity and management overhead compared to native TLS. Option C is incorrect because implementing third-party application-level encryption before storing data shifts encryption logic into the application, increasing development and operational burden when RDS provides transparent encryption at rest. Option D is incorrect because a VPN connection provides private network connectivity but is unnecessary operational overhead for encrypting database traffic, which is better handled by SSL/TLS directly between the application and RDS.

198.text:
Option A is correct because AWS App2Container automatically containerizes the existing .NET application and generates deployment artifacts, and deploying to Amazon ECS on AWS Fargate gives a serverless container platform with no servers to patch or manage. Fargate scales tasks based on Amazon CloudWatch metrics, directly meeting the requirements for containerization, metric-based scaling, and the least operational maintenance.

Option B is incorrect because deploying ECS on Amazon EC2 instances requires managing, patching, and scaling the underlying instances, adding operational maintenance that Fargate eliminates. Option C is incorrect because AWS App Runner is a standalone fully managed service that runs containers directly; it does not containerize applications and is not used to deploy to ECS on Fargate as described. Option D is incorrect for the same reason and because App Runner does not deploy workloads to Amazon EKS on EC2 instances, and EKS on EC2 would add cluster and node management overhead.

199.text:
Options A and B are correct because Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose provides a fully managed, automatically scaling ingestion layer that buffers and delivers high-volume streaming data without provisioning (Option A), and AWS Lambda orchestrated by AWS Step Functions provides serverless processing that can run and coordinate the workload while scaling with demand (Option B). Step Functions also handles long-running, multi-step orchestration and parallelism to reduce the latency caused by large data bursts.

Option C is incorrect because AWS Database Migration Service is designed for migrating and replicating databases, not for ingesting near-real-time application streams. Option D is incorrect because EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group are not serverless and add instance management overhead, conflicting with the serverless requirement. Option E is incorrect because, although Fargate is serverless containers, the question's chosen serverless processing pattern pairs Lambda with Step Functions for orchestrated, event-driven scaling rather than running long-lived ECS tasks.

200.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon DynamoDB with DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) delivers in-memory caching that provides single-digit-millisecond to microsecond (sub-millisecond) read latency for frequently accessed gaming data. Using DynamoDB's native table export to Amazon S3 and querying that data with Amazon Athena enables one-time, serverless queries on historical data with no infrastructure to manage, meeting both needs with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon RDS cannot guarantee sub-millisecond read latency, and a periodic custom export script adds maintenance overhead. Option B is incorrect because storing data directly in S3 cannot serve sub-millisecond reads for live gameplay, and Glacier Deep Archive is for archival, not active access. Option D is incorrect because plain DynamoDB without DAX does not provide sub-millisecond reads, and adding Kinesis Data Streams plus Firehose to land data in S3 introduces extra streaming components and operational complexity that Athena-on-export avoids.

201.text:
Option C is correct because the Amazon VPC CNI plugin for Kubernetes assigns pods native VPC IP addresses and supports custom networking, allowing the company to designate specific custom subnets in the VPC for pod placement. Because pods receive real VPC addresses, they communicate securely within the VPC using standard VPC constructs such as security groups, satisfying both the custom-subnet and secure-communication requirements.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Transit Gateway connects VPCs and on-premises networks; it does not manage subnet assignment for EKS pods. Option B is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect provides dedicated connectivity from on premises to AWS and is unrelated to assigning custom pod subnets. Option D is incorrect because a Kubernetes network policy with pod anti-affinity influences scheduling and restricts traffic between pods, but it does not control which VPC subnet supplies pod IP addresses, which is exactly what the VPC CNI plugin custom subnet configuration provides.

202.text:
Option A is correct because an Amazon SQS FIFO queue guarantees that each message is processed exactly once and in order through its exactly-once processing and deduplication capabilities, satisfying the requirement to process each order exactly once. SQS decouples producers from consumers and buffers requests, so an AWS Lambda function target can absorb unpredictable traffic surges by scaling automatically without degrading the customer experience.

Option B is incorrect because an Amazon SNS standard topic provides at-least-once delivery and can deliver duplicates, so it cannot guarantee exactly-once order processing. Option C is incorrect because Amazon AppFlow transfers data between SaaS applications and AWS services and is not a transactional order-processing queue. Option D is incorrect because AWS X-Ray is a distributed tracing and debugging tool, and orders cannot be pulled from Amazon CloudWatch for processing; this combination does not provide exactly-once delivery or surge handling.

203.text:
Option C is correct because creating an individual IAM role for each Lambda function and assigning it as that function's execution role grants only the specific function access to the S3 bucket. This enforces the principle of least privilege by scoping permissions per function, ensuring that only authorized Lambda functions can retrieve the sensitive data and that permissions can be tuned independently.

Option A is incorrect because granting full S3 access through a shared IAM role gives every function broad permissions, directly violating least privilege. Option B is incorrect because controlling access by VPC endpoint IP addresses is fragile, hard to maintain, and does not reliably identify which specific Lambda function is calling, so it cannot enforce per-function authorization. Option D is incorrect because S3 bucket policies cannot grant access based on a Lambda function ARN; access for Lambda is controlled through the function's IAM execution role identity, making per-function IAM roles the correct mechanism.

204.text:
Option B is correct because an Amazon Route 53 multivalue answer routing policy returns multiple healthy records (one per Region's endpoint) and integrates with Route 53 health checks, so DNS responses include only endpoints that are healthy. This distributes traffic across both Regions for growth and disaster recovery while ensuring traffic is not served from a Region where the website is unhealthy.

Option A is incorrect because a simple routing policy does not support health checks, so it could continue returning an unhealthy Region's record. Option C is incorrect because an Application Load Balancer is a single-Region resource and its instance-ID target type cannot register EC2 instances located in a different Region. Option D is incorrect because, although an ALB can use IP-based targets, a single ALB in one Region directing to instances in another Region creates cross-Region dependency and a single point of failure, and does not provide the Region-level health-based DNS distribution that the multivalue answer policy delivers.

205.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EventBridge provides a loosely coupled, event-driven bus where each validation step has its own event rule, and the input transformer reshapes the event so each target Lambda function receives only the subset of order data it requires. This satisfies least-privilege data exposure and lets the company add or change validation steps independently as business needs evolve.

Option A is incorrect because, although SQS queues per step are loosely coupled, building and maintaining a custom transformer Lambda to reformat data and publish to each queue adds avoidable complexity compared to EventBridge's native input transformer. Option B is incorrect because SNS message filtering selects which messages a subscriber receives but does not transform or trim the message payload to a per-function subset. Option D is incorrect because a single Lambda synchronously invoking the validation functions in parallel on separate threads tightly couples the components and reduces resilience, contradicting the loose-coupling requirement.

206.text:
Option A is correct because an S3 Lifecycle rule transitioning objects to S3 Intelligent-Tiering is the most operationally efficient choice for data with unknown or unpredictable, random access patterns. Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between frequent and infrequent access tiers based on usage, reducing storage costs while keeping frequently accessed objects immediately available with no retrieval fees or manual monitoring.

Option B is incorrect because storing data in S3 Glacier introduces retrieval latency and does not provide immediate availability for frequently accessed objects. Option C is incorrect because relying on storage class analysis to author Standard-IA lifecycle rules requires ongoing analysis and rule management, and Standard-IA charges retrieval fees that penalize the random access pattern. Option D is incorrect because building a custom Lambda function to move objects back to S3 Standard on access adds significant operational overhead and reactive complexity that Intelligent-Tiering handles automatically.

207.text:
Option C is correct because deploying the application to EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group behind an Application Load Balancer provides automatic scaling and high availability across Availability Zones, and Amazon Aurora Serverless for MySQL provides a MySQL-compatible database that automatically scales capacity and is highly available, fully decoupling the database from the single EC2 instance. This meets the requirements for high availability and automatic scaling at both the application and database tiers.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Redshift is a data warehouse for analytics, not a transactional MySQL replacement for the application database. Option B is incorrect because, although an RDS for MySQL Multi-AZ deployment improves availability, it does not automatically scale capacity the way Aurora Serverless does, and placing instances only in a target group omits the Auto Scaling group needed for application scaling. Option D is incorrect because Amazon ElastiCache for Redis is an in-memory cache, not a relational database, and there is no MySQL connector that turns it into the primary datastore.

208.text:
Option A is correct because moving the files to an Amazon S3 bucket with an Intelligent-Tiering lifecycle policy is the most cost-effective option, since S3 offers lower storage costs than EFS and Intelligent-Tiering automatically optimizes cost across access tiers without retrieval fees. Because the company accepts application and service changes, updating the application to use the S3 API to store and retrieve files aligns the workload with the lowest-cost durable object storage while keeping the RDS index.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server uses the SMB/CIFS protocol for Windows workloads and is more costly and operationally heavier than S3 for this Linux file-indexing use case. Option C is incorrect because FSx for OpenZFS is a high-performance file system that does not minimize storage cost the way S3 does. Option D is incorrect because S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval is archival storage with retrieval delays and is not suitable for files the application actively stores and retrieves.

209.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon AppFlow has a native Salesforce connector that loads existing records and ongoing changes into Amazon Redshift with minimal configuration and the least development effort. Using AWS PrivateLink keeps the data flow off the public internet by providing private connectivity for the AppFlow Salesforce integration, satisfying both the integration and the no-public-internet requirements.

Option A is incorrect because a VPN to Salesforce is impractical for a SaaS provider and AWS Glue DataBrew is a visual data-preparation tool, not a managed Salesforce-to-Redshift transfer service, increasing development effort. Option B is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect provides dedicated links to AWS, not to a SaaS application like Salesforce, and DataBrew again is not the appropriate transfer mechanism. Option D is incorrect because VPC peering connects two VPCs you control and cannot peer with Salesforce's SaaS environment, so it cannot meet the private-connectivity requirement.

210.text:
Option A is correct because creating an A record for the development website pointing to the Application Load Balancer, combined with an ALB listener rule that forwards requests for that hostname to the target group containing the development instance, routes engineers to that instance through the load balancer. Because the ALB forwards to a target group rather than a fixed address, the routing continues to work automatically even if the development instance is replaced and re-registered.

Option B is incorrect because pointing the A record at the instance's public IP breaks whenever the instance is replaced, since the new instance receives a different IP. Option C is incorrect because ALB listener rules cannot redirect to a raw EC2 public IP address, and doing so would not survive instance replacement. Option D is incorrect because placing all instances in the same target group would send development traffic to production instances as well, defeating the requirement to reach one specific development instance.

211.text:
Option D is correct because creating an AWS KMS key and enabling the Lambda encryption helpers encrypts environment variables with that customer managed key. The encryption helpers store the values encrypted and decrypt them at runtime within the function, so developers viewing the configuration see ciphertext rather than plaintext, meeting the requirement to hide environment variables.

Option A is incorrect because moving code to Amazon EC2 instances abandons the serverless model and does not address protecting environment variables. Option B is incorrect because Lambda does not use SSL encryption with AWS CloudHSM to encrypt environment variables; that is not how Lambda variable encryption works and CloudHSM adds unnecessary complexity. Option C is incorrect because AWS Certificate Manager issues TLS certificates for securing network connections and cannot be used to encrypt Lambda environment variables; the correct mechanism is KMS-based encryption through the Lambda encryption helpers.

212.text:
Option B is correct because placing the EC2 instances in private subnets behind an internal Application Load Balancer keeps the web application unreachable from the public internet, and restricting the ALB security group's inbound source to the company's office network CIDR block limits access to traffic arriving over the Site-to-Site VPN. NAT gateways in public subnets, with an internet gateway attached to the VPC, allow the private instances to make outbound connections to download security patches without exposing them to inbound internet traffic.

Option A is incorrect because a public ALB with an inbound source of 0.0.0.0/0 exposes the application to the entire internet. Option C is incorrect because it misplaces resources (public-subnet instances behind an internal ALB, NAT gateways in private subnets) and uses an outbound security group rule, which does not control who can reach the application. Option D is incorrect because a public ALB exposes the application, and an outbound destination of 0.0.0.0/0 does not restrict inbound access to the office network.

213.text:
Option C is correct because the production instances run constantly, so a Compute Savings Plan provides the deepest sustained discount with the flexibility to cover that steady usage. The nonproduction instances run only during weekday business hours, so using On-Demand Instances and shutting them down when not in use means the company pays only for the hours actually consumed, which is the most cost-effective approach for that intermittent pattern.

Option A is incorrect because Dedicated Hosts are expensive, dedicated physical servers intended for licensing or compliance needs, not cost optimization for intermittent nonproduction use. Option B is incorrect because Reserved Instances for the nonproduction environment commits to capacity that sits idle nights and weekends, wasting money, and shutting them down does not recover the commitment. Option D is incorrect because Dedicated Hosts for production are far more costly than a Compute Savings Plan, and committing an EC2 Instance Savings Plan to part-time nonproduction instances is wasteful.

214.text:
Options A and D are correct because AWS Lambda provides serverless compute that requires the least infrastructure management, with no servers to provision, patch, or scale (Option A), and Amazon SQS FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once message processing through deduplication and ordered delivery (Option D). Together they replace the third-party middleware's exactly-once guarantee while minimizing operational management.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon EC2 instances require provisioning, patching, scaling, and ongoing maintenance, which is the opposite of least infrastructure management. Option C is incorrect because Amazon SNS standard topics provide at-least-once delivery and may deliver duplicate messages, so they cannot guarantee exactly-once delivery for application messaging. Option E is incorrect because Amazon EKS containers still require managing cluster configuration, worker nodes, and scaling, adding more operational overhead than the fully serverless Lambda option.

215.text:
Option B is correct because the objects are frequently replaced with multipart uploads, and any incomplete or failed multipart upload leaves orphaned parts that continue to accrue S3 storage charges. Enabling an S3 Lifecycle policy that automatically deletes incomplete multipart uploads removes these abandoned parts, which explains and eliminates the steadily rising storage costs even though the visible object count and size remain constant.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration speeds up uploads over long distances but does not reduce storage costs and does not address orphaned multipart parts. Option C is incorrect because S3 Inventory only reports on objects and their metadata; it does not delete data or reduce storage charges. Option D is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery cache that reduces data transfer and request load but does not reduce the number of objects stored in S3 or clean up incomplete multipart uploads.

216.text:
Option B is correct because the Kubernetes Cluster Autoscaler is the standard mechanism that watches for pods that cannot be scheduled due to insufficient capacity and automatically adds nodes to the EKS cluster, then removes them when they are no longer needed. This directly resolves the failure of nodes to scale out when existing nodes reach maximum capacity, and it integrates with EKS node groups with the least administrative overhead.

Option A is incorrect because simply tracking memory usage does not by itself add nodes to the cluster; it lacks the node-provisioning logic the Cluster Autoscaler provides. Option C is incorrect because writing and maintaining a custom AWS Lambda function to resize the cluster adds significant operational and code maintenance overhead compared to the purpose-built autoscaler. Option D is incorrect because the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler scales pods while node scaling for EKS is handled by the Cluster Autoscaler; a standalone EC2 Auto Scaling group is not the managed solution for adding schedulable EKS nodes in response to pending pods.

217.text:
Option A is correct because creating a transit gateway and associating the Direct Connect connection with a transit virtual interface (VIF) provides a single hub that connects all 30 VPCs and the on-premises network. Turning on the transit gateway's route propagation automatically distributes routes among the attachments, giving centralized management and any-to-any connectivity between every VPC and on premises with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because a Direct Connect gateway with per-VPC virtual private gateways does not provide VPC-to-VPC routing, so VPCs cannot communicate with each other, and managing many virtual private gateways is operationally heavy. Option C is incorrect because building a transit VPC and full-mesh VPC peering across 30 VPCs requires managing hundreds of peering connections and route tables, which is high overhead. Option D is incorrect because creating individual Site-to-Site VPN connections to each VPC adds tunnel management, lower bandwidth, and does not enable VPC-to-VPC communication through a central, managed hub.

218.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon ECS lets you decompose the monolith into independently deployable microservices running as containerized tasks, so each tightly coupled module can be patched and updated on its own without taking down the whole application. ECS service auto scaling adjusts task count based on demand, providing flexibility and scalability, and rolling deployments update tasks gradually to minimize downtime. Containers also isolate failures so report generation is not forced to restart globally after a single module update.

Option A is incorrect because Lambda functions have a 15-minute maximum execution timeout, which cannot accommodate a 20-minute report job, and packaging the entire monolith as one function does not address the tight coupling. Option B is incorrect because Spot Instances can be reclaimed with little notice, which would interrupt long-running report jobs and force restarts, conflicting with the goal of minimizing downtime. Option D is incorrect because an all-at-once Elastic Beanstalk deployment to a single environment replaces all instances simultaneously, causing downtime during every update and keeping the application as a single deployable unit rather than gradually improvable microservices.

219.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon MSK public access can only be enabled on clusters deployed in subnets that route through an internet gateway, and the cleanest, most operationally efficient way to achieve this is to create a new VPC with public subnets and deploy the cluster there. MSK public access additionally requires that the cluster use TLS-only encryption in transit and an authentication method such as mutual TLS or SASL, so enabling mutual TLS authentication satisfies the encryption-in-transit requirement while exposing brokers over the internet securely.

Option A is incorrect because MSK does not support turning on public access for a cluster that was originally deployed in private subnets; you cannot simply convert the existing private cluster's subnets, making this approach unworkable. Options C and D are incorrect because Application Load Balancers and Network Load Balancers do not front Kafka brokers for public MSK access; MSK public connectivity is a native cluster feature, not a load-balancer construct, and placing either load balancer in private subnets would not expose the brokers to the internet at all.

220.text:
Option C is correct because a Route 53 private hosted zone provides private DNS records that resolve only within the VPCs associated with it, and it is the most secure option because the records are never exposed to the public internet. Associating the private hosted zone with the application's VPC lets the EC2 resources resolve the names used to reach the on-premises services privately over the existing VPN connection, keeping name resolution internal to AWS and the company network.

Option A is incorrect because a Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoint forwards DNS queries from the VPC to on-premises DNS resolvers; it is used when on-premises systems own the DNS zone, not for the VPC to host private records itself. Option B is incorrect because a Resolver inbound endpoint lets on-premises systems resolve names hosted in AWS, which is the reverse of this requirement. Option D is incorrect because a public hosted zone publishes records to the internet, which is the least secure choice and contradicts the requirement to use private DNS records.

221.text:
Option C is correct because adding reader instances to the Aurora cluster scales read capacity to absorb heavy read load, and Aurora can do this with minimal downtime since readers are added to the shared cluster volume. Placing an Amazon RDS Proxy in front of the cluster pools and reuses database connections, reducing connection exhaustion and timeouts during traffic spikes, and RDS Proxy improves availability by reducing failover times by up to 66 percent. Together these make the architecture more scalable and highly available with the least disruption.

Option A is incorrect because EventBridge with a Lambda function only logs cluster state-change events; it adds observability but does nothing to scale read or write capacity or to prevent timeouts. Option B is incorrect because zero-downtime restart is an automatic engine behavior, not a scaling mechanism, and Database Activity Streams is a security auditing feature unrelated to performance. Option D is incorrect because replicating to ElastiCache via AWS DMS adds significant operational complexity and application changes and does not directly relieve the write-load bottleneck.

222.text:
Option C is correct because a Network Load Balancer operates at Layer 4 and supports the UDP protocol, making it the appropriate choice for game traffic. NLB is built to handle millions of requests per second at ultra-low latency while preserving the high throughput needed for real-time gaming, and it scales automatically without pre-warming, which makes it the most cost-effective managed option for this workload.

Option A is incorrect because an Application Load Balancer works only at Layer 7 with HTTP and HTTPS and cannot route UDP traffic at all. Option B is incorrect because a Gateway Load Balancer is designed to deploy and scale third-party virtual appliances such as firewalls and intrusion detection systems using the GENEVE protocol; it is not intended to front game servers for client UDP traffic. Option D is incorrect because launching duplicate fleets across multiple Regions and routing traffic to both substantially increases cost and operational complexity and does not inherently provide the Layer 4 UDP load balancing the requirement calls for.

223.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon ECS with the AWS Fargate launch type is a fully managed, serverless container platform, so the company runs containers without provisioning or managing EC2 instances or clusters. Amazon EFS provides fully managed, elastic shared file storage that Fargate tasks can mount as a persistent volume, so the container's persistent data survives task restarts and is shared across tasks. This combination eliminates all server and storage infrastructure management, matching the requirement exactly.

Option A is incorrect because EKS with self-managed nodes and an EBS volume attached to an EC2 instance still requires the company to manage servers and storage. Option C is incorrect because Amazon S3 is object storage and cannot be natively mounted as a POSIX file-system volume for containers, so it does not replace the host volume the application expects. Option D is incorrect because the ECS EC2 launch type requires the company to provision, patch, and scale the EC2 container instances, which violates the requirement to avoid managing servers.

224.text:
Option B is correct because a gateway VPC endpoint for Amazon S3 enables EC2 instances in the VPC to reach S3 directly over the AWS private network using route table entries, so traffic never traverses the public internet. Gateway endpoints are purpose-built for S3 and DynamoDB, incur no additional charge, and provide a secure, private path to the bucket, satisfying the requirement precisely.

Option A is incorrect because a Route 53 private hosted zone provides private DNS name resolution; it does not create a private network path to the S3 service. Option C is incorrect because a NAT gateway routes outbound traffic to the public internet to reach S3's public endpoint, which directly violates the requirement that traffic must not traverse the internet. Option D is incorrect because Site-to-Site VPN connects a VPC to an on-premises network over an encrypted tunnel; you cannot establish a VPN connection to an S3 bucket, so this option does not apply.

225.text:
Option D is correct because distributing the Auto Scaling group across three Availability Zones gives the compute tier high availability so the application survives the loss of an AZ, while migrating the embedded NoSQL database to Amazon DynamoDB removes the self-managed database from the instances. DynamoDB is a fully managed, multi-AZ service that supports eventually consistent reads by default and requires no server maintenance, delivering the required high availability and consistency with the least operational overhead. Using AWS DMS performs the migration with minimal effort.

Option A and Option B are incorrect because replacing the ALB with a Network Load Balancer does nothing to improve availability of an application still running in a single Availability Zone, leaving a single point of failure. Options A and C are further incorrect because keeping the embedded NoSQL database with its own replication on the EC2 instances forces the company to operate and maintain the database itself, increasing operational overhead rather than reducing it as the requirement demands.

226.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Storage Gateway configured as a cached volume gateway stores the primary copy of the data in Amazon S3 while retaining a percentage of frequently accessed data in a local cache, giving on-premises systems low-latency local access. Data written to the gateway is automatically and securely uploaded to AWS over an encrypted connection, and mounting the iSCSI gateway volumes provides the local access the company needs, making this the correct backup replacement.

Options A and B are incorrect because Snowball and Snowball Edge are designed for one-time bulk data migration via physical shipment; they do not provide an ongoing, automatically synchronized backup with continuous local access.
