Option D is incorrect because a stored volume gateway keeps the primary dataset on premises and asynchronously backs up point-in-time snapshots to S3; while it provides local access, the cached volume configuration is the model that stores primary data in AWS as a backup solution while keeping a local cache, which is what this scenario describes.

227.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EFS provides a fully managed, POSIX-compliant shared file system that multiple Linux web servers can mount concurrently using standard NFS. Because EFS presents a normal file-system interface, the existing application can read and write shared content without any code changes, and EFS scales automatically as content grows, satisfying both the shared-access and no-application-change requirements.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 is an object store accessed through an API, not a mountable file system, so using it would require modifying the application. Option B is incorrect because CloudFront with an S3 origin is a content delivery solution for caching and distributing content, not a writable shared file store for application servers. Option D is incorrect because an Amazon EBS gp3 volume can be attached to only one instance at a time within an Availability Zone (Multi-Attach is limited to io1/io2 and is not a general-purpose shared file system), so it cannot serve as a shared file store mounted by all the web servers.

228.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon EFS is a fully managed shared file system that multiple Amazon EC2 instances can mount and access simultaneously over NFS. EFS stores data redundantly across multiple Availability Zones, providing the built-in redundancy required for core company assets, and it grows and shrinks automatically as files are added, so it scales seamlessly as the number of case files increases over time.

Option B is incorrect because an Amazon EBS volume is block storage attached to a single instance in one Availability Zone and cannot be simultaneously accessed by multiple application servers as a general-purpose shared file system. Option C is incorrect because S3 Glacier Deep Archive is designed for long-term cold archival with retrieval times of hours; it is not suitable for files that must be simultaneously and readily accessible by running application servers. Option D is incorrect because AWS Backup is a backup orchestration service, not a primary, concurrently accessible storage layer for active application data.

229.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Config provides a managed rule that continuously evaluates whether EBS volumes are encrypted and automatically flags any volume that is not compliant. This is the lowest-cost and lowest-effort approach because it requires no custom code or compute; you simply enable the managed rule and Config handles ongoing evaluation, recording, and compliance reporting, which directly standardizes the encryption check.

Option A is incorrect because writing custom API calls and scheduling them with EventBridge and Lambda introduces code that must be developed, tested, and maintained, increasing configuration effort. Option B is incorrect for the same reason and adds running a Fargate task, which incurs additional compute cost compared to a managed Config rule. Option C is incorrect because requiring tags through an IAM policy and using Cost Explorer to find untagged resources checks tagging, not encryption status, so it does not actually verify whether volumes are encrypted and relies on error-prone manual remediation.

230.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Cost Explorer lets you build and visualize a report of billed items and group costs by dimensions such as linked account, service, or tag, then download the report directly. This is the most efficient way to produce a breakdown suitable for creating department budgets without building any custom querying infrastructure.

Option A is incorrect because using Amazon Athena to query billing data requires first enabling and configuring Cost and Usage Reports delivery to S3 and writing SQL queries, which is far more setup and effort than the requirement warrants. Option C is incorrect because the billing dashboard bill details give a static summary of charges but do not provide the flexible grouping and report generation that Cost Explorer offers for budget planning. Option D is incorrect because AWS Budgets is designed to set spending thresholds and send alerts through Amazon SNS or email; it is a proactive alerting tool, not a mechanism for generating a historical report of billed items by user.

231.text:
Option C is correct because an AWS Transit Gateway acts as a central cloud router that connects many VPCs and on-premises networks through a single hub. You attach VPCs with VPC attachments and connect the data centers with VPN attachments, and as new accounts and VPCs are added you simply create additional attachments rather than building and maintaining a growing mesh of point-to-point links. This delivers simplified administration and scalability with the least administrative overhead.

Option A is incorrect because VPC peering is non-transitive and creates a full mesh that grows combinatorially as VPCs increase, making administration unmanageable at scale. Option B is incorrect because running VPN software on a self-managed EC2 instance creates a single point of failure and requires patching, scaling, and high-availability engineering, increasing overhead. Option D is incorrect because connecting a central VPC by Direct Connect and then using peering to reach other VPCs still relies on non-transitive peering, so traffic cannot flow between spokes and the design does not scale cleanly.

232.text:
Option C is correct because the Management VPC currently relies on VPNs that terminate at a single customer gateway device in the data center, so that single device is a single point of failure. Adding a second set of VPN connections from a second, independent customer gateway device removes that dependency, providing redundant on-premises termination points so connectivity survives the failure of either device or its tunnels.

Option A is incorrect because adding VPNs between the Management and Production VPCs addresses inter-VPC connectivity rather than the vulnerable single customer gateway, and the VPCs already communicate through peering. Option B is incorrect because the virtual private gateway belongs to the Production VPC's Direct Connect setup and is already backed by two Direct Connect connections; the Management VPC uses a customer gateway, so adding a virtual private gateway there does not fix the identified weakness. Option D is incorrect because a second VPC peering connection between the same two VPCs provides no additional resiliency, since peering is already a managed, redundant AWS service and the failure point is the customer gateway device.

233.text:
Option B is correct because creating a customer managed key in AWS KMS and using it with SSE-KMS gives the company full control over the key's lifecycle, including defining key policies for who can use it, enabling automatic or on-demand rotation, and disabling or scheduling deletion of the key. SSE-KMS handles the server-side encryption of S3 objects transparently, so the company meets its control requirements with minimal effort and no custom encryption code.

Option A is incorrect because SSE-S3 uses keys that AWS fully owns and manages, so the company cannot create, rotate on its own schedule, or disable those keys. Option C is incorrect because an AWS managed KMS key is controlled by AWS; its rotation is automatic and fixed, and customers cannot disable it or edit its key policy, so it does not satisfy the requirement for full user control. Option D is incorrect because downloading objects to EC2 to encrypt them manually introduces significant operational overhead and security risk and abandons the seamless server-side encryption S3 provides.

234.text:
Options A and D are correct because deploying an Amazon ECS cluster and running an ECS service with the AWS Fargate launch type gives the company a fully managed, serverless way to run containers. Fargate provisions and scales the underlying compute automatically, so there is no EC2 infrastructure to manage, and specifying a desired task count of two or more spreads the microservice tasks for availability while ECS service auto scaling adjusts capacity, minimizing ongoing maintenance and scaling effort.

Option B is incorrect because deploying the Kubernetes control plane on EC2 instances means self-managing the cluster control plane, which is exactly the additional infrastructure the company wants to avoid. Option C is incorrect because the ECS EC2 launch type requires the company to provision, patch, and scale the EC2 container instances, adding the infrastructure management the requirement prohibits. Option E is incorrect because running Kubernetes worker nodes on EC2 instances likewise forces the company to maintain and scale node infrastructure, conflicting with the goal of minimizing operational effort.

235.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon GuardDuty is a managed threat-detection service that continuously analyzes CloudTrail management and S3 data events, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs to identify malicious activity across the account, workloads, and access patterns to S3 buckets. Sending its findings to AWS Security Hub aggregates and reports the suspicious activity and presents it on a centralized security dashboard, satisfying every part of the requirement.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Macie focuses on discovering and classifying sensitive data in S3, not on detecting account-wide malicious activity or anomalous access behavior. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Inspector assesses workloads for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure rather than continuously monitoring for active threats and access patterns, and CloudTrail is a logging service, not a findings dashboard. Option D is incorrect because AWS Config tracks resource configuration changes and compliance, not malicious activity, so it cannot detect the attacks described or surface them as security findings.

236.text:
Option D is correct because copying automatic RDS snapshots to another Region every 24 hours aligns exactly with the 24-hour RPO and RTO and is the most cost-effective disaster recovery option, since cross-Region snapshot copies incur only storage and transfer charges and require no continuously running standby infrastructure. In a disaster, the company restores a new instance from the most recent copied snapshot in the secondary Region, comfortably within the 24-hour objectives.

Option A is incorrect because a cross-Region read replica keeps a running standby instance continuously, which costs more than periodic snapshot copies and provides recovery far tighter than the relaxed 24-hour requirement needs. Option B is incorrect because AWS DMS is intended for data migration and ongoing replication rather than RDS disaster recovery, adding replication-instance cost and operational complexity that the modest RPO/RTO does not justify. Option C is incorrect because exporting and copying native SQL Server backups to S3 across Regions is a more manual, higher-effort process than simply copying the automated RDS snapshots that AWS already creates.

237.text:
Option C is correct because the batch job runs on a predictable schedule, starting at 1 AM every night with the same peak capacity, so configuring scheduled scaling to scale the Auto Scaling group up to the desired level just before 1 AM ensures the capacity is already in place when the jobs begin. This avoids the hour-long ramp caused by reactive scaling, and the group can scale back down afterward, providing the fast availability the requirement needs cost-effectively.

Option A is incorrect because permanently increasing the minimum capacity would keep the extra instances running all day, wasting cost outside the nightly window. Option B is incorrect because increasing the maximum capacity only raises the ceiling for scaling; it does not make capacity available sooner, so the slow ramp-up persists. Option D is incorrect because adding more instances per scaling step still depends on reactive metric-driven triggers that begin only after the load appears, which does not guarantee the desired capacity is ready at the known 1 AM start time.

238.text:
Option C is correct because an Amazon RDS Multi-AZ DB cluster deployment provisions one writer and two readable standby instances across three Availability Zones. This delivers high availability with automatic failover while the two standby replicas can also serve read traffic, directly providing the increased read capacity the company needs. Because it is a native RDS deployment option for engines such as PostgreSQL, it meets these goals with the most operational efficiency and no custom replication management.

Option A is incorrect because the application requires a PostgreSQL engine, and DynamoDB is a NoSQL key-value service that would require re-architecting the data model. Option B is incorrect because a standard Multi-AZ deployment uses a single standby that does not accept read traffic, so it provides high availability but no additional read capacity. Option D is incorrect because cross-Region read replicas add read capacity in a different Region but do not provide synchronous high availability with automatic failover within the primary Region, and they introduce cross-Region replication overhead.

239.text:
Option B is correct because a Network Load Balancer operates at Layer 4 and natively supports the UDP protocol required for the multiplayer game's client-to-server communication, while scaling to handle large, spiky traffic with ultra-low latency in front of the Auto Scaling group. Amazon DynamoDB on-demand capacity mode stores the non-relational gamer scores and instantly scales throughput up and down to match unpredictable demand without any capacity planning or intervention, matching every requirement.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Route 53 provides DNS-based routing, not Layer 4 UDP load balancing across the instances, and Aurora Serverless is a relational engine for SQL workloads rather than the non-relational store specified. Option C is incorrect because, although NLB suits UDP, Aurora Global Database is a relational, multi-Region solution that does not fit the non-relational requirement and adds unneeded complexity. Option D is incorrect because an Application Load Balancer is Layer 7 and supports only HTTP/HTTPS, so it cannot handle the game's UDP traffic.

240.text:
Option C is correct because at 100 Mbps the company's internet link would take far longer than two weeks to move 600 TB, so a physical transfer is required. Ordering several AWS Snowball Edge Storage Optimized devices lets the company copy the data locally and ship it to AWS for import into Amazon S3, completing the transfer within the deadline. Snowball encrypts all data at rest on the device and in transit, satisfying the encryption requirement, and it is the most cost-effective option for this one-time bulk move.

Provide context: Option A is incorrect because S3 multi-part upload still relies on the 100 Mbps internet connection, which is far too slow to transfer 600 TB in two weeks. Option B is incorrect for the same bandwidth reason; a VPN traverses the same constrained internet link and cannot meet the deadline. Option D is incorrect because provisioning a 10 Gbps Direct Connect circuit typically takes weeks or months to establish and is costly for a one-time transfer, so it cannot satisfy the two-week, cost-effective requirement.

241.text:
Option B is correct because a rate-based rule in a Regional AWS WAF web ACL automatically counts requests from each source IP and blocks any IP that exceeds the configured threshold, which is precisely how to mitigate an HTTP flood attack. Associating the web ACL directly with the API Gateway stage protects the Regional API endpoint with a managed, fully serverless control that requires no custom code, giving the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because putting CloudFront in front of the endpoint with a long TTL caches responses but does not detect or throttle abusive request rates, so a flood of unique or non-cacheable requests still reaches the origin. Option C is incorrect because monitoring the Count metric with CloudWatch only alerts the team after the fact; it provides no automatic blocking and relies on manual response. Option D is incorrect because building rate-limiting logic in a Lambda@Edge function requires writing and maintaining custom code to track and block IPs, which is far more operational overhead than the native AWS WAF rate-based rule.

242.text:
Option D is correct because placing an Amazon SQS queue between the S3 event source and the Lambda function decouples ingestion from processing and durably buffers requests. During the marketing campaign, the burst of S3 uploads exceeded what Lambda could process, and direct S3-to-Lambda invocations have limited asynchronous retry behavior, so events were dropped. SQS retains messages durably, lets Lambda poll and scale consumers to match demand, and provides retries plus a dead-letter queue, ensuring no document is lost.

Option A is incorrect because increasing the timeout to 15 minutes addresses per-invocation duration, not the throttling and dropped events caused by a traffic spike. Option B is incorrect because S3 replication copies objects to another bucket for redundancy or region failover; it does nothing to reliably trigger or buffer processing. Option C is incorrect because deploying a second Lambda function and manually load balancing adds operational complexity, and Lambda already scales horizontally automatically, so this does not solve the underlying lack of durable buffering for the surge.

243.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon FSx for Lustre is a fully managed, high-performance file system purpose-built for workloads that require Lustre clients, such as the gaming application described. It delivers the low-latency, high-throughput shared access that compute-intensive applications need, integrates with S3, and removes the burden of provisioning and maintaining a Lustre cluster, satisfying both the Lustre client and fully managed requirements.

Option A is incorrect because AWS DataSync is a data transfer service for moving data between storage systems; it does not present a Lustre-compatible mountable file system for ongoing shared access. Option B is incorrect because AWS Storage Gateway file gateway exposes NFS and SMB file shares backed by S3, not the Lustre protocol. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EFS is an NFS-based elastic file system and cannot be configured to support Lustre clients; EFS and Lustre are distinct, incompatible protocols, so this option does not meet the stated requirement.

244.text:
Option A is correct because S3 Intelligent-Tiering is designed exactly for data with unknown or changing access patterns. A single Lifecycle rule transitions objects into Intelligent-Tiering, which then automatically moves each object between frequent and infrequent access tiers based on observed usage, optimizing cost with no performance impact, no retrieval fees, and no manual analysis. This delivers the most operational efficiency across many buckets with varying frequency.

Option B is incorrect because using storage class analysis and then manually moving each object is highly operationally intensive at petabyte scale and does not adapt automatically as patterns change. Option C is incorrect because S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval targets long-lived, rarely accessed data and would impose retrieval costs and risk for objects that may be accessed frequently. Option D is incorrect because S3 One Zone-IA stores data in a single Availability Zone, reducing durability against AZ loss, and assumes infrequent access, which contradicts the unknown access patterns described.

245.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon EBS fast snapshot restore (FSR) fully initializes volumes created from a snapshot, eliminating the latency normally incurred when blocks are lazily loaded from S3 on first access. By enabling FSR on the snapshot and building the AMI from it, instances launched by the Auto Scaling group reach full I/O performance immediately, providing the minimum initialization latency needed to absorb sudden demand spikes.

Option A is incorrect because register-image and Step Functions orchestration do not address first-access block initialization latency; the underlying volume would still hydrate lazily. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager automates snapshot and AMI creation and retention, but it does not pre-warm volumes to reduce launch latency. Option D is incorrect because using EventBridge and AWS Backup to provision AMIs adds orchestration complexity without warming the volume blocks, so newly launched instances would still suffer the initialization delay that FSR specifically removes.

246.text:
Option C is correct because GP3 volumes let you provision IOPS and throughput independently of capacity, which directly meets the requirement to decouple disk performance from storage size. GP3 supports up to 16,000 IOPS, comfortably above the 15,000 IOPS daily peak, and is more cost-effective than Provisioned IOPS volumes because the baseline performance is included and additional IOPS are inexpensive.

Option A is incorrect because GP2 ties performance to volume size at 3 IOPS per GiB, so achieving 15,000 IOPS would require over-provisioning capacity, increasing cost and coupling performance to size. Option B is incorrect because io2 is a Provisioned IOPS volume aimed at mission-critical, high-durability workloads and costs significantly more than GP3 for the same 15,000 IOPS. Option D is incorrect because io1 is also a higher-cost Provisioned IOPS type designed for very high or sustained IOPS needs; for a 15,000 IOPS workload it is not the most cost-effective choice compared to GP3.

247.text:
Option A is correct because the application tier currently runs in a single Availability Zone, which is the single point of failure. Placing the stateless EC2 instances in a Multi-AZ Auto Scaling group and fronting them with an Application Load Balancer distributes traffic across healthy instances in multiple AZs, automatically replaces failed instances, and scales with demand. Combined with the existing RDS Multi-AZ database, this makes the full stack highly available.

Option B is incorrect because taking EC2 snapshots and shipping them to another Region is a disaster-recovery or backup measure, not an active high-availability design, and involves manual recovery. Option C is incorrect because Route 53 latency-based routing assumes multiple regional endpoints already exist; it does not provide AZ-level redundancy for the single-AZ tier. Option D is incorrect because while a Multi-AZ ALB helps, adding Route 53 rules alone does not create redundant compute capacity; without an Auto Scaling group spanning AZs, the application tier remains a single point of failure.

248.text:
Options A and E are correct because AWS Organizations with all features enabled lets the company centrally create and govern many new accounts for its business units under a single consolidated structure. Setting up AWS IAM Identity Center within that organization and integrating it with the company's existing corporate directory provides centralized federated authentication, so users sign in with their corporate credentials and receive permissions across the member accounts.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon Cognito provides identity for application end users, not workforce single sign-on to AWS accounts, and IAM Identity Center is not designed to consume a Cognito identity pool for this purpose. Option C is incorrect because SCPs restrict permissions but do not authenticate users, and the phrasing of adding IAM Identity Center to Directory Service misrepresents the integration. Option D is incorrect because AWS Organizations does not authenticate users directly against Directory Service; authentication is handled through IAM Identity Center, making this option technically inaccurate.

249.text:
Option B is correct because AWS CloudFormation provides infrastructure as code, letting the company codify the validated prototype, including the Auto Scaling group, Application Load Balancer, and RDS database, into a reusable template. The same template can then be deployed repeatedly and consistently to create development and production environments spanning two Availability Zones in a fully automated, repeatable fashion, which is exactly what the company requires.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager manages and operates existing instances and configurations; it is not a service for declaratively provisioning a full stack of new infrastructure across AZs. Option C is incorrect because AWS Config records and evaluates resource configurations for compliance and inventory; it cannot deploy or provision infrastructure. Option D is incorrect because AWS Elastic Beanstalk provisions and manages its own opinionated environments and cannot simply reference an existing manually built prototype to reproduce that specific architecture, making CloudFormation the appropriate choice for faithful, automated replication.

250.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon EKS supports envelope encryption of Kubernetes secrets using a customer-managed AWS KMS key. By creating a KMS key and enabling EKS KMS secrets encryption (secrets encryption) on the cluster, the secrets stored in the etcd key-value store are encrypted with a KMS-managed data key, satisfying the requirement that all secrets in etcd are encrypted at rest.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Secrets Manager stores and rotates secrets externally to the cluster; it does not encrypt the secrets that Kubernetes natively stores inside etcd. Option C is incorrect because creating the cluster with default options and adding the Amazon EBS CSI driver only provisions persistent block storage for pods and does nothing to encrypt etcd secrets. Option D is incorrect because enabling default EBS volume encryption with the aws/ebs key encrypts EBS volumes, not the Kubernetes secrets held in the etcd datastore, so it does not meet the stated requirement.

251.text:
Option B is correct because S3 Object Lock, when used with S3 Versioning, applies a retention period that prevents any user, including administrators, from modifying or deleting the objects until the designated date, satisfying the write-once-read-many requirement in the most secure way. Configuring static website hosting and a bucket policy that grants read-only access makes the files publicly readable while the lock enforces immutability.

Option A is incorrect because IAM read-only permissions can be changed at any time and rely on configuration rather than enforced immutability, so files could still be altered or deleted. Option C is incorrect because a Lambda-based restore is reactive; it permits a window during which objects can be modified or deleted before being replaced, and it adds operational complexity. Option D is incorrect because S3 Object Lock is enabled at the bucket and object level, not on a selected folder, and pairing it only with mutable IAM permissions does not provide the guaranteed immutability that Object Lock with a retention period delivers.

252.text:
Option C is correct because the most secure and standard way to grant permissions to users organized into IAM groups is to author an IAM policy that follows least privilege and attach it directly to each group. Users added to a group automatically inherit its permissions, so as new department users are onboarded they receive exactly the access defined by the group's policy, keeping permissions consistent, auditable, and tightly scoped.

Option A is incorrect because service control policies operate at the AWS Organizations account or OU level to set permission guardrails; they do not grant permissions to individual users in groups. Option B is incorrect because IAM roles are assumed, not attached to groups, and you cannot grant group members permissions by attaching a role to a group. Option D is incorrect because a permissions boundary only sets the maximum permissions an identity can have; it does not by itself grant any permissions, so it cannot serve as the mechanism to give the new users their access.

253.text:
Option D is correct because an egress-only internet gateway is purpose-built for IPv6 traffic, allowing instances in the VPC to initiate outbound connections to the internet while statefully blocking any externally initiated inbound connections. This precisely satisfies the security policy that EC2 instances must reach external services but external services must never start a connection to the instances.

Option A is incorrect because a NAT gateway provides outbound-only access for IPv4 private addresses, not IPv6, so it cannot serve the IPv6 instances described. Option B is incorrect because a standard internet gateway permits bidirectional traffic, which would allow external services to initiate inbound connections to the instances, violating the security policy. Option C is incorrect because a virtual private gateway is used to establish VPN or Direct Connect links to on-premises networks, not to provide controlled outbound IPv6 internet access, so it does not meet the requirement.

254.text:
Option B is correct because the requirement explicitly calls for REST APIs, so designing a REST API in Amazon API Gateway is appropriate. To reach backend containers running in private VPC subnets on Amazon ECS, a private integration using a VPC link is required; the VPC link lets API Gateway forward requests securely to a Network Load Balancer fronting the private ECS tasks without exposing them to the internet.

Options A and C are incorrect because they specify a WebSocket API, but the application must present REST APIs, so a WebSocket API does not meet the stated interface requirement. Option D is incorrect because, although it correctly uses a REST API, it proposes attaching a security group to allow API Gateway to access ECS; API Gateway is a managed service that does not reside in your VPC, so it cannot reach private subnets through a security group. Private connectivity to ECS in a private subnet must be established through a VPC link, which only option B provides.

255.text:
Option A is correct because AWS DataSync, using an agent deployed in the corporate data center, transfers the 700 TB of NAS data over the existing 10 Gbps Direct Connect connection efficiently and securely. DataSync performs incremental, parallelized transfers with built-in verification, and because the source NAS remains online, the company can continue to access and update the data during the transfer, comfortably completing within the 90-day window without disruption.

Option B is incorrect because Snowball Edge is an offline transfer method suited to limited or no network bandwidth; with a 10 Gbps Direct Connect link already available, shipping devices is unnecessary, and mounting an S3 bucket on the on-premises file system is not how Snowball operates. Option C is incorrect because rsync over Direct Connect is a manual, error-prone approach lacking the scheduling, parallelism, integrity checking, and management of DataSync. Option D is incorrect because backing up to physical tapes and shipping them is slow, operationally heavy, and does not support continued live access and updates during transfer.

256.text:
Option C is correct because a Compute Savings Plan delivers the broadest discount, applying flexibly across both the EC2 instances and the Lambda functions, which maximizes savings on all application resources as the number of functions grows over the at-least one-year term. Connecting the Lambda functions to the private subnet that contains the EC2 instances gives the required direct network access and keeps latency low because traffic stays within the VPC, while optimizing duration, memory, invocations, and data transfer further reduces cost.

Option A is incorrect because an EC2 Instance Savings Plan does not cover Lambda usage, so it fails to maximize savings across all resources. Option B is incorrect for the same Savings Plan reason and because placing Lambda in a public subnet adds no benefit and is unnecessary for reaching private EC2 instances. Option D is incorrect because keeping Lambda in the Lambda service VPC prevents the required direct private network access to the EC2 instances and can introduce additional latency.

257.text:
Option A is correct because a public virtual interface over the existing AWS Direct Connect connection lets the on-premises application reach Amazon S3 public endpoints privately across the dedicated link, without any internet access. This is the most cost-effective option because it uses the existing Direct Connect connection and avoids the hourly and data-processing charges of additional infrastructure like NAT gateways.

Option B is incorrect because creating a VPC and NAT gateway introduces ongoing per-hour and per-GB processing costs and is designed for outbound internet access from private subnets, which does not fit reaching S3 from an isolated on-premises network. Option C is incorrect because an S3 interface endpoint (powered by AWS PrivateLink) carries per-hour and per-GB charges and additional complexity, making it less cost-effective than a public VIF for this use case. Option D is incorrect because VPC peering connects two VPCs and cannot be established between an on-premises network and Direct Connect, so it does not provide the required S3 access.

258.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Global Accelerator improves the performance of TCP and UDP applications by routing user traffic onto the AWS global network at the nearest edge location and using optimized, congestion-aware paths back to the application. For globally distributed reporters streaming via RTMP over TCP, this provides the accelerated, more reliable TCP connections to the broadcast system that the solution requires, improving stream quality.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network optimized for caching and distributing content outbound to viewers, not for accelerating inbound RTMP ingest connections from broadcasters. Option C is incorrect because AWS Client VPN provides secure remote network access but does not accelerate or optimize TCP transport performance for live streaming. Option D is incorrect because deploying EC2 instances with Elastic IP addresses provides static public endpoints but no global network acceleration, so connections still traverse the public internet without the path optimization Global Accelerator delivers.

259.text:
Option C is correct because a presigned URL grants time-limited, scoped access to the specific CloudFormation template object in the private S3 bucket without weakening the bucket's Block Public Access settings. Generating the presigned URL in response to a specific user request and configuring the stack to use it follows least-privilege security best practices and provides on-demand access only when the test environment needs to be created.

Option A is incorrect because a gateway VPC endpoint provides private connectivity for resources inside a VPC to reach S3; CloudFormation accessing a template by object URL is not solved by an endpoint, and it does not grant the controlled, request-based access required. Option B is incorrect because fronting the bucket with an API Gateway REST API adds unnecessary complexity and a new access surface rather than securely granting scoped access to one object. Option D is incorrect because allowing public access, even temporarily, violates security best practices and exposes the template during the window it is public.

260.text:
Option A is correct because the S3 Storage Lens dashboard, with advanced metrics enabled, provides organization-wide and per-bucket activity and usage analytics out of the box. It surfaces access patterns and identifies buckets that are no longer accessed or rarely accessed without building any custom pipeline, delivering the goal with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because the basic S3 console dashboard does not provide the detailed activity and access-pattern analytics needed to reliably identify rarely accessed buckets, requiring manual interpretation. Option C is incorrect because the CloudWatch BucketSizeBytes metric reports storage size, not access frequency, and wiring it into Amazon Athena to infer access patterns adds significant setup and maintenance effort. Option D is incorrect because enabling CloudTrail object-level logging and analyzing logs through CloudWatch Logs introduces substantial configuration, storage, and analysis overhead, making it far more operationally intensive than the purpose-built Storage Lens dashboard.

261.text:
Option C is correct because AWS DataSync is a managed online transfer service that maximizes performance through parallel, multi-threaded transfers and integrity verification, and it natively supports migrating data into Amazon FSx for Windows File Server. Critically, DataSync lets you set a bandwidth limit on each task, so the laboratory can throttle usage to minimize impact on the shared 1 Gbps university link while still completing the 30 TB transfer within the five-day deadline.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Snowcone is a small physical device for offline or edge transfers; shipping it adds latency and is unnecessary given an available network link, and it is not the optimal fit for a managed online migration with bandwidth control. Option B is incorrect because Amazon FSx File Gateway provides on-premises cached access to FSx, not a controlled bulk migration service. Option D is incorrect because AWS Transfer Family delivers managed SFTP, FTPS, and FTP endpoints for ongoing file exchange and lacks the optimized, bandwidth-throttled bulk migration capabilities that DataSync provides.

262.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon ECS is a managed container orchestration service that requires minimal application changes, and configuring Service Auto Scaling with target tracking maintains performance automatically. Setting the minimum capacity to 3 and using a task placement strategy that spreads tasks across the Availability Zone attribute ensures tasks are distributed across the three AZs, delivering high availability with the least operational overhead, especially when run on AWS Fargate-style managed compute.

Option B is incorrect because EKS self-managed nodes require you to provision, patch, and manage the worker nodes, adding considerable operational overhead compared with ECS. Option C is incorrect because manually launching EC2 instances in a spread placement group with Reserved Instances and an Auto Scaling group is more operationally intensive and a placement group does not guarantee balanced distribution across three AZs as cleanly as ECS task spread. Option D is incorrect because moving a containerized application to AWS Lambda would require significant re-architecture, violating the minimal-changes requirement.

263.text:
Option B is correct because deploying EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group across multiple Availability Zones removes the single-AZ point of failure and automatically replaces unhealthy instances, while a Multi-AZ RDS deployment provides synchronous standby failover for the database. Distributing static assets through Amazon CloudFront improves resilience and performance by caching content at edge locations, together producing the robust, resilient architecture the company wants.

Option A is incorrect because deploying compute and the database in a single Availability Zone leaves the application exposed to an AZ failure, and S3 versioning alone does not provide application high availability. Option C is incorrect because the web tier remains in a single AZ, so a single-AZ outage takes the application offline, and serving static assets directly from EC2 increases load and reduces resilience. Option D is incorrect because storing static assets in EFS One Zone-IA confines them to one Availability Zone, undermining durability and availability, which conflicts with the goal of a resilient architecture.

264.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP is a fully managed service that natively supports both NFS and SMB protocols and, importantly, allows the same data to be accessed across both protocols through its multi-protocol access feature. Deployed in a Multi-AZ configuration, it provides redundancy at the Availability Zone level, consolidating the company's Windows SMB and Linux NFS file shares into a single managed solution.

Option B is incorrect because running two self-managed EC2 instances places the burden of patching, scaling, high availability, and protocol management on the company and does not provide a managed service or cross-protocol sharing. Option C is incorrect because Amazon FSx for Lustre is a high-performance computing file system, not an NFS file-sharing service for general workloads, and splitting protocols across two systems prevents sharing the same data between them. Option D is incorrect because Amazon S3 with an S3 File Gateway is object storage fronted by a file interface and does not deliver true simultaneous multi-protocol NFS and SMB file sharing of the same data.

265.text:
Option B is correct because Network Load Balancers now support security groups, and recreating the NLB with a security group that allows only trusted IP addresses restricts access at the network layer with minimal architectural change. This directly prevents unauthorized source IPs from reaching the application while preserving the existing NLB-based TCP design for real-time processing.

Option A is incorrect because AWS WAF integrates with Application Load Balancers, Amazon CloudFront, and API Gateway to inspect Layer 7 HTTP traffic; it cannot be applied directly to a Network Load Balancer, which operates at Layer 4. Option C is incorrect because deploying a second parallel NLB adds infrastructure and complexity without cleanly restricting access to the existing endpoint, and is not a minimal change. Option D is incorrect because AWS Shield Advanced focuses on mitigating DDoS attacks rather than enforcing an IP allow list to block general unauthorized access attempts, so it does not meet the stated requirement as precisely as a security group.

266.text:
Option D is correct because creating separate AWS KMS keys for each customer provides true cryptographic isolation, ensuring each customer's financial data is encrypted with a distinct key. KMS is a centralized, managed key management service, so per-key granular IAM and key policy access controls plus AWS CloudTrail logging satisfy compliance requirements with minimal operational overhead and no infrastructure to maintain.

Option A is incorrect because storing raw encryption keys in an S3 bucket is insecure, bypasses KMS entirely, and offers no centralized key management or rotation. Option B introduces significant operational overhead by requiring you to deploy, patch, and manage a hardware security appliance, contradicting the LEAST overhead goal; CloudHSM or KMS already provide managed key custody. Option C is incorrect because a single shared KMS key for all customers provides no cryptographic separation between tenants, failing the requirement that each customer's data be encrypted separately and making blast-radius isolation impossible.

267.text:
Option A is correct because a single io1 EBS volume is already provisioned at 64,000 IOPS, which is the maximum IOPS for a single io1 volume attached to most instances. To exceed that ceiling you must attach multiple io1 volumes and stripe them with an OS-level LVM (or RAID 0) configuration, aggregating the IOPS and throughput across volumes to surpass the per-volume limit and improve database storage performance.

Option B is incorrect because 64,000 IOPS is the per-volume maximum for io1, so you cannot simply increase a single volume beyond it. Option C is incorrect because increasing capacity to 2 TB does not raise IOPS on a Provisioned IOPS volume; IOPS is provisioned independently of size and the bottleneck is the IOPS ceiling, not storage space. Option D is incorrect because changing to a storage optimized instance type without adding EBS capacity does not raise the EBS volume's IOPS limit; the io1 volume remains capped at 64,000 IOPS, so storage performance is unchanged.

268.text:
Options B and D are correct because the application operates at the OSI transport layer (TCP/Layer 4), which is exactly what a Network Load Balancer handles, distributing connections across targets. Pairing the NLB with an Auto Scaling group spanning multiple Availability Zones, using EC2 health checks, delivers high availability cost-effectively: unhealthy instances are replaced automatically and traffic is balanced across healthy targets in multiple AZs.

Option A is incorrect because Route 53 DNS-based routing alone provides no connection-level load balancing or rapid health-based failover, and managing instances without an ASB is less resilient. Option C is incorrect because adding an Application Load Balancer for HTTP/HTTPS is unnecessary for a transport-layer application and adds cost; an ALB operates at Layer 7, not the required Layer 4. Option E is incorrect because a CloudWatch alarm that merely restarts stopped instances does not provide multi-AZ high availability or load distribution, and it leaves the workload in a single Availability Zone exposed to an AZ failure.

269.text:
Option B is correct because S3 Object Lock in compliance mode enforces a write-once-read-many (WORM) model where a protected object version cannot be overwritten or deleted by any user, including the root account, for the duration of the retention period. This provides the strongest immutability guarantee, which is exactly what regulatory data retention policies require for fixed-period protection from deletion or modification.

Option A is incorrect because Object Lock governance mode allows users with the s3:BypassGovernanceRetention permission to alter or delete objects, so it does not strictly prevent modification for regulatory compliance. Option C is incorrect because versioning with a lifecycle deletion policy does not prevent users from deleting or overwriting objects and actively removes data after a period, the opposite of immutable retention. Option D is incorrect because transitioning objects to S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval changes storage class for cost savings but does not lock objects against modification or deletion, so the documents remain mutable.

270.text:
Option A is correct because a long-running cluster keeps the primary and core nodes available for the daily 6-hour workload, and placing core nodes on On-Demand Instances protects HDFS data, since core nodes store data on the cluster. Running only the task nodes on Spot Instances reduces cost without risking data loss, because task nodes do not store HDFS data and their interruption only delays compute, not the dataset.

Option B is incorrect because a transient cluster terminates after the job, which is fine, but the description still uses the same node placement as A; A is preferred for a consistent recurring daily workload, though the data-safety rationale is identical. Options C and D are incorrect because they place core nodes on Spot Instances. Core nodes hold HDFS data, so a Spot interruption can cause data loss, directly violating the requirement that no data be lost while the process runs.

271.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon API Gateway with AWS Lambda and Amazon DynamoDB is a fully serverless, managed architecture with the least operational overhead. API Gateway receives the sensor HTTP requests, Lambda processes and aggregates per-tenant usage, and DynamoDB durably stores the data with automatic scaling. This event-driven design is loosely coupled, making it easy to add independent components as future features are introduced.

Option B is incorrect because an Elastic Load Balancer with an Auto Scaling group of EC2 instances requires managing servers (patching, scaling, capacity), increasing operational overhead compared to serverless. Option C is incorrect because it adds a self-managed Microsoft SQL Server Express database on EC2, reintroducing instance management and licensing concerns that contradict using managed services with least overhead. Option D is incorrect because it both relies on self-managed EC2 instances and stores aggregated data on Amazon EFS, a shared file system that is not suited to structured, queryable per-tenant usage records and still carries the operational burden of managing instances.

272.text:
Option D is correct because the standard, secure way to grant a Lambda function permissions to AWS services is to create an IAM execution role with the required Amazon S3 permissions and attach it to the function. Lambda assumes this role at runtime, obtaining temporary, automatically rotated credentials, so no long-term keys are embedded in code and access follows least-privilege best practices.

Option A is incorrect because a Lambda resource policy controls who or what can invoke the function, not what AWS resources the function itself may access; it cannot grant S3 write permissions. Option B is incorrect because manually creating signed requests with a user's IAM credentials is unnecessary, insecure, and requires embedding or managing static credentials rather than using a role. Option C is incorrect because creating a new IAM user and hardcoding its long-term credentials in the Lambda function is an anti-pattern that exposes static secrets and increases security risk; execution roles with temporary credentials are the recommended approach.

273.text:
Option C is correct because a mix of On-Demand and Spot Instances optimizes cost savings without any long-term commitment. Spot Instances offer the deepest discounts (up to 90% off On-Demand) and integrate well with an Auto Scaling group that scales with demand, while On-Demand Instances provide a baseline of reliable capacity. Neither purchasing option requires a one- or three-year commitment.

Option A is incorrect because Dedicated Instances are the most expensive option and provide physical isolation that this workload does not need, offering no cost savings. Option B is incorrect because On-Demand Instances only carry the standard rate with no discount, so they do not optimize cost savings. Option D is incorrect because Reserved Instances require a one-year or three-year commitment to achieve their discount, which directly violates the stated requirement to avoid a long-term commitment.

274.text:
Options B and C are correct because an Auto Scaling group launching private EC2 instances across multiple Availability Zones behind a new Application Load Balancer makes the web tier scalable and highly available. Migrating MySQL to an Amazon Aurora MySQL cluster with the primary and a reader instance in separate Availability Zones provides database high availability and, critically, offloads reads to the reader DB instance to reduce MySQL read latency.

Option A is incorrect because a single second EC2 instance in another Region with failover routing is not scalable and adds cross-Region complexity without an auto-scaling, load-balanced tier. Option D is incorrect because an Auto Scaling group cannot span multiple AWS Regions, and an Application Load Balancer is regional, so this configuration is not valid. Option E is incorrect because Aurora cross-Region read replicas address global read distribution and disaster recovery rather than reducing local read latency, adding unnecessary cross-Region cost and complexity for this single-Region requirement.

275.text:
Option A is correct because creating an Amazon Route 53 public hosted zone and importing the existing zone file is the fastest way to migrate authoritative DNS for an internet-facing domain to a resilient AWS managed service. A public hosted zone resolves queries from the public internet, and Route 53 provides high availability and a global anycast network, after which you update the registrar's name servers to point to Route 53.

Option B is incorrect because a private hosted zone only resolves DNS within associated VPCs and is not reachable from the public internet, so a public website's records would not resolve for external users. Option C is incorrect because Simple AD and AWS Directory Service provide directory services for Active Directory, not public authoritative DNS hosting for an internet domain. Option D is incorrect because a Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoint forwards DNS queries from on-premises into a VPC for hybrid resolution; it does not host or migrate the public authoritative records for the domain.

276.text:
Option C is correct because creating a 7-day retention rule in Recycle Bin for EBS snapshots requires the least development effort and directly prevents data loss. Recycle Bin retains deleted snapshots for the configured period so an accidental deletion, such as one caused by the cleanup script, can be recovered. After the 7-day window the snapshots are permanently removed, satisfying the requirement not to retain them indefinitely.

Option A is incorrect because denying snapshot deletion in the IAM policy would also block the legitimate cleanup script, breaking the intended expiration workflow rather than providing recoverability. Option B is incorrect because copying snapshots to another Region adds cost and a custom process without preventing the same script from deleting the originals, and it implies indefinite retention. Option D is incorrect because EBS snapshots cannot simply be copied to S3 Standard-IA as objects; this would require significant custom development and still does not protect against accidental deletion within the existing workflow.

277.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Shield Advanced is the managed DDoS protection service that includes proactive engagement from the AWS Shield Response Team (SRT) and access to the DDoS response team during attacks. Adding the Application Load Balancer and Route 53 hosted zones as protected resources extends advanced detection, automatic mitigation, and cost protection across the application's internet-facing components.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Config evaluates resource configuration compliance and has no capability to detect or mitigate DDoS attacks. Option B is incorrect because AWS WAF filters Layer 7 web requests using rules and is not a managed DDoS service with proactive engagement; it lacks the dedicated response team and infrastructure-layer protections of Shield Advanced. Option C is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat detection service analyzing logs for malicious activity and does not provide proactive DDoS mitigation or automated DDoS prevention, and it cannot take the preventative actions described.

278.text:
Option A is correct because resizing the EC2 instance to a type with more CPU addresses the high CPU utilization, and constraining the Auto Scaling group to a fixed size of 1 respects the application's single-instance design constraint. Adding an RDS read replica offloads read requests to a separate instance, improving read performance while writes continue to flow to the primary database.

Option B is incorrect because redirecting all read and write traffic to the read replica is invalid; RDS read replicas are read-only and cannot accept writes. Option C is incorrect because scaling the Auto Scaling group to a maximum of 2 violates the application's requirement to run on only a single EC2 instance, and resizing the DB instance alone does not separate read traffic as effectively as a read replica. Option D is incorrect because simply resizing the RDS DB instance adds cost and does not offload reads to a dedicated replica; a read replica is the targeted way to improve read request performance.

279.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon Kinesis Data Streams natively ingests high-volume streaming data from many producers in real time, and delivering that data into Amazon OpenSearch Service provides exactly the search and near-real-time analytics capability the on-premises search application requires. Amazon QuickSight then connects to OpenSearch to build visualizations, forming a fully AWS-native streaming, search, and visualization pipeline.

Option A is incorrect because batching data into S3 and querying with Athena introduces latency that does not meet the real-time search and update requirement, and it is not a streaming search engine. Option B is incorrect because Amazon EMR and Redshift target large-scale batch analytics and data warehousing rather than real-time streaming ingestion and full-text search. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EKS would require self-managing containerized ingestion, DynamoDB is a key-value store not a search engine, and CloudWatch dashboards are for operational metrics, not application search and visualization of streaming data.

280.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon SQS durably stores the job items in a queue, decoupling producers from consumers so the application is loosely coupled and no work is lost. An Auto Scaling group built from the launch template scales EC2 instances based on the number of items in the SQS queue (using a backlog-per-instance target tracking metric), so processing runs in parallel and instances are added or removed precisely as the job count changes.

Option A is incorrect because SNS is a pub/sub notification service that pushes messages and does not durably retain unprocessed jobs for polling, so it does not satisfy durable storage, and scaling on CPU does not track job backlog. Option B is incorrect because, although SQS is appropriate, scaling on network usage is an indirect signal that does not accurately reflect the number of pending jobs. Option D is incorrect because SNS does not durably store job items for worker retrieval, and scaling on messages published to a topic does not reflect the actual processing backlog.

281.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Aurora Serverless automatically scales database capacity up and down based on actual demand, making it the most cost-effective managed relational option for a variable, read-heavy workload with occasional writes. The company pays only for the capacity consumed, avoiding over-provisioning during low-usage periods while still scaling to meet read spikes and supporting steady user growth.

Option A is incorrect because provisioning RDS with Provisioned IOPS SSD storage incurs fixed, always-on capacity and premium storage costs that are not cost-optimal for variable usage. Option C is incorrect because DynamoDB is a NoSQL key-value and document database, but the requirement explicitly calls for a relational database, so it does not fit the data model. Option D is incorrect because magnetic storage is a legacy, low-performance EBS option ill-suited to the required performance, and statically provisioned read replicas add fixed cost without the automatic scale-to-demand economics of Aurora Serverless.

282.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Compute Optimizer is a managed service that analyzes utilization and delivers right-sizing and cost-optimization recommendations across EC2 instances, Auto Scaling groups, and EBS volumes from a single service. This provides the most operationally efficient solution because all three resource types are covered automatically without building custom reports or analysis.

Option A is incorrect because the AWS Cost and Usage Report provides detailed billing line items but does not itself generate right-sizing recommendations, requiring manual analysis. Option B is incorrect because CloudWatch billing alerts only notify you when spending crosses thresholds and provide no resource-level optimization recommendations. Option D is incorrect because it is less efficient than option C: it uses Compute Optimizer only for EC2 and then forces manual searching of a Cost and Usage Report for the Auto Scaling group and EBS volumes, even though Compute Optimizer already covers all three resource types directly.

283.text:
Option B is correct because a VPC endpoint, specifically a gateway VPC endpoint for Amazon S3, allows EC2 instances to access S3 directly over the AWS private network. Traffic between the VPC and S3 stays on the AWS backbone and never traverses the public internet, satisfying the security officer's compliance requirement without needing a NAT gateway or internet gateway for that traffic.

Option A is incorrect because AWS KMS provides encryption key management; the objects are already encrypted, and KMS does nothing to keep the network path off the public internet. Option C is incorrect because placing instances in a private subnet alone does not provide private connectivity to S3; without a VPC endpoint, S3-bound traffic would still route through a NAT gateway and the public internet. Option D is incorrect because a virtual private gateway is used for VPN or AWS Direct Connect connectivity to on-premises networks, not for private access to the regional S3 service.

284.text:
Option B is correct because hosting the static frontend on Amazon S3 fronted by Amazon CloudFront delivers the content securely and globally through edge locations with TLS. Refactoring the microservices to AWS Lambda behind Amazon API Gateway provides a fully serverless, scalable backend with no servers to manage, and migrating the database to Amazon RDS for MySQL offloads database administration, together delivering global availability and security with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because it keeps MySQL on a self-managed EC2 Reserved Instance, retaining patching, backup, and high-availability burdens rather than using the managed RDS service. Option C is incorrect because Lambda functions cannot be registered in a target group behind a Network Load Balancer; only Application Load Balancers support Lambda targets, making this design invalid. Option D is incorrect because it serves the frontend from S3 without CloudFront, reducing global performance and security, and again uses a self-managed EC2 MySQL instance instead of RDS, increasing operational overhead.

285.text:
Option D is correct because transferring 10 PB over a 500 Mbps link, even using 80% of the bandwidth, would take far longer than the 6-week deadline. Ordering multiple AWS Snowball devices, copying the data locally at high speed, and shipping them to AWS for ingestion into Amazon S3 is the only practical way to move petabyte-scale data within the timeframe without saturating the limited internet uplink shared by other applications.

Options A, B, and C are all incorrect because they transfer data over the internet uplink. At roughly 400 Mbps of usable bandwidth, moving 10 PB would require many months or years, vastly exceeding 6 weeks. AWS DataSync (A), rsync (B), and the AWS CLI with parallel copies (C) optimize online transfers but cannot overcome the fundamental physical bandwidth limit of the 500 Mbps shared link, so none can meet the deadline for this data volume.

286.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon RDS Proxy maintains a pool of established database connections and manages failover on behalf of the application. During a Multi-AZ failover, RDS Proxy automatically routes connections to the new primary, reducing failover times by up to 66% and preserving application connections, which directly addresses the customer-reported application timeouts.

Option B is incorrect because a read replica serves read traffic and improves read scalability but does nothing to reduce failover time for the primary; in fact standard read replicas are separate instances, not an automatic failover mechanism. Option C is incorrect because Performance Insights is a monitoring and diagnostics tool that helps identify load and bottlenecks but provides no reduction in failover time. Option D is incorrect because taking and copying snapshots across Regions supports backup and disaster recovery, not faster failover; restoring from a snapshot is far slower than a Multi-AZ failover and would not resolve the timeout issue.

287.text:
Option B is correct because AWS DMS Serverless automatically provisions, scales, and de-provisions replication capacity based on the actual workload, allocating only the capacity the replication requires. Because the volume of changes to replicate from the on-premises Oracle database to Amazon RDS for Oracle varies throughout the day, serverless capacity management matches resources to demand without manual instance sizing.

Option A is incorrect because a Multi-AZ replication instance improves availability through a standby in another Availability Zone but uses a fixed, statically provisioned instance size, so it does not allocate only the required capacity. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling does not manage the size of a DMS replication instance; DMS replication instances are not members of an EC2 Auto Scaling group. Option D is incorrect because manually building a DMS replication solution on Amazon ECS with AWS Fargate is unnecessary and operationally complex, since DMS Serverless already provides automatic capacity provisioning natively.

288.text:
Option A is correct because a canary release deployment in API Gateway lets you deploy the new API version alongside the existing version and shift only a configurable percentage of production traffic to the canary. This minimizes customer impact and data loss because most traffic continues hitting the stable version while you verify the new one, and after validation you promote the canary to receive all production traffic.

Option B is incorrect because importing a new API in merge mode to a new endpoint and deploying directly to production does not provide gradual, percentage-based traffic shifting or safe rollback, increasing customer risk. Option C is incorrect for the same reason, and overwrite mode replaces the existing definition outright, which is riskier and offers no controlled exposure of the new version. Option D is incorrect because pointing the Route 53 alias to an entirely new endpoint is an abrupt all-at-once cutover that lacks incremental traffic control and can cause customer disruption and in-flight request loss during the switch.

289.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built to securely store database credentials and natively supports automatic rotation, including built-in rotation for Amazon RDS, on a schedule such as every 30 days. The Lambda function retrieves the connection credentials from Secrets Manager at runtime, eliminating hardcoded secrets and providing the least operational overhead through managed, automated rotation.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store does not provide native automatic secret rotation; achieving rotation would require building and maintaining custom logic, adding operational overhead. Option C is incorrect because storing credentials in an encrypted Lambda environment variable and writing a custom scheduled rotation function reintroduces significant development and maintenance effort, the opposite of least overhead. Option D is incorrect because AWS KMS manages encryption keys, not database credentials; KMS key rotation rotates cryptographic key material and cannot store or rotate a database username and password connection string.

290.text:
Option C is correct because a Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB) is purpose-built to insert third-party virtual security appliances transparently into the traffic path. By deploying the GWLB in the centralized networking account and exposing it through GWLB endpoints in each application account, traffic is sent for inspection using the GENEVE protocol while preserving the original source and destination. This provides a scalable, managed way to inspect bump-in-the-wire traffic across accounts.

Option A is incorrect because an NLB combined with an interface VPC endpoint distributes Layer 4 traffic to targets but does not transparently insert an inline inspection appliance or preserve flow symmetry for stateful inspection. Option B is incorrect because an Application Load Balancer operates at Layer 7 and routes application requests; it cannot transparently mirror or insert all inter-application traffic into a security appliance. Option D is incorrect because an interface VPC endpoint alone provides private connectivity to a service but offers no mechanism to forward and inspect traffic through a security appliance.

291.text:
Option D is correct because an Application Load Balancer distributes incoming web traffic at Layer 7, EC2 Auto Scaling groups automatically add and remove capacity to absorb traffic spikes without degrading the customer experience, and Amazon SQS decouples order intake by durably buffering unprocessed orders so none are lost during surges. Amazon RDS with a Multi-AZ deployment then provides a durable, highly available store for processed orders with automatic failover.

Option A is incorrect because a NAT gateway provides outbound internet access for private subnets and does not distribute inbound web traffic to the application. Option B is incorrect because chaining an NLB to an ALB is unnecessary, and Amazon Redshift is a data warehouse for analytics, not an appropriate transactional store for live customer orders. Option C is incorrect because a Gateway Load Balancer is for inserting inline appliances, not for managing web traffic, and a GWLB cannot capture or store unprocessed orders as a queue would.

292.text:
Option C is correct because an S3 event notification that invokes an AWS Lambda function executes only when a photo is actually uploaded, so you pay only for the roughly 150 short invocations per day. This event-driven, serverless pattern requires no idle infrastructure, scales automatically, and is the most cost-effective way to generate thumbnails on demand and write them to the second bucket.

Option A is incorrect because a long-running Amazon EMR cluster polled every minute incurs continuous cluster costs and is vastly oversized for generating about 150 thumbnails daily. Option B is incorrect because an always-on, memory-optimized EC2 instance polling every minute runs and bills 24/7 regardless of upload activity, wasting money during idle periods. Option D is incorrect because S3 Storage Lens is an analytics and storage-metrics dashboard; it cannot invoke a Lambda function in response to object uploads, so it is technically unable to trigger the workflow.

293.text:
Option B is correct because enabling detailed monitoring on the EC2 instances publishes CloudWatch metrics at 1-minute granularity instead of the default 5-minute basic monitoring. A 1-minute resolution satisfies the requirement for analysis granularity of no more than 2 minutes, and CloudWatch metrics let the solutions architect track and analyze performance during the holiday traffic surge directly.

Option A is incorrect because shipping CloudWatch logs to Amazon Redshift and analyzing them in QuickSight is an overly complex pipeline that does not by itself improve the metric collection interval. Option C is incorrect because building a Lambda function to fetch logs adds unnecessary custom code and still relies on whatever monitoring resolution is configured, so it does not inherently deliver sub-2-minute metrics. Option D is incorrect because exporting logs to S3 and processing them through Redshift and QuickSight is a batch analytics workflow that introduces latency and overhead rather than providing near-real-time performance metrics at fine granularity.

294.text:
Option D is correct because creating an Amazon RDS read replica in the same us-east-1 Region offloads the read-heavy reporting and ML forecasting queries from the primary instance, relieving the contention that slows the database during business hours. A same-Region read replica is the most cost-effective option because it avoids cross-Region data transfer charges while still providing a dedicated, near real-time read endpoint.

Option A is incorrect because a cross-Region read replica works but adds inter-Region data transfer costs and replication latency that are unnecessary when the application runs in us-east-1. Option B is incorrect because the Multi-AZ standby is a passive failover target that cannot serve read traffic, so it would not offload the reporting workload. Option C is incorrect because using AWS DMS to continuously replicate to a separate database introduces a replication instance, ongoing operational overhead, and additional cost compared with a native read replica.

295.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL is wire-compatible with PostgreSQL, so migrating the existing database requires minimal application code change while relational queries continue to work. Directing the hours-long reporting queries to an Aurora Replica isolates that read load from the primary writer, so frequent document updates and new document additions proceed without being blocked by reporting.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon DocumentDB has MongoDB-compatible APIs, which would force a significant rewrite of the application's relational PostgreSQL queries. Option C is incorrect because an RDS Multi-AZ standby cannot be queried; it is solely a failover target, so the reporting module cannot read from it. Option D is incorrect because moving metadata to DynamoDB abandons relational queries entirely, demanding extensive application redesign, and its key-value model is poorly suited to the ad hoc relational reporting the company performs.

296.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Backup provides centralized, policy-driven backups with a backup vault for long-term retention. A backup plan with a daily schedule and a 2-year retention (expiration) rule produces consistent, fully restorable RDS snapshots, and assigning the DB instances to the plan automates the entire lifecycle, meeting the data retention policy with minimal effort.

Option B is incorrect because automated RDS snapshot retention is capped at 35 days, so it cannot satisfy a 2-year requirement, and Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager manages EBS snapshots and AMIs rather than RDS backups. Option C is incorrect because CloudWatch Logs stores transaction log text, not consistent point-in-time database backups, so the data would not be restorable as a database. Option D is incorrect because configuring AWS DMS with CDC to stream changes to S3 creates a complex replication pipeline that produces data files, not consistent restorable RDS backups, adding significant operational overhead.

297.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Security Hub is designed to continuously monitor and report on compliance posture against security standards. By designating a Security Hub delegated administrator in AWS Organizations and enabling it across all member accounts, the company gets an aggregated, organization-wide view, and enabling the NIST and PCI DSS standards runs automated controls and produces the evidence auditors need that controls are implemented and functioning.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Inspector scans for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure, not adherence to NIST or PCI DSS compliance frameworks. Option B is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat-detection service that analyzes for malicious activity and does not provide compliance standard scoring against NIST or PCI DSS. Option C is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail records API activity for auditing events; it does not evaluate resources against security standards or generate compliance control findings.

298.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Transfer Family is a fully managed service that provides SFTP endpoints backed directly by Amazon S3. Creating a Transfer Family SFTP-enabled endpoint lets the legacy vendors keep using their existing SFTP clients while files land natively in S3 alongside the API uploads, all with the least operational overhead because AWS manages the server, scaling, and availability.

Option A is incorrect because AWS DMS migrates and replicates databases, not file uploads over SFTP, so it does not address the vendors' protocol requirement. Option C is incorrect because running an SFTP server on a self-managed EC2 instance forces the company to patch, scale, secure, and maintain the server, which is the opposite of using a managed service. Option D is incorrect because Amazon S3 File Gateway presents an SMB or NFS file share, not SFTP, so it would not work with the vendors' existing SFTP-based applications.

299.text:
Option A is correct because S3 Event Notifications can place a message with image details onto an Amazon SQS queue the moment a photo is uploaded, and an AWS Lambda function can then process each image. Since each job runs only 2 minutes and needs 512 MB, Lambda is ideal and most cost-effective: you pay only per invocation and duration with no idle compute, the queue smooths bursts, and processing happens immediately on upload.

Option B is incorrect because an EC2 Reserved Instance must run continuously and is billed even when no images are arriving, wasting money for short, sporadic jobs. Option C is incorrect because SNS pushes to subscribers without durable buffering, and an always-running ECS container instance adds cost and management overhead for tiny tasks. Option D is incorrect because AWS Elastic Beanstalk provisions and runs persistent EC2 capacity, which is far more costly and operationally heavy than serverless Lambda for brief per-image processing.

300.text:
Option D is correct because the only way to keep the encryption keys entirely outside the AWS Cloud is to use client-side encryption: the company encrypts the data in its own data center using keys it manages locally, then uploads the already-encrypted objects to Amazon S3. Combined with TLS for the upload, this satisfies encryption both in transit and at rest while ensuring AWS never has access to the keys.

Options A, B, and C are incorrect because all three rely on S3 server-side encryption, where AWS performs the encryption and the keys are held within AWS Key Management Service or managed by S3. A KMS customer managed key, an AWS managed key, and the default SSE all keep key material inside the AWS Cloud, directly violating the requirement to manage the encryption keys externally. Only client-side encryption performed before upload keeps key control fully outside AWS.

301.text:
Option B is correct because configuring an AWS Glue crawler to catalog the clickstream data in the AWS Glue Data Catalog and then querying it with Amazon Athena is a fully serverless approach with the least operational overhead. The crawler automatically infers the schema, and Athena runs standard SQL directly against the data in S3 with no clusters to manage, letting the company quickly analyze the data and decide whether further processing is warranted.

Option A is incorrect because creating external tables in a Spark catalog and running Glue jobs requires authoring and managing ETL jobs, which is heavier than ad hoc Athena queries.
