Option C is incorrect because a Hive metastore with Spark jobs on Amazon EMR means provisioning and operating a cluster, adding substantial management overhead. Option D is incorrect because Kinesis Data Analytics is designed to query streaming data, not data already at rest in S3, making it an awkward fit for this batch analysis.

302.text:
Option D is correct because the workloads are explicitly described as long-running, and the deepest discounts come from the longest, most-committed pricing. Purchasing 3-year All Upfront RDS Reserved Instances and a 3-year All Upfront EC2 Instance Savings Plan provides the maximum cost reduction over on-demand pricing, which makes this the most cost-effective choice for stable, long-term capacity.

Options A, B, and C are incorrect because they all commit for shorter terms or with less upfront payment, yielding smaller discounts. Option A uses on-demand RDS pricing and only a 1-year No Upfront Compute Savings Plan, the weakest savings. Options B and C use 1-year terms (No Upfront and Partial Upfront), which discount less than a 3-year All Upfront commitment. For workloads known to run for years, the longer term and full upfront payment in Option D consistently deliver the lowest effective cost.

303.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon EFS provides a fully managed, high-performance shared file system that can be mounted concurrently by many EC2 instances across multiple Availability Zones within the VPC. It delivers low-latency, POSIX-compliant shared access for the media store and keeps the data inside the VPC, exactly matching the requirement for performant data sharing among all instances.

Option A is incorrect because, although Amazon S3 stores data, accessing it through service APIs requires application changes and is object storage over the public service endpoint rather than a shared file system. Option B is incorrect because S3 cannot be natively mounted as a standard volume across instances for concurrent read/write file access. Option C is incorrect because an Amazon EBS volume (outside of specific Multi-Attach configurations) attaches to a single instance and cannot be shared across instances in multiple Availability Zones, so it does not meet the concurrent multi-AZ sharing requirement.

304.text:
Option C is correct because AWS IAM Access Analyzer includes policy and access analysis capabilities, and its unused-access and last-accessed findings help identify IAM users that have more permissions than they actually use. It evaluates resources and access across the organization with minimal setup, providing the least-administrative-overhead way to spot over-permissioned identities.

Option A is incorrect because Network Access Analyzer evaluates network reachability and pathways in a VPC, not IAM identity permissions. Option B is incorrect because a CloudWatch alarm on resource creation or modification only reacts to activity after the fact and does not review or compare the permissions granted to IAM users. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Inspector scans for software vulnerabilities and network exposure on workloads; it does not analyze IAM policies to find excessive permissions.

305.text:
Options A and D are correct because AWS Global Accelerator improves availability and performance for both TCP and UDP workloads by routing traffic over the AWS global network to the optimal endpoint and providing static anycast IP addresses with fast failover. Pairing it with Network Load Balancers in each Region is ideal: NLBs operate at Layer 4 and support both the stateful TCP EC2 workload and the UDP traffic that fronts the on-premises stateless workload, and Global Accelerator can register those NLBs as endpoints.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is optimized for caching HTTP/HTTPS content and does not accelerate arbitrary TCP and UDP workloads like this hybrid application. Options C and E are incorrect because they rely on Application Load Balancers, which operate at Layer 7 and support only HTTP/HTTPS, so they cannot handle the UDP-based workload or the raw stateful TCP traffic that requires Layer 4 Network Load Balancers.

306.text:
Option A is correct because the job is stateful, cannot tolerate interruptions, and needs at least an hour to run on a schedule. Running it as an AWS Fargate task on Amazon ECS provides dedicated, serverless compute that runs to completion without the interruption risk of Spot, and Amazon EventBridge Scheduler triggers it reliably each week before the work week begins, all with no servers to manage.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Lambda has a maximum execution timeout of 15 minutes, so it cannot run a job that requires at least an hour. Option C is incorrect because EC2 Spot Instances can be reclaimed by AWS with little notice, which would interrupt a job that explicitly cannot tolerate interruptions. Option D is incorrect because AWS DataSync is a data transfer service for moving files between storage systems, not a platform for running a custom stateful analysis job.

307.text:
Option A is correct because it combines centralized federated console access with secure, keyless operating system access. AWS IAM Identity Center grants the external engineers controlled Management Console access without local IAM users, and AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides shell access to the Amazon Linux instances in private subnets through the SSM Agent and an instance profile. This eliminates bastion hosts, open SSH ports, and shared keys, and all sessions are authenticated, authorized, and logged.

Option B is incorrect because it provisions local IAM user credentials in each account, which is less secure and harder to manage than federated access. Option C is incorrect because it exposes instances to inbound SSH from external IP ranges and distributes SSH key pairs, expanding the attack surface. Option D is incorrect because a public bastion host with open SSH and distributed key pairs plus local IAM users is the least secure design and adds operational overhead compared with Session Manager.

308.text:
Option D is correct because when an on-premises identity store is not SAML 2.0 compatible, the supported pattern is to build a custom identity broker that authenticates users against the LDAP directory and then calls AWS STS to obtain short-lived, temporary credentials. The broker constructs a sign-in URL with those temporary credentials, allowing authenticated LDAP users to access the AWS Management Console securely without long-term IAM credentials.

Option A is incorrect because IAM Identity Center federation typically relies on SAML-based identity sources, and the directory is explicitly stated to be non-SAML-compatible. Option B is incorrect because you cannot embed AWS credentials into an IAM policy or push a policy into LDAP; policies define permissions, not authentication. Option C is incorrect because synchronizing or rotating static IAM credentials to mirror LDAP credentials is insecure, operationally fragile, and is not a supported federation mechanism for console access.

309.text:
Option D is correct because a Direct Connect gateway is a globally available resource that lets a single Direct Connect connection reach VPCs in multiple AWS Regions. By associating the existing Direct Connect connections with a Direct Connect gateway and attaching the virtual private gateways of the VPCs in both us-east-1 and eu-west-2, the company gains scalable cross-Region connectivity between the data centers and all VPCs while minimizing operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because inter-Region VPC peering connects VPCs to each other but does not provide connectivity between the VPCs and the on-premises data centers over Direct Connect. Option B is incorrect because private virtual interfaces are associated with a single Region's VPC through a virtual private gateway and cannot natively span Regions without a Direct Connect gateway. Option C is incorrect because building a fully meshed VPN over self-managed EC2 instances and VPN CloudHub introduces significant management overhead and does not scale cleanly.

310.text:
Options A and D are correct because migrating from RDS for MySQL to Aurora PostgreSQL is a heterogeneous migration that requires converting the schema and continuously replicating ongoing changes. AWS DMS Schema Conversion transforms the source MySQL database objects into PostgreSQL-compatible objects, and an AWS DMS task configured with change data capture (CDC) replicates the data changes that occur during the migration so the new Aurora PostgreSQL database stays in sync until cutover.

Option B is incorrect because DMS Schema Conversion converts schema objects; it does not create a cross-engine read replica on the RDS for MySQL instance. Option C is incorrect because an Aurora MySQL read replica keeps data in the MySQL engine and cannot serve as a path to a PostgreSQL target. Option E is incorrect because you cannot promote a nonexistent Aurora PostgreSQL read replica from a MySQL source; cross-engine replication here must be handled by a DMS CDC task rather than native replica promotion.

311.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon EFS is a fully managed, elastic file system that supports a hierarchical directory structure and allows multiple EC2 instances across Availability Zones to mount it and read and write concurrently with low latency. This shared POSIX file system directly matches the requirement for rapid, concurrent access to common storage from instances in two Availability Zones.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 is flat object storage accessed via APIs rather than a hierarchical file system, so it does not natively provide concurrent file read/write semantics for the applications. Option C is incorrect because an io2 EBS volume normally attaches to a single instance, so it cannot be shared concurrently across instances in different Availability Zones in the standard configuration. Option D is incorrect because per-instance EBS volumes with manual synchronization add complexity, risk data inconsistency, and do not deliver true concurrent shared access.

312.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon AppFlow is a fully managed integration service with a native Salesforce connector built specifically to transfer data between SaaS applications and AWS services like Amazon S3. AppFlow encrypts data in transit and lets you specify an AWS KMS customer managed key (CMK) to encrypt the data at rest in S3, meeting both encryption requirements with minimal custom development.

Option A is incorrect because building Lambda functions to integrate with the Salesforce API requires writing and maintaining custom code for authentication, pagination, and encryption, which is far more effort than the managed AppFlow connector. Option B is incorrect because AWS Step Functions orchestrates workflows but does not itself provide a managed Salesforce-to-S3 data transfer connector, so it would still rely on custom integration code. Option D is incorrect because building a custom Salesforce connector is unnecessary reinvention when AppFlow already provides a secure, managed Salesforce integration.

313.text:
Option D is correct because images are accessed only once or twice a year, which is exactly the infrequent-access pattern that S3 Standard-Infrequent Access targets. S3 Standard-IA offers the same high durability and millisecond access as S3 Standard but at a lower storage cost for rarely retrieved objects, and serving the images directly from S3 as a static website avoids running any servers, making it the most cost-effective highly available solution.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon EBS plus an EC2 web server requires running and paying for instances and provides storage tied to a single Availability Zone, which is neither the most cost-effective nor inherently highly available. Option B is incorrect because Amazon EFS with an EC2 web server is more expensive than S3 for this access pattern and still requires managing compute. Option C is incorrect because S3 Standard is priced for frequently accessed data, so for images retrieved only once or twice a year it costs more than S3 Standard-IA while offering no benefit.

314.text:
Option D is correct because a service control policy (SCP) in AWS Organizations is the only mechanism that can enforce a guardrail uniformly across multiple member accounts from a central management account. An SCP that denies the RunInstances action for large instance types sets the maximum available permissions, so even account administrators and root users cannot launch the prohibited instances. This delivers the requirement with the least operational overhead because it is configured once at the organization level rather than per account.

Option A is incorrect because IAM policies must be created and maintained separately in every account and attached to every user or group, which scales poorly and is easy to bypass if a policy is missed. Option B is incorrect because AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) shares resources such as subnets and transit gateways across accounts; it does not restrict what instance types can be launched. Option C is incorrect because IAM roles still must be created in each account individually and do not centrally constrain permissions; it also adds the operational burden of managing role assumption rather than preventing launches outright.

315.text:
Option A is correct because converting an existing Amazon RDS for MySQL DB instance to a Multi-AZ deployment is a built-in, single configuration change that RDS performs automatically. RDS provisions a synchronous standby replica in a second Availability Zone and handles failover automatically, removing the single point of failure with minimal effort and no application changes. Applying it during a maintenance window avoids disruption.

Option B is incorrect because migrating to DynamoDB is a heterogeneous database migration requiring data model redesign and AWS DMS work, which is far more implementation effort and changes the database engine entirely. Option C is incorrect because manually creating a new instance and restoring from a snapshot is labor-intensive, risks data loss for transactions after the snapshot, and requires repointing the application, far more effort than a simple Multi-AZ modification. Option D is incorrect because RDS is a managed service that cannot be placed in an EC2 Auto Scaling group, and using Route 53 simple routing across DB instances does not provide synchronous replication or automatic failover for a relational database.

316.text:
Option D is correct because Session Manager natively streams session logs to CloudWatch Logs, and a CloudWatch Logs subscription filter can continuously push incoming log events to an Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream that delivers them to Amazon S3. This pipeline is fully managed, automatic, and near real-time, providing the most operationally efficient way to archive session data to S3 with no manual export steps.

Option A is incorrect because while Session Manager can write directly to S3, this distractor describes an oversimplified setting and the question's intended efficient archival pattern relies on the streaming pipeline; direct S3 logging lacks the continuous, automated delivery the scenario emphasizes. Option B is incorrect because exporting logs from a CloudWatch log group to S3 is a manual, batch-oriented operation that must be repeatedly initiated, adding operational overhead. Option C is incorrect because building a custom Systems Manager document scheduled by EventBridge to scrape server logs is a bespoke, high-maintenance solution that does not directly capture Session Manager session data.

317.text:
Option D is correct because an AWS Lambda function invoked on a schedule by Amazon EventBridge is a serverless approach that starts and stops EC2 instances and RDS DB instances outside business hours with no servers to maintain. You pay only for the brief invocations, minimizing cost, and EventBridge cron rules provide reliable scheduling, so this meets the requirement to minimize both cost and infrastructure maintenance.

Option A is incorrect because elastic resize changes instance size rather than stopping instances to save cost, and RDS instances cannot be scaled to zero. Option B is incorrect because purchasing a Marketplace partner solution typically adds licensing cost and another component to manage, which is less efficient than a native serverless function. Option C is incorrect because running a dedicated EC2 instance with crontab introduces an always-on instance that must be patched, monitored, and paid for continuously, directly contradicting the goals of minimizing cost and infrastructure maintenance.

318.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon SageMaker is the purpose-built AWS service for end-to-end ML model development, training, and deployment. Amazon QuickSight natively integrates with AWS services to visualize data and build business intelligence dashboards, providing the least operational overhead for this workflow.

Option A is a common distractor because while AWS Glue has ML transforms for data deduplication, it is not a general-purpose tool for building and training custom ML models. Option C is incorrect because managing an AMI requires significant manual patching, scaling, and infrastructure maintenance. Option D is wrong because QuickSight is a visualization tool; while it supports calculated fields, it lacks the robust ML capabilities required to build and train complex models.

319.text:
Option C is correct because AWS PrivateLink allows the vendor to expose a service privately through a Network Load Balancer placed in front of the RDS for MySQL database, and the company accesses it through an interface VPC endpoint. This works entirely over the AWS private network without requiring an internet gateway, Direct Connect, or Site-to-Site VPN, and it avoids exposing the database publicly or peering the VPCs.

Option A is incorrect because Direct Connect provides dedicated connectivity from on-premises to AWS and is unrelated to private cross-account access, and VPC peering alone would still require route and CIDR coordination plus broad network exposure. Option B is incorrect because Client VPN connects remote user devices to a VPC, not two VPCs to each other, so it does not satisfy the requirement. Option D is incorrect because Transit Gateway and VPC peering both establish broad network-level connectivity and require non-overlapping CIDR ranges and route management, which is heavier and less secure than the targeted PrivateLink endpoint that exposes only the single database service.

320.text:
Option D is correct because the scenario optimizes for the LOWEST latency on uploads while 90% of content is consumed in the same Region where it is uploaded. Storing content in an Amazon S3 bucket in the Region closest to the user puts the write endpoint physically near the uploader, minimizing upload latency, and using multiple CloudFront distributions efficiently serves the region-local reads. This regional placement directly addresses the short-lived, locally-consumed content pattern.

Option A is incorrect because CloudFront accelerates downloads from edge caches and is not designed to speed up origin uploads. Option B is incorrect because S3 Transfer Acceleration optimizes long-distance uploads to a single bucket Region over the CloudFront edge network; when content is consumed locally, routing uploads to a distant bucket adds latency rather than reducing it. Option C is incorrect because uploading to EC2 instances and then copying to S3 introduces extra hops, server management overhead, and additional latency compared with writing directly to a nearby S3 bucket.

321.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Transfer Family provides a fully managed SFTP service that preserves the existing SFTP protocol and integrates with AWS Directory Service. Using AD Connector, the on-premises Microsoft Active Directory can authenticate Transfer Family users directly, so employees keep their existing credentials and file access mechanisms while data lands in Amazon S3, all with minimal operational overhead because AWS manages the SFTP server.

Option A is incorrect because S3 File Gateway exposes SMB or NFS shares, not SFTP, changing the protocol and client behavior the company wants to keep. Option B is incorrect because running an SFTP solution on an EC2 Auto Scaling group means self-managing servers, patching, scaling, and AD integration, which is significant operational overhead. Option D is incorrect because it references the AWS Directory Service identity provider but omits the AD Connector needed to federate the existing on-premises Active Directory; without AD Connector, the on-premises directory is not connected, so it does not fully satisfy keeping the current authentication mechanism.

322.text:
Option C is correct because a single Amazon SNS topic with message filtering can fan out each quote request to the appropriate Amazon SQS queue based on the quote type attribute, and each backend application server polls its own queue. SQS persistently stores messages until processed and supports the 24-hour response window, ensuring messages are separated by type and not lost. This combination is fully managed and maximizes operational efficiency with minimal maintenance.

Option A is incorrect because Kinesis data streams require managing shards and the Kinesis Client Library, adding maintenance complexity, and are designed for streaming analytics rather than durable per-type work queues. Option B is incorrect because routing each quote through a Lambda function and SNS topic does not provide a durable buffer; if processing fails, messages can be lost, undermining the must-not-get-lost requirement. Option D is incorrect because Kinesis Data Firehose into OpenSearch is built for search and analytics indexing, not reliable task queuing, and adds unnecessary components and cost.

323.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EFS provides a fully managed, elastic, shared file system that can be mounted concurrently by many EC2 instances across multiple Availability Zones using NFS. It handles large files easily and eliminates the need to transfer files between instances, directly resolving the availability issues caused by employees accessing large files. EFS scales automatically and provides POSIX file semantics the application already expects.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 is object storage accessed through APIs, not a mounted file system, so it would require application changes and does not provide standard file access. Option B is incorrect because an EBS volume created from a snapshot can be attached to only one instance at a time (standard volumes), so it cannot be shared across many instances and Availability Zones. Option D is incorrect because instance store volumes are ephemeral, local to a single instance, and lost when the instance stops, providing neither shared access nor durability across Availability Zones.

324.text:
Option B is correct because S3 Standard-Infrequent Access (S3 Standard-IA) stores data across multiple Availability Zones, providing the same high durability, availability, and resiliency as S3 Standard while charging a lower storage price for infrequently accessed data. Using a lifecycle rule to transition objects after 30 days minimizes storage costs while keeping all data immediately accessible, exactly matching the requirements.

Option A is incorrect because S3 Glacier Deep Archive requires retrieval times of hours, so data would not remain immediately accessible. Option C is incorrect because S3 One Zone-IA stores data in a single Availability Zone, which reduces resiliency and availability and therefore does not preserve the same high availability and resiliency the company requires. Option D is incorrect for the same single-AZ resiliency reason as Option C, and moving data immediately ignores the observed pattern that objects are still accessed regularly during the first 30 days.

325.text:
Options A and B are correct because AWS Fargate provides a serverless launch type to run the cloud application containers without managing EC2 infrastructure, while Amazon ECS Anywhere uses the external launch type to register and run containers on the company's on-premises hardware, giving one consistent ECS control plane across on-premises, hybrid, and cloud environments. An Application Load Balancer is the appropriate choice to distribute HTTP traffic to the cloud ECS services because it operates at Layer 7 and supports content-based routing for web applications.

Option C is incorrect because a Network Load Balancer operates at Layer 4 and is intended for TCP/UDP performance, not the HTTP traffic the scenario specifies. Option D is incorrect because Fargate cannot run on-premises containers; the on-premises tier must use the ECS Anywhere external launch type. Option E is incorrect because ECS Anywhere uses the external launch type, not a Fargate launch type, on premises, so pairing ECS Anywhere with Fargate is not a valid configuration.

326.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Security Lake is a purpose-built service that automatically centralizes security data from AWS services, on-premises sources, and third-party providers into a customer-owned S3 data lake, normalizing it into the Open Cybersecurity Schema Framework (OCSF). Because it handles collection, normalization, and storage natively, it lets the company assess security across the entire organization and improve workload protection with the least development effort.

Option A is incorrect because building a data lake with Lake Formation and custom Glue crawlers requires designing ingestion, schemas, and transformation logic, which is significant development effort. Option B is incorrect because writing a custom Lambda function to collect and upload CSV data is a bespoke pipeline that must be built and maintained, and CSV lacks the normalized security schema needed for cross-source analysis. Option D is incorrect because AWS DMS loads database records between relational stores and is not designed to aggregate diverse security telemetry, making it an inappropriate and high-effort choice.

327.text:
Option B is correct because an Aurora global database asynchronously replicates from the primary Region (us-east-1) to a secondary Region (us-west-1) with typical replication lag under one second, easily meeting the 5-minute RPO. Managed planned failover and the ability to promote the secondary cluster meet the 20-minute RTO, and converting the existing cluster to a global database minimizes configuration changes, making it the most operationally efficient solution.

Option A is incorrect because a cross-Region Aurora read replica provides DR but requires more manual promotion steps and lacks the integrated managed failover orchestration of a global database, reducing operational efficiency. Option C is incorrect because creating a new cluster with generic cross-Region replication adds setup and maintenance compared with simply converting to the purpose-built global database feature. Option D is incorrect because using AWS DMS to continuously sync two clusters introduces a replication instance to manage and monitor, increasing operational overhead and risking higher lag than Aurora's native storage-level replication.

328.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server provides a fully managed native Windows file system that supports the SMB protocol out of the box, integrates with Active Directory, and handles backups, patching, and high availability. SMB clients can connect directly, so it meets the shared storage requirement with the least administrative overhead.

Option A is incorrect because a Storage Gateway Volume Gateway presents iSCSI block volumes, not SMB file shares, so it does not support SMB client access as required. Option B is incorrect because a Tape Gateway provides a virtual tape library for backup and archival to S3 and Glacier, which is unrelated to providing live SMB file access. Option C is incorrect because building a Windows file server on an EC2 instance requires the company to manage the operating system, file share role, patching, scaling, and availability, which is considerably more administrative overhead than the managed FSx for Windows File Server service.

329.text:
Option A is correct because an Auto Scaling group spanning two Availability Zones increases availability and automatically replaces unhealthy instances, an Application Load Balancer distributes traffic and serves as the Auto Scaling group's target, and AWS WAF attaches directly to the ALB to inspect and filter web traffic. AWS WAF integrates natively with the ALB, so this architecture both raises availability and incorporates WAF correctly.

Option B is incorrect because a cluster placement group packs instances into a single Availability Zone for low-latency networking, which reduces availability, and WAF cannot be attached to a placement group. Option C is incorrect because using two fixed EC2 instances without an Auto Scaling group does not automatically recover or scale capacity, providing lower availability than an Auto Scaling group. Option D is incorrect because AWS WAF must be associated with a supported resource such as an ALB, CloudFront distribution, or API Gateway; it cannot be connected to an Auto Scaling group.

330.text:
Option B is correct because migrating to a Multi-AZ Amazon RDS for SQL Server DB instance offloads patching, backups, replication, and failover to AWS, reducing operational overhead, while a synchronous standby in a second Availability Zone increases availability. Encrypting the database with an AWS KMS managed key secures the sensitive data at rest, satisfying both the security and reduced-overhead requirements for the relational SQL Server workload.

Option A is incorrect because running SQL Server on EC2 instances keeps the company responsible for OS and database administration, patching, and high-availability configuration, which does not reduce operational overhead. Option C is incorrect because moving relational data to an S3 bucket discards the relational database engine entirely and Macie only discovers and classifies sensitive data rather than running the workload. Option D is incorrect because DynamoDB is a NoSQL key-value store unsuited to a relational SQL Server schema, and CloudWatch Logs provides monitoring, not data security or encryption.

331.text:
Option C is correct because CloudFront caches the static website content at edge locations, so even after a successful CI/CD deployment updates the objects in Amazon S3, edge caches continue serving the previous versions until they expire. Invalidating the CloudFront cache forces CloudFront to fetch the latest objects from the S3 origin, immediately reflecting the updated content on the website.

Option A is incorrect because adding an Application Load Balancer addresses traffic distribution to compute targets and has nothing to do with stale cached static content served from CloudFront and S3. Option B is incorrect because ElastiCache caches database query results to improve backend performance, but the problem is stale static front-end content in the CDN, not database read latency. Option D is incorrect because validating an SSL certificate with AWS Certificate Manager concerns HTTPS encryption and trust, not whether updated content is being served, so it would not resolve the stale-content issue.

332.text:
Option D is correct because EBS Snapshot Lock applies an immutability control directly to snapshots, preventing them from being deleted for a defined retention period even by users with delete permissions, including the storage administrator. Because the lock operates on the snapshot resource itself rather than on the user's IAM permissions, it protects against accidental deletion with the least administrative effort and without changing the administrator's existing rights.

Option A is incorrect because routing deletions through a separate EC2 instance and IAM role adds infrastructure and process complexity and does not actually prevent accidental deletion. Option B is incorrect because attaching an IAM deny policy to the storage administrator changes that user's administrative rights, which the requirement explicitly forbids. Option C is incorrect because Recycle Bin retention rules only recover snapshots after they have been deleted within a retention window rather than preventing deletion outright, and it requires ongoing tag management, so it is less direct than locking the snapshots.

333.text:
Option B is correct because the AWS Step Functions Map state in Distributed mode is purpose-built for large-scale parallel processing of data stored in Amazon S3, supporting up to 10,000 concurrent child workflow executions and iterating over millions of items. It is serverless and orchestrates the parallel processing of thousands of semistructured items with the most operational efficiency and no infrastructure to manage.

Option A is incorrect because the Map state in Inline mode is limited to 40 concurrent iterations and holds state in memory, so it cannot scale to the thousands of parallel items required. Option C is incorrect because AWS Glue is an ETL service oriented toward batch data transformation rather than on-demand, fan-out parallel processing of individual items, and it is less efficient for this orchestration pattern. Option D is incorrect because manually coordinating many separate Lambda functions requires custom code to handle batching, concurrency limits, error handling, and aggregation, which is far less operationally efficient than the managed Distributed Map orchestration.

334.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon ElastiCache for Redis places an in-memory cache in front of the RDS database, delivering sub-millisecond response times for the frequently read game metadata and relieving the database load that scaling alone could not fix. Redis uniquely supports the required capabilities of snapshots (backups) and replication (read replicas and Multi-AZ), matching all three stated requirements.

Option A is incorrect because migrating to Aurora with replicas improves database scaling but does not provide the in-memory sub-millisecond latency, and the metrics already show that scaling the database will not help. Option B is incorrect because moving to DynamoDB with global tables is a major data model change and, while fast, is not framed around the caching solution with snapshot and replication features the question targets. Option D is incorrect because ElastiCache for Memcached does not support snapshots or replication; it is a simpler, non-persistent cache, so it fails the explicit snapshot and replication requirements that Redis satisfies.

335.text:
Option B is correct because an AWS Storage Gateway file gateway presents a local file interface backed by Amazon S3, caching the most recently and frequently accessed data on premises for low-latency access while storing the full dataset durably in S3. This lets researchers immediately access the working subset with minimal lag, keeps the entire dataset in cost-effective cloud storage, and reduces ongoing capital expenditure on storage arrays.

Option A is incorrect because AWS DataSync as a scheduled job only migrates copies of data to S3 and does not provide an ongoing local file interface with a hot cache for immediate low-lag access. Option C is incorrect because a volume gateway exposes iSCSI block volumes rather than a file interface, which does not match the researchers' need to browse and access files and adds block management complexity. Option D is incorrect because keeping all data on an EFS file system over a Site-to-Site VPN does not reduce capital expense as effectively and introduces VPN latency and ongoing networking management.

336.text:
Option B is correct because AWS CloudFormation provides automation by defining infrastructure as code in templates, enabling consistent, repeatable provisioning of the systems and network infrastructure. AWS Config continuously records configuration changes and maintains a history of every incremental change to resources, giving the security team the ability to track and audit changes over time, satisfying both the automation and auditing requirements.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Organizations governs multi-account structure and policies but does not itself automate infrastructure provisioning the way CloudFormation does. Option C is incorrect because, beyond the Organizations limitation, AWS Service Catalog is used to curate and distribute approved products to users rather than to track and audit incremental configuration changes. Option D is incorrect because while CloudFormation correctly automates the infrastructure, Service Catalog does not provide the continuous change tracking and configuration history that AWS Config delivers for auditing.

337.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Firewall Manager lets you centrally define and enforce a common security group policy across all accounts in an AWS Organization. By creating primary security groups in a central administrative account and applying a Firewall Manager common security group policy, the company manages CIDR rule updates in one place and automatically propagates them organization-wide, minimizing administrative overhead cost-effectively.

Option A is incorrect because security groups are scoped to a single VPC and account, so groups created only in the management account cannot be referenced by resources in other accounts, and manual per-account updates do not scale. Option B is incorrect because a customer managed prefix list shared via AWS RAM helps manage CIDR lists but still requires referencing the prefix list in every security group across the organization and does not centrally enforce the security group rules themselves. Option C is incorrect because AWS managed prefix lists are maintained by AWS and cannot be edited, and Security Hub does not enforce security group rule changes, making the described Lambda-based update approach invalid and complex.

338.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Glue DataBrew is a visual, no-code data preparation service that lets users build transformation steps such as filtering anomalies, normalizing dates and times, and generating aggregates without writing code. DataBrew automatically provides data profiling and data lineage, and transformation logic is captured as reusable recipes that can be shared across the company, meeting every stated requirement.

Option A is incorrect because, although AWS Glue Studio offers a visual canvas, it generates code-based Glue jobs and does not provide the built-in no-code data profiling and lineage-focused recipe sharing that DataBrew delivers. Option B is incorrect because EMR Serverless requires writing Spark code, which violates the no-code requirement and lacks integrated profiling and lineage. Option D is incorrect because creating Athena tables and writing SQL queries is a code-based approach that does not provide the prebuilt no-code transformation experience, data profiling, or lineage the company needs.

339.text:
Option B is correct because ALB access logs capture detailed information about every request the load balancer receives, including client IP addresses, request paths, and timestamps that reveal abnormal traffic patterns. Delivering those logs to Amazon S3 and querying them with Amazon Athena provides a serverless, SQL-based analysis layer with no infrastructure to manage, making it the most operationally efficient way to gain visibility into request-level abnormalities.

Option A is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail records management and API activity in the account, not the request-level traffic flowing through the ALB, so it cannot reveal client access patterns. Option C is operationally inefficient and impractical at scale; manually opening and reading individual log files in a text editor is slow, error-prone, and does not support aggregation or filtering. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EMR on a dedicated EC2 instance introduces significant management overhead for cluster provisioning and scaling, and an ALB cannot be queried directly for traffic logs; logs must be exported to S3 first.

340.text:
Option B is correct because creating a read replica offloads the reporting workload from the primary database to a separate, asynchronously replicated copy. Pointing the script at the read replica isolates the read-heavy reporting queries so they no longer contend with the critical application's transactions, improving application performance with minimal cost and almost no operational overhead since RDS manages replication automatically.

Option A is incorrect because a Multi-AZ deployment does not provide additional readable endpoints; the standby instance is not available for reads, and adding connection-counting logic increases code complexity without solving the contention. Option C is incorrect because manual daily exports introduce operational burden, human error, and stale data, and do not address the performance impact during the day. Option D is incorrect because ElastiCache adds cost and complexity for caching, and reporting queries that return new entries are not well suited to caching since the results constantly change.

341.text:
Option B is correct because AWS DataSync is purpose-built to move data between on-premises NFS and SMB storage and AWS services such as Amazon S3. A lightweight DataSync agent deployed on the on-premises servers handles scheduling, data integrity checks, and incremental syncs, making it the most cost-effective and operationally simple choice for periodic backups of small amounts of data.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Glue is a serverless ETL service for data cataloging and transformation, not a tool for backing up files from on-premises NFS servers to S3. Option C is incorrect because AWS Transfer for SFTP incurs a higher hourly endpoint cost and is designed for SFTP-based file exchange workflows rather than simple periodic backups. Option D is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect requires provisioning a dedicated network connection, which is expensive and over-engineered for periodically backing up small amounts of data.

342.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Shield Advanced provides enhanced DDoS protection for resources such as an ALB, including detailed attack diagnostics and visibility that serve as an audit trail of attack sources. It also grants access to the AWS DDoS Response Team (DRT), who can help build and integrate mitigating controls, all with minimal configuration changes to the existing architecture.

Option A is incorrect because while AWS WAF can block traffic, a basic web ACL alone does not provide the dedicated DDoS audit trail or DRT engagement, and crafting rules for random IP sources is difficult. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Inspector assesses workloads for software vulnerabilities and is unrelated to DDoS mitigation or the DRT. Option D is incorrect because it requires significant architectural change by introducing a CloudFront distribution and reconfiguring the origin, which exceeds the requirement for minimal configuration changes.

343.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Lake Formation natively supports fine-grained access control through data filters, enabling row-level and cell-level security on tables. This lets administrators restrict access to portions of data containing sensitive information declaratively, without modifying the underlying data, providing a secure solution with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because granting an IAM role access to Lake Formation tables controls table-level access but cannot prevent access to specific sensitive rows or cells within a table. Option C is incorrect because writing a Lambda function to strip sensitive data before ingestion permanently destroys information, adds custom code to maintain, and removes data that may still be needed for some users. Option D is incorrect because periodically querying and deleting sensitive data with Lambda is operationally heavy, introduces a window where sensitive data is exposed, and irreversibly removes data rather than controlling access to it.

344.text:
Option B is correct because the high latency at the start of each day is caused by Lambda cold starts when concurrency must scale up from idle. Configuring provisioned concurrency keeps a set of execution environments initialized and ready to respond instantly, and using scheduled scaling to raise provisioned concurrency before employees log in directly eliminates the cold-start latency during the morning ramp-up.

Option A is incorrect because increasing the API Gateway throttling limit affects request rate limits, not initialization latency, so it does not address cold starts. Option C is incorrect because invoking a Lambda function from a CloudWatch alarm does not pre-warm the concurrency needed for the actual workload and is an unreliable warming hack. Option D is incorrect because increasing memory speeds execution somewhat but does not eliminate the cold-start initialization delay that causes the morning latency spikes.

345.text:
Option D is correct because an On-Demand Capacity Reservation reserves EC2 compute capacity in a specific Availability Zone within the failover Region, guaranteeing that the instances will be available when a disaster recovery failover occurs. This directly satisfies the business requirement that the DR strategy must ensure capacity is available in the failover Region.

Option A is incorrect because On-Demand Instances provide no capacity guarantee; during a large-scale event capacity may be unavailable exactly when failover is needed. Option B is incorrect because an EC2 Savings Plan is a billing commitment that provides discounted pricing but does not reserve or guarantee capacity. Option C is incorrect because regional Reserved Instances provide a billing discount and, unlike zonal RIs, do not reserve capacity in a specific Availability Zone, so they cannot guarantee capacity availability during a failover.

346.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Aurora Serverless v2 is a managed, MySQL-compatible database that automatically scales capacity up and down in fine-grained increments to match inconsistent workloads. Setting a minimum capacity ensures the database can satisfy the steady baseline memory requirement while auto scaling handles unexpected spikes, all with the least administrative overhead since AWS manages provisioning and scaling.

Option A is incorrect because DynamoDB is a NoSQL database that is not MySQL-compatible, requiring a significant data model rewrite and migration. Option B is incorrect because a provisioned Aurora cluster does not automatically scale compute capacity in response to workload changes the way Aurora Serverless v2 does, so it would not meet the auto scaling requirement without manual intervention. Option D is incorrect because Amazon RDS for MySQL uses a fixed instance class and does not automatically scale compute capacity, meaning unexpected workload increases would require manual instance resizing.

347.text:
Option B is correct because configuring an S3 object-created event notification to invoke an AWS Lambda function provides a fully serverless, event-driven architecture that scales automatically. Lambda handles bursts from a few files per hour to hundreds of concurrent uploads, and because processing completes in under 5 seconds and you pay only for invocations, it is highly cost-effective with no idle infrastructure.

Option A is incorrect because CloudTrail logging of S3 API calls adds latency and is meant for auditing, and AWS AppSync is a managed GraphQL service, not a file-processing engine. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Kinesis Data Streams is designed for streaming record ingestion and adds unnecessary cost and complexity for discrete file-upload processing. Option D is incorrect because Amazon SNS is a pub/sub messaging service that does not process files; while it can fan out notifications, directly triggering Lambda from the S3 event is simpler and more cost-effective.

348.text:
Option A is correct because a Lambda function URL provides a dedicated, built-in HTTPS endpoint for a single function with no additional services to configure. It is the most operationally efficient way to expose the function for a third-party webhook callback, since the URL is generated automatically and supports synchronous invocation directly over HTTP.

Option B is incorrect because deploying an Application Load Balancer in front of Lambda adds infrastructure, cost, and configuration overhead that is unnecessary for a simple webhook endpoint. Option C is incorrect because Amazon SNS is a pub/sub messaging service that does not expose a public hostname webhooks can call, and it is not designed to receive arbitrary inbound HTTP callbacks. Option D is incorrect because Amazon SQS is a message queue accessed through the AWS API and SDKs, not a public HTTP endpoint that a third party can post webhook callbacks to.

349.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between access tiers based on changing access patterns, optimizing cost for unpredictable workloads where some photos are viewed heavily for months and others briefly. Storing the photo metadata and S3 object locations in DynamoDB provides fast, scalable lookups to determine which photos to display, combining cost-effective object storage with efficient metadata querying.

Option A is incorrect because storing up to 20 MB photos in DynamoDB exceeds the 400 KB item size limit and is far more expensive than S3 for blob storage. Option C is incorrect because a fixed 30-day lifecycle rule to S3 Standard-IA does not match the unpredictable access patterns and can incur retrieval charges and minimum storage durations for objects that become hot again. Option D is incorrect because S3 Glacier and Glacier Deep Archive impose retrieval delays unsuitable for photos that users view interactively.

350.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon S3 provides virtually unlimited, highly durable object storage capable of holding petabytes of engineering drawings, and Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network that caches content at edge locations close to users. Together they minimize load times by serving cached drawings from the nearest edge while S3 supplies scalable, cost-effective origin storage.

Option B is incorrect because S3 Glacier is an archival storage class with retrieval delays unsuitable for interactive viewing, and ElastiCache is an in-memory data store for database query results, not for caching large files to end users. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EBS volumes are attached to individual EC2 instances and do not scale to petabytes of shared web-accessible storage. Option D is incorrect because AWS Storage Gateway is designed to bridge on-premises environments to AWS storage, and ElastiCache does not cache large drawing files for distributed users the way CloudFront does.

351.text:
Option B is correct because VPC Flow Logs capture information about IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in the VPC and can be delivered to a CloudWatch Logs log group. Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose can subscribe to the log group and stream the data to Amazon OpenSearch Service in near real time, with Firehose handling buffering, transformation, and delivery as a fully managed service.

Option A is incorrect because Kinesis Data Streams requires you to build and manage consumer applications to deliver data to OpenSearch, whereas Firehose delivers natively with no custom code. Options C and D are incorrect because AWS CloudTrail records account API activity, not network interface traffic, and VPC Flow Logs cannot be sent to a CloudTrail trail; flow logs are delivered to CloudWatch Logs or S3, making the CloudTrail destination invalid.

352.text:
Option A is correct because generating an Amazon S3 Inventory report lets you identify and filter the existing unencrypted objects, and an S3 Batch Operations job can then re-encrypt that list of objects using server-side encryption with a customer-provided key (SSE-C), satisfying the customer-provided key requirement. Configuring S3 default encryption with SSE-C ensures all future objects are also encrypted with the customer-provided key.

Option B is incorrect because S3 Storage Lens provides usage and activity metrics, not a per-object encryption inventory, and SSE-KMS uses AWS-managed key infrastructure rather than a customer-provided key. Option C is incorrect because it relies on the AWS usage report (which lacks object-level encryption status) and AWS Batch, plus SSE-KMS does not meet the customer-provided key requirement. Option D is incorrect because it only sets default encryption for future objects and never re-encrypts the existing millions of unencrypted archival files.

353.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon Kinesis Data Streams ingests large, spiky volumes of streaming score updates and preserves the order of records within each shard, satisfying the requirement to process updates in order of receipt. Processing the stream with AWS Lambda is serverless and scales automatically, and storing results in Amazon DynamoDB provides a highly available, fully managed database, minimizing overall management overhead.

Option B is incorrect because using a self-managed fleet of Auto Scaling EC2 instances and Amazon Redshift adds operational burden, and Redshift is a data warehouse not ideal for high-velocity leaderboard writes. Option C is incorrect because Amazon SNS does not guarantee ordered delivery and a SQL database on EC2 is self-managed, conflicting with the availability and low-overhead goals. Option D is incorrect because standard SQS does not preserve message order and the EC2-based processing fleet introduces management overhead that the serverless approach avoids.

354.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon GuardDuty RDS Protection analyzes login activity to supported Aurora databases and uses machine learning to detect suspicious behavior, including anomalous and brute-force failed or incomplete login attempts. Enabling it for the organization's member accounts provides continuous, managed threat detection across all accounts in the most operationally efficient way, with no log parsing to build or maintain.

Option A is incorrect because service control policies set permission guardrails and cannot identify or analyze database login attempts. Option C is incorrect because publishing Aurora general logs to CloudWatch Logs and exporting to S3 requires building custom analysis and alerting to detect abnormal logins, adding significant operational overhead. Option D is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail records AWS API calls to the RDS service control plane, not the in-database authentication events such as failed application login attempts to the PostgreSQL engine.

355.text:
Option C is correct because the data resides on SMB-based file storage and the company does not want to change its applications, so the destination must present a native Windows SMB file share, which Amazon FSx for Windows File Server provides. AWS DataSync efficiently migrates the millions of small files in deep directory hierarchies while preserving the directory structure and metadata, delivering the migration with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because migrating to Amazon S3 turns files into objects, which would require changing applications to use object APIs instead of SMB file access. Option B is incorrect because Amazon FSx for Lustre is a high-performance file system for compute-intensive workloads using Linux clients, not SMB-based Windows file shares. Option D is incorrect because a Storage Gateway volume gateway presents block (iSCSI) storage rather than an SMB file share and would not provide the native file access the applications require without changes.

356.text:
Option A is correct because a Network Load Balancer operates at Layer 4 and supports assigning a static Elastic IP address to each Availability Zone. This gives clients a fixed, predictable IP address that they can authorize on their firewall allow lists, directly meeting the requirement that clients can only reach IP addresses permitted by their firewalls.

Option B is incorrect because an Application Load Balancer does not support associating Elastic IP addresses; its IP addresses are managed by AWS and can change. Option C is incorrect because pointing a Route 53 A record at an Elastic IP does not solve the problem, since an ALB or ELB does not have a stable Elastic IP to point to, and DNS does not provide a fixed load balancer IP. Option D is incorrect because running a self-managed EC2 proxy with a public IP adds a single point of failure, operational overhead, and scaling concerns compared to using a Network Load Balancer with Elastic IPs.

357.text:
Option C is correct because the AWS managed AdministratorAccess policy is an identity-based policy that grants full access to AWS services and resources. Attaching it to the IAM user group and placing the five designated employees' IAM users in that group efficiently grants all of them the required full administrative access through a single, centrally managed permission set.

Options A and D are incorrect because IAM user groups are identities, and you attach identity-based policies to them, not resource-based policies; resource-based policies are attached to resources such as S3 buckets, so these options describe an invalid configuration. Option B is incorrect because the SystemAdministrator policy grants a limited subset of administrative permissions and does not provide the full access to all AWS services and resources that the requirement specifies, whereas AdministratorAccess does.

358.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon RDS storage autoscaling automatically increases the allocated storage when the database approaches its capacity limit, without requiring downtime or manual intervention. Enabling this single setting meets the requirement to add disk space with the least effort while keeping the application available.

Option B is incorrect because increasing the DB instance size changes the compute class rather than directly solving the disk space problem and can involve a maintenance reboot. Option C is incorrect because changing the storage type to Provisioned IOPS targets I/O performance, not capacity, and a storage type conversion is unnecessary effort for simply adding space. Option D is incorrect because backing up, increasing capacity, restoring, and switching instances is a manual, multi-step process that causes downtime and is the most labor-intensive option, the opposite of least effort.

359.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Backup uses tag-based resource selection in backup plans, so every resource must be tagged to be included. Using AWS Config to identify untagged resources and then programmatically applying the required tags ensures all resources are captured by the tag-based backup plan, providing complete coverage with the least ongoing operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because identifying resources that are not running does not relate to backup eligibility, and you assign resources to backups through backup plans and tags, not by manually adding them to a vault. Option C is incorrect because relying on account owners to manually review and identify resources is error-prone, inconsistent, and operationally burdensome. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Inspector assesses workloads for security vulnerabilities and does not identify resources for backup or manage backup coverage.

360.text:
Option B is correct because the company must continue managing users and groups in its self-managed on-premises Active Directory. Enabling AWS IAM Identity Center and using AWS Directory Service for Microsoft Active Directory to establish a two-way forest trust with the self-managed Active Directory lets IAM Identity Center provide single sign-on across all Organizations accounts while the on-premises directory remains the authoritative source of identities.

Option A is incorrect because creating a new managed Active Directory as the identity source would require moving or duplicating user management into AWS, contradicting the requirement to keep managing users in the on-premises directory. Option C is incorrect because it omits IAM Identity Center, which is the service that delivers the centralized single sign-on across all the Organizations accounts. Option D is incorrect because deploying and operating a self-managed identity provider on Amazon EC2 adds significant management overhead and does not leverage the existing Active Directory through a trust relationship.

361.text:
Option A is correct because the Amazon SQS Extended Client Library for Java is purpose-built to handle messages larger than the 256 KB SQS limit by transparently storing the large payload in Amazon S3 and passing a reference through SQS. It integrates with the existing Java application with minimal code changes, enabling messages as large as the configured limit (well beyond 50 MB) while preserving the SQS workflow.

Option B is incorrect because switching to Amazon EventBridge would require substantial application rework and EventBridge has its own payload size limits, so it does not minimize code changes. Option C is incorrect because the 256 KB maximum message size in standard SQS is a hard service limit that cannot be raised by configuration. Option D is incorrect because storing payloads in Amazon EFS and referencing them requires custom integration code and infrastructure, far more change than the managed Extended Client Library approach.

362.text:
Option B is correct because storing the database credentials in AWS Secrets Manager and configuring the application to load them from Secrets Manager removes the embedded credentials with minimal code change. Secrets Manager supports automated rotation, and providing an AWS Lambda function to rotate the RDS for MySQL credentials enables secure, hands-off credential rotation, improving security with the least programming effort.

Option A is incorrect because AWS KMS manages encryption keys, not database username and password secrets, so it does not provide a mechanism to store and rotate RDS credentials. Option C is incorrect because Secrets Manager rotation is implemented through a rotation Lambda function rather than a rotation schedule configured on the RDS database itself, so the described mechanism is not how rotation works. Option D is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store does not provide built-in managed credential rotation for RDS, making it less suitable and requiring more custom effort than Secrets Manager.

363.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon ElastiCache for Redis provides an in-memory data store that delivers the lowest possible latency for both the relatively static catalog data and the volatile shopping cart and session data. Redis supports replication and Multi-AZ with automatic failover, making the cart data highly available, and because session state is stored in the shared cache rather than on an individual instance, a user's session persists if they disconnect and reconnect to a different instance.

Option A is incorrect because sticky sessions tie a user to a single instance, so session data is lost if that instance fails or the user reconnects elsewhere; it does not preserve cart data. Option C is incorrect because Amazon OpenSearch Service is a search and analytics engine, not a low-latency caching layer for session and cart state. Option D is incorrect because a single EC2 instance with EBS and snapshots provides neither high availability nor low-latency scalable access, and snapshots only protect against data loss after the fact rather than keeping live cart data continuously available.

364.text:
Option A is correct because DynamoDB point-in-time recovery (PITR) is a fully managed, single-setting feature that continuously backs up table data and lets you restore to any second within the last 35 days, easily covering the 24-hour requirement. Enabling PITR involves no scripts, schedules, or custom infrastructure, so it meets the requirement with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because while AWS Backup can protect DynamoDB tables, it provides scheduled snapshot-based recovery points rather than continuous any-second restore, so reverting to an arbitrary point within 24 hours would not be guaranteed and it adds backup plan configuration overhead. Option C is incorrect because a custom Lambda function running hourly requires writing, scheduling, and maintaining code and only provides hourly granularity, not any-point recovery. Option D is incorrect because DynamoDB Streams capture item-level changes for processing but do not provide a native restore mechanism; rebuilding the table from streamed records stored in S3 would require significant custom development and operational effort.

365.text:
Option A is correct because an S3 Object Lambda Access Point lets you attach an AWS Lambda function that transforms object data as it is retrieved, so the function can redact PII from each conversation file on the fly without creating or storing a second copy. The external provider accesses files through the Object Lambda Access Point and only ever sees redacted content, and because the redaction happens at retrieval time it scales automatically with S3 and the number of conversations, requiring minimal ongoing operations.

Option B is incorrect because building and operating a web application on an EC2 instance to list, redact, and serve files introduces significant operational overhead for patching, scaling, and availability, and it duplicates data by storing redacted versions. Option C is incorrect because pre-processing every new file into a DynamoDB table requires building and maintaining an ingestion pipeline and a custom data model, adds storage duplication, and is far more operationally complex than transforming objects on read with Object Lambda.

366.text:
Option C is correct because migrating the polling script to an AWS Lambda function makes the workload fully serverless and event-driven. Lambda integrates natively with Amazon SQS, automatically scaling out the number of concurrent executions as the queue depth grows and scaling to zero when there are no messages. The company pays only for actual execution time rather than for a continuously running EC2 instance, which reduces operational cost while transparently handling a growing message volume.

Option A is incorrect because a larger EC2 instance increases cost and still runs continuously regardless of queue activity, and a single instance does not scale elastically with message volume. Option B is incorrect because using EventBridge to stop an underutilized instance adds management complexity and risks leaving messages unprocessed while the instance is off, and it still relies on a manually managed server. Option D is incorrect because Systems Manager Run Command executes scripts on demand on managed instances; it does not provide continuous, scalable, automatic processing of a steadily growing queue.

367.text:
Option C is correct because an Amazon SQS access policy is a resource-based policy attached directly to the queue. It can grant a principal in another AWS account permission to perform actions such as ReceiveMessage on the queue. Because the permission is granted on the resource itself, the external company can poll the queue using its own existing IAM credentials in its own account, without having to assume a role or give up its own account permissions.

Option A is incorrect because an instance profile attaches a role to an EC2 instance within the development team's own account and does not grant cross-account access to the other company. Option B is incorrect because an identity-based IAM policy in the development team's account can only be attached to principals in that account, not to the external company's users. Option D is incorrect because an SNS access policy governs access to an SNS topic, which is a different service and does not control who can poll the SQS queue.

368.text:
Option B is correct because at 100 Mbps available only at night, transferring 150 TB over the network would take many months, far exceeding the one-month deadline, so a physical transfer is required. Multiple AWS Snowball devices let the company load 150 TB locally and ship them to AWS, which is the most cost-effective offline option for this data volume and easily meets the deadline regardless of the limited and time-restricted internet bandwidth.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Snowmobile is designed for exabyte-scale migrations (typically 10 PB or more) and is vastly oversized and not cost-effective for only 150 TB. Option C is incorrect because Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration still relies on the same constrained 100 Mbps night-only link and cannot overcome the fundamental bandwidth limit to meet the deadline. Option D is incorrect because an S3 VPC endpoint and a VPN still depend on the limited internet upload capacity, so the transfer would not complete within a month.

369.text:
Option A is correct because creating an IAM role in the company's account and configuring its trust policy to allow the vendor's IAM role to assume it is the AWS best practice for cross-account access. The vendor's automated tool assumes the role and receives temporary security credentials, so no long-lived access keys are created or shared. This delegated, temporary-credential model is the most secure approach and lets the company attach exactly the permissions the vendor requires.

Option B is incorrect because creating an IAM user with a long-lived password or access keys requires sharing static credentials, which is less secure and harder to rotate or revoke than temporary role credentials. Option C is incorrect because you cannot add an IAM user from another account to an IAM group in your account; group membership is limited to principals within the same account. Option D is incorrect because permission boundaries limit the maximum permissions of an identity within the account and do not establish secure cross-account trust; it still relies on a static IAM user.

370.text:
Options C and D are correct because sharing an AMI backed by KMS-encrypted snapshots requires two separate grants. Option D adds the development team's account ARN to the AMI's launch permission list so that account is authorized to launch instances from the AMI. Option C updates the KMS key policy to allow the development team's OU to use the key, because instances cannot launch from an encrypted snapshot unless the launching account also has permission to use the KMS key that decrypts it. Both the AMI launch permission and the KMS key access are mandatory.

Option A is incorrect because AMI launch permissions are granted to specific account IDs (or made public), not to an OU ARN. Option B is incorrect for the same reason and because granting to the Organizations root is broader than required and still not a valid launch-permission target. Option E is incorrect because recreating the KMS key is unnecessary and disruptive; you simply update the existing key policy, and granting to the Organizations root rather than the development OU is overly broad.

371.text:
Option C is correct because the company must both migrate existing data and capture ongoing changes during the migration. AWS Schema Conversion Tool converts the heterogeneous Oracle schema to Aurora PostgreSQL, and AWS Database Migration Service supports a full-load-plus-change-data-capture (CDC) task that migrates the existing data and then continuously replicates ongoing changes from the source over the Site-to-Site VPN until cutover. This keeps the target in sync and minimizes downtime.

Option A is incorrect because a DMS full-load-only task migrates a point-in-time copy but does not capture changes that occur during the migration, violating the requirement. Option B is incorrect because AWS DataSync moves files and objects rather than performing relational database migration with schema conversion and continuous change capture. Option D is incorrect because an AWS Snowball device is an offline bulk transfer mechanism that cannot replicate ongoing transactional changes, and it is unnecessary given the existing VPN connection.

372.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon RDS automated backups already perform daily backups and capture transaction logs, and the retention period is a simple configurable setting that can be set up to 35 days. Increasing the retention period to 30 days satisfies the regulatory requirement directly through a native managed feature, with no custom code, scheduling, or scripts, giving it the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because writing and maintaining a Lambda function to create daily snapshots adds development and operational overhead and would also require separate logic to enforce the 30-day retention and clean up old snapshots. Option C is incorrect because Systems Manager Maintenance Windows schedule maintenance tasks and are not used to set the RDS backup retention period; this adds unnecessary complexity. Option D is incorrect because creating manual snapshots daily via the CLI requires custom scheduling and lifecycle management and is far more operationally intensive than simply adjusting the automated backup retention setting.

373.text:
Options B and E are correct because they together provide both scalability and high availability without changing the application code. Option E places an Application Load Balancer in a public subnet to receive internet traffic and distribute it across multiple instances, and Option B configures EC2 Auto Scaling across multiple Availability Zones in private subnets so capacity automatically grows and shrinks with demand while the instances remain protected behind the load balancer. Spanning multiple AZs ensures the application survives the failure of any single zone.

Option A is incorrect because replacing the instance with a larger compute optimized instance is vertical scaling on a single instance, which has an upper limit and provides no high availability. Option D is incorrect for the same reason; a larger memory optimized instance still leaves a single point of failure and limited scalability. Option C is incorrect because a NAT gateway only enables outbound internet access for resources in private subnets and does not receive or distribute inbound web requests.

374.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Application Discovery Service is purpose-built to collect usage and configuration data, including server specifications, performance metrics, and network dependencies, from on-premises servers to support migration planning. Setting the home AWS Region in AWS Migration Hub centralizes and stores this discovery data, giving the company the inventory and utilization insights needed to plan a migration to AWS.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager is designed for operational management and automation of resources rather than for the discovery and dependency-mapping of on-premises servers for migration assessment. Options C and D are incorrect because the AWS Schema Conversion Tool is used to convert database schemas, not to gather server usage data; AWS Trusted Advisor provides best-practice recommendations for existing AWS accounts rather than discovering on-premises servers; and AWS Database Migration Service replicates database data and does not collect general server and workload configuration data for planning.

375.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Glue DataBrew provides managed, no-code data preparation jobs for cleaning and aggregating the marketing data, while an AWS Step Functions state machine orchestrates those jobs. Step Functions natively manages execution state, built-in error handling, and retry logic, and it can run jobs in parallel as well as enforce a specific sequential order for the jobs that must run later. This combination removes the operational burden of writing custom orchestration code.

Option A is incorrect because chaining Lambda functions requires building your own state tracking, retry, and error-handling logic, which is exactly the overhead the company wants to remove, and Lambda is poorly suited to long-running aggregation jobs. Option B is incorrect because Athena runs SQL queries and EventBridge Scheduler only triggers them; this does not provide orchestrated parallel-then-sequential job flows with managed state and retries. Option D is incorrect because AWS Data Pipeline is a legacy service, and scheduling a single midnight run does not meet the requirement for regular parallel execution with ordered dependencies and managed error handling.

376.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon Quantum Ledger Database (QLDB) is a fully managed ledger database that maintains a complete, immutable, and cryptographically verifiable history of all changes to the data. This directly meets the requirement for immutable, cryptographically verifiable logs of data changes for accounting records, and as a serverless managed service it requires minimal operational support and is cost-effective for this purpose.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Redshift is a data warehouse for analytics and does not provide immutable, cryptographically verifiable change logs. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Neptune is a graph database for highly connected data and offers no built-in cryptographic verification of an immutable change history. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Timestream is a time-series database optimized for telemetry and IoT measurements, not for tamper-evident, cryptographically verifiable ledgers of accounting transactions.

377.text:
Option A is correct because a cluster placement group packs EC2 instances close together within a single Availability Zone on high-bandwidth, low-latency network segments. This is the recommended configuration for tightly coupled, node-to-node HPC workloads that need low-latency network performance and high throughput, and it can be combined with Elastic Fabric Adapter to further accelerate inter-node communication.

Option B is incorrect because Dedicated Instance tenancy only ensures the hardware is not shared with other AWS customers; it addresses isolation and compliance, not network latency or throughput between nodes. Option C is incorrect because Spot Instances are a pricing and capacity option that can be reclaimed at any time, which does not improve network performance and is risky for tightly coupled HPC jobs. Option D is incorrect because an On-Demand Capacity Reservation guarantees compute capacity availability but does nothing to reduce node-to-node network latency or increase throughput.

378.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon ECS with the AWS Fargate launch type runs containers serverlessly, removing the need to provision or manage EC2 instances.
