Option D is correct because AWS Transfer Family provides a fully managed FTP-compatible server, so existing FTP clients need minimal or no changes, and it can land incoming files directly in Amazon S3 Standard for immediate access. An S3 event notification invokes an AWS Lambda function the moment each file arrives, processing files as soon as possible, and because each file takes only 3-8 minutes it fits comfortably within Lambda's 15-minute limit; the Lambda function deletes each file after successful processing, meeting all requirements with high operational efficiency.

Options A and B are incorrect because running an FTP server on a self-managed EC2 instance adds operational overhead, and using EventBridge to process files nightly retains the slow overnight batch pattern instead of processing files immediately; A also stores files in Glacier Flexible Retrieval, which requires retrieval delays. Option C is incorrect because storing files on an EBS volume rather than S3 prevents using S3 event notifications to trigger processing on arrival and adds unnecessary complexity compared with Lambda-based processing directly from S3.

453.text:
Option D is correct because enabling Amazon EBS encryption for the EC2 instance volumes and Amazon RDS encryption, both using AWS KMS keys, encrypts all PII at rest on both the compute and database tiers transparently. These are native, built-in encryption features that require no application or schema changes, so they meet the compliance mandate for encryption at rest with the least amount of infrastructure change.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Certificate Manager issues TLS/SSL certificates for encryption in transit and cannot be used to encrypt database volumes at rest. Option B is incorrect because AWS CloudHSM provides dedicated hardware security modules for key management but introduces significant additional setup and operational complexity, which is more change than required. Option C is incorrect because SSL protects data in transit rather than at rest, and KMS keys are used directly for at-rest encryption of EBS and RDS volumes, not to configure SSL for volume encryption, so this option misapplies the technology to the requirement.

454.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Neptune is a fully managed graph database purpose-built to store and query highly connected data as vertices (user profiles) and edges (connections). Many-to-many relationships and deep traversals such as finding mutual connections up to five levels are exactly what graph query languages execute efficiently, delivering performant results that relational JOINs cannot match at this depth and scale.

Option A is incorrect because storing the data in Amazon S3 and using Amazon Athena to run multi-level recursive JOINs would require chaining many self-joins, which is slow, complex, and not performance efficient for five levels of relationship traversal. Option C is incorrect because Amazon QuickSight is a business intelligence visualization tool and cannot traverse multi-level graph relationships to compute mutual connections. Option D is incorrect because Amazon RDS with multiple tables and SQL JOINs degrades severely when performing five levels of recursive many-to-many joins across millions of connections, making it inefficient for this graph traversal workload.

455.text:
Option A is correct because using Amazon Elastic Container Registry as the private repository for the container images and enabling scan on push lets ECR automatically scan each image for CVEs as it is pushed, covering both existing images that are re-pushed and any new images. Because ECS already pulls images from a registry, adopting ECR with basic scanning requires the fewest changes to the existing workloads and adds no new infrastructure or pipelines.

Options B and D are incorrect because they require moving images into Amazon S3 and building custom scanning workflows; Amazon Macie classifies sensitive data and does not scan container images for CVEs, and wiring S3 events to Lambda and Amazon Inspector adds substantial new components and changes. Option C is incorrect because, although ECR enhanced scanning detects CVEs, migrating the workloads from ECS to Amazon EKS is a major architectural change that is unnecessary to meet the requirement, conflicting with the goal of the fewest changes to the workloads.

456.text:
Option C is correct because using an AWS Glue crawler to catalog the table metadata for the Parquet files in Amazon S3 and querying with Amazon Athena lets the company run SQL directly against the data in place. Athena is serverless and charges only for data scanned per query, and since the data is already in columnar Parquet format, scans are minimal; for occasional analysis there is no cluster to provision or pay for when idle, making it the most cost-effective option.

Option A is incorrect because loading 10 TB into Amazon Aurora via AWS DMS incurs migration effort plus the ongoing cost of a running database for only occasional queries. Option B is incorrect because an Amazon Redshift cluster, even with Spectrum, requires provisioning and paying for cluster nodes that run continuously despite infrequent use. Option D is incorrect because an Amazon EMR cluster running Spark SQL adds cluster management overhead and continuous compute cost that is not justified for occasional ad hoc SQL analysis.

457.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Config provides a managed rule named restricted-ssh that automatically evaluates security groups and flags any that allow inbound SSH from 0.0.0.0/0 as noncompliant. Enabling this prebuilt rule and routing its compliance change events through Amazon EventBridge to an Amazon SNS notification delivers automated detection and alerting with the least operational overhead, since there is no custom code to write or maintain and it can be deployed quickly.

Option A is incorrect because writing and maintaining a custom AWS Lambda script to poll security groups duplicates the functionality of the managed Config rule and adds operational overhead. Option C is incorrect because creating an IAM role and notifying when it is assumed does not actually detect or report security groups that allow SSH from 0.0.0.0/0. Option D is incorrect because an SCP that blocks non-administrative users from editing security groups changes the permission model, does not detect existing noncompliant rules, and relies on manual ticketing rather than automated breach notification.

458.text:
Options A and C are correct because running the EC2 instances in a cluster placement group packs them onto hardware that is physically close together within a single Availability Zone, providing the lowest possible network latency and high per-flow throughput between nodes. Enabling and configuring enhanced networking (using the Elastic Network Adapter with SR-IOV) further reduces latency and jitter and increases packets-per-second performance, which together optimize node-to-node communication for the streaming workload.

Option B is incorrect because grouping instances into separate AWS accounts has no effect on inter-node latency and would actually complicate networking between them. Option D is incorrect because attaching multiple elastic network interfaces increases the number of network connections an instance can have but does not lower latency between nodes. Option E is incorrect because Amazon EBS-optimized instance types dedicate bandwidth to EBS storage traffic, improving disk throughput rather than reducing network latency between EC2 instances.

459.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon S3 Same-Region Replication (SRR) automatically and asynchronously copies objects from the source buckets into a single centralized bucket within us-west-2, satisfying the requirement that logs never leave the Region. SRR is a fully managed, native S3 feature that requires only a replication configuration, so it incurs minimal operational overhead and is cost-effective because you pay only for the replication request and storage, with no compute to manage.

Option A is incorrect because S3 Lifecycle policies transition or expire objects within their own bucket; they cannot copy objects to a different destination bucket. Option C is incorrect because a daily script that re-copies entire bucket contents using PutObject is operationally heavy, wasteful (re-copying unchanged data), and not near-real-time. Option D works technically but requires writing, deploying, and maintaining Lambda functions and event triggers in every account, adding significant operational overhead and per-invocation cost compared with the managed SRR feature.

460.text:
Option B is correct because predictive scaling analyzes historical CloudWatch metrics to forecast daily and weekly workload trends and proactively launches capacity ahead of anticipated demand, while combining it with dynamic scaling using target tracking handles real-time deviations by adjusting capacity to keep a metric (such as average CPU utilization) at a target value. Together they satisfy both the forecast-based and live-utilization scaling requirements with minimal manual effort.

Option A is incorrect because step scaling reacts only to current CloudWatch alarms; it cannot forecast based on historical daily or weekly trends. Option C is incorrect because scheduled scaling requires you to manually define fixed scaling times and capacities, and it does not automatically learn from historical trends or respond to live changes in utilization. Option D is incorrect because a simple scaling policy with a longer cooldown is purely reactive, scales slowly, and provides no forecasting capability, leaving the application under-provisioned during sudden demand spikes.

461.text:
Option A is correct because a gateway VPC endpoint for Amazon S3 enables private connectivity between EC2 instances and S3 over the AWS network without traversing the public internet, and pairing it with an S3 bucket policy that restricts access to that VPC endpoint (using the aws:sourceVpce condition) ensures the classified data is reachable only through the private, secure path. This satisfies both the private connection and secure access requirements.

Option B is incorrect because granting read-write access through an IAM policy controls permissions but does nothing to make the network path private or secure; traffic could still flow over the public internet. Option C is incorrect because a NAT gateway routes traffic to the internet, which is the opposite of a private connection and exposes data to the public network. Option D is incorrect because static access keys are a credential management approach that does not provide a private network connection and introduces long-lived secrets, which is a security risk.

462.text:
Options A and C are correct because the application uses both TCP and UDP, and a Network Load Balancer operates at Layer 4 and natively supports both protocols to distribute traffic to the application in each Region. AWS Global Accelerator provides static anycast IP addresses and routes user traffic over the AWS global backbone to the optimal regional endpoint (the load balancers), delivering high availability with automatic regional failover and minimum latency for global users.

Option B is incorrect because an Application Load Balancer operates at Layer 7 and supports only HTTP/HTTPS, so it cannot handle the required UDP traffic. Option D is incorrect because Route 53 geolocation routing directs users based on their location rather than lowest latency, and DNS-based failover is slower and less seamless than Global Accelerator's anycast routing. Option E is incorrect because CloudFront is a CDN optimized for HTTP/HTTPS content delivery and does not support UDP or arbitrary TCP gaming protocols.

463.text:
Options B and D are correct because the applications use both NFS file storage and block storage and must continue working without re-architecting. AWS Storage Gateway file gateway presents an NFS (and SMB) interface backed by Amazon S3, replacing the NFS storage transparently, while the volume gateway presents iSCSI block volumes backed by AWS storage, replacing the block storage. Both provide local caching of frequently accessed data for high performance and require no application changes.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon S3 is object storage and cannot be natively mounted as a POSIX file system to support existing NFS applications. Option C is incorrect because AWS Snowball Edge is a data-transfer and edge-compute device, not a persistent, cloud-backed storage tier with local caching for ongoing application use. Option E is incorrect because Amazon EFS provides only NFS file storage and does not offer block storage, so it cannot replace the block-storage requirement, and EFS lacks the local on-premises caching that Storage Gateway provides.

464.text:
Options B and E are correct because Amazon EFS is a fully managed, POSIX-compliant shared file system that can be mounted concurrently on all EC2 instances across multiple Availability Zones and provides strong read-after-write consistency, so new content is visible immediately after a change. Alternatively, storing content in S3 and setting the Cache-Control header to no-cache forces CloudFront and clients to revalidate with the origin, ensuring the latest content is served as soon as changes occur.

Option A is incorrect because a Storage Gateway volume gateway exposes iSCSI block volumes that are not designed for concurrent shared access across many EC2 instances and is intended for hybrid on-premises use. Option C is incorrect because a standard EBS volume cannot be shared by instances across different Availability Zones. Option D is incorrect because DataSync performs scheduled, periodic data transfers rather than continuous, immediately consistent synchronization, so instances could serve stale content between sync runs.

465.text:
Options C and D are correct because S3 multipart upload splits a large file into parts that are uploaded in parallel, dramatically increasing upload throughput from the on-premises data center and improving resilience to network errors. For downloads to the EC2 transcoding instances, fetching multiple byte-ranges of an object in parallel (parallel GET requests using the Range header) maximizes download throughput by using multiple connections simultaneously.

Option A is incorrect because S3 access points simplify managing access permissions for shared datasets; they do not increase transfer throughput. Option B is incorrect because spreading files across multiple buckets does not improve per-object throughput, since S3 already scales request rates automatically per prefix. Option E is incorrect because adding random prefixes was a legacy technique for request distribution that S3 no longer requires, as S3 now automatically scales to high request rates per prefix, so it provides no throughput benefit for these GB-sized file transfers.

466.text:
Option B is correct because AWS WAF is a managed web application firewall that inspects incoming HTTP/HTTPS requests and blocks common web exploits such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting using managed rule groups, protecting the application with the least operational overhead. RDS parameter groups are used to configure database engine security settings, complementing the layered protection without requiring custom infrastructure.

Option A is incorrect because security groups and network ACLs filter traffic at the network and port level but cannot inspect request payloads to detect SQL injection or other application-layer attacks. Option C is incorrect because AWS Network Firewall protects VPC traffic at the network layer and is more complex to operate; it is not purpose-built to block application-layer web exploits like SQL injection. Option D is incorrect because using least-privilege database accounts is a good practice already implied in the scenario, but it does not actively detect or block SQL injection and web-based attacks reaching the application.

467.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon Inspector is a vulnerability management service that continually scans EC2 instances for software vulnerabilities (CVEs) and unintended network exposure. With the Amazon Inspector agent (via the Systems Manager agent) deployed, it actively assesses the instances, and an AWS Lambda function can automate generating and distributing reports of the findings, directly meeting the requirement to scan for vulnerabilities and report on them.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Shield protects against DDoS attacks and does not scan instances for software vulnerabilities. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Macie discovers and protects sensitive data stored in Amazon S3; it does not perform vulnerability scanning on EC2 instances. Option C is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat-detection service that analyzes logs and network activity for malicious behavior, but it is not a vulnerability scanner that assesses installed software for known CVEs, which is precisely what the scenario requires.

468.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) is a fully managed, in-memory cache purpose-built for DynamoDB that delivers microsecond read latency for eventually consistent reads. It is API-compatible with DynamoDB, so the only application change needed is pointing the client to the DAX endpoint, satisfying the low-latency requirement with minimal code changes.

Option B is incorrect because DynamoDB does not have a separate read replica feature with a distinct read endpoint; reads are served directly by DynamoDB or by DAX, so this option describes functionality that does not exist. Option C is incorrect because increasing read capacity units improves throughput but does not reduce per-read latency the way an in-memory cache does. Option D is incorrect because ElastiCache for Redis requires significant application changes to manage cache population, invalidation, and fallback logic to DynamoDB, which is far more than the minimal change requirement and adds operational complexity compared with the drop-in DAX cache.

469.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) is a fully managed, in-memory caching layer designed specifically for DynamoDB that reduces read response times from single-digit milliseconds to microseconds. Because DAX is API-compatible with DynamoDB and AWS handles provisioning, patching, and scaling of the cache nodes, routing reads through DAX decreases latency with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because ElastiCache for Redis requires you to write and maintain custom code for cache population and invalidation, increasing operational overhead. Option C is incorrect for the same reason and additionally Memcached lacks the tight DynamoDB integration that DAX provides. Option D is incorrect because building a DynamoDB Streams plus Lambda pipeline to populate ElastiCache is the most complex option, requiring you to develop, deploy, and operate stream processing, a Lambda function, and a cache, which adds substantial operational overhead compared with the fully managed DAX cluster.

470.text:
Option C is correct because AWS CloudTrail records API calls and management events made in the account, capturing who performed each action, the source IP, the time, and the parameters. To determine which IAM user modified the security group rules, the solutions architect can search CloudTrail event history for the AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress or RevokeSecurityGroup API calls and identify the responsible user identity.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat-detection service that identifies malicious or anomalous activity; it does not provide a definitive audit log of which user made a specific configuration change. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Inspector scans for software vulnerabilities and network exposure, not user activity. Option D is incorrect because while AWS Config tracks resource configuration changes and compliance over time, the authoritative record of which IAM user invoked the change comes from CloudTrail; Config relies on CloudTrail data to attribute changes and is oriented toward configuration state rather than user attribution.

471.text:
Option C is correct because the batch jobs are fault-tolerant—if a job fails on one instance another instance reprocesses it—which makes Amazon EC2 Spot Instances ideal. Spot Instances offer up to 90% savings over On-Demand pricing, and the application's ability to tolerate interruptions means occasional Spot reclamation is acceptable. Configuring the Auto Scaling group's launch template to use Spot and scaling out on CPU usage provides the most cost-effective capacity for these nightly jobs.

Option A is incorrect because a Savings Plan commits to a steady hourly spend for a full year, which is wasteful for a workload that runs only six hours per day. Option B is incorrect for the same reason: Reserved Instances require a year-long commitment and are billed continuously, so they are inefficient for an intermittent batch workload. Option D is incorrect because increasing instance size keeps On-Demand pricing and does not address cost; larger instances simply cost more without leveraging the workload's fault tolerance.

472.text:
Option B is correct because the application uses both TCP and UDP traffic, and a Network Load Balancer operates at Layer 4 and natively supports both protocols, distributing traffic to the EC2 instances in the Auto Scaling group. Placing the NLB behind an AWS Global Accelerator accelerator provides static anycast IPs and routes global users over the AWS backbone to the nearest healthy endpoint, ensuring the lowest possible latency.

Option A is incorrect because an Application Load Balancer is a Layer 7 load balancer that supports only HTTP/HTTPS and cannot handle UDP traffic. Options C and D are both incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is a CDN optimized for HTTP/HTTPS content delivery and does not support UDP or arbitrary TCP gaming protocols, so it cannot front this application. Option D compounds the problem by also using an ALB, which cannot process the required UDP traffic. Only the NLB-plus-Global Accelerator combination satisfies both the protocol and global low-latency requirements.

473.text:
Option B is correct because it addresses every reliability gap. Enabling Multi-AZ on the RDS instance provides automatic failover to a standby in another Availability Zone, and deletion protection prevents accidental deletion of the database, which caused the prior 24-hour outage. Placing the EC2 web servers behind an Application Load Balancer in an Auto Scaling group across multiple Availability Zones removes the single-AZ point of failure and automatically replaces unhealthy instances, maximizing availability.

Option A is incorrect because deleting one EC2 instance reduces redundancy, and termination protection on a single instance still leaves a single point of failure within one Availability Zone. Option C is incorrect because writing to two independent DB instances via Lambda introduces a complex, error-prone custom data-replication design rather than using the managed Multi-AZ feature. Option D is incorrect because using Spot Instances for a persistent web tier risks interruption and unavailability, undermining the reliability the company requires.

474.text:
Option B is correct because AWS security best practice is to lock away the root user and use IAM users (or roles) for daily administrative tasks, granting only the permissions needed. Enabling multi-factor authentication on the root user adds a critical second layer of protection so that even if the root password is compromised, an attacker cannot sign in without the MFA device, securing the most privileged account.

Option A is incorrect because the root user cannot be disabled or deleted; it always exists for account-level tasks, so MFA is the correct way to protect it. Option C is incorrect because generating and using a root access key for daily tasks is explicitly discouraged, as long-lived root credentials in use dramatically increase the risk of compromise. Option D is incorrect because sharing root credentials for routine administration violates least-privilege and accountability principles; daily work should be performed by individual IAM identities, not the root user.

475.text:
Option B is correct because the devices communicate over UDP, which requires a Network Load Balancer operating at Layer 4 to handle UDP listeners. Fronting NLBs in two Regions with AWS Global Accelerator provides static anycast IPs and routes traffic over the AWS backbone for minimal latency, while delivering rapid, automatic failover to the healthy Region without DNS propagation delays. Processing the data in Amazon ECS on Fargate provides serverless, managed compute for the immediate processing requirement.

Options C and D are incorrect because they use an Application Load Balancer, which is a Layer 7 HTTP/HTTPS balancer that does not support UDP. Options A and D are incorrect because they rely on Route 53 failover routing, which depends on DNS TTL and client-side caching, causing slower failover than Global Accelerator's anycast-based regional failover. Option A additionally cannot meet the requirement well because Route 53 failover is not as rapid as the Global Accelerator approach in Option B.

476.text:
Option A is correct because the application uses UDP, which requires a Network Load Balancer at Layer 4 to support UDP listeners, and the NLBs can target the on-premises endpoints by IP address. Registering these NLBs with AWS Global Accelerator provides static anycast IP addresses and routes users over the optimized AWS global backbone to the nearest healthy endpoint, improving both performance and availability while keeping the application hosted on premises as required.

Options B and D are incorrect because an Application Load Balancer operates at Layer 7 and supports only HTTP/HTTPS, so it cannot serve the UDP-based application. Options C and D are incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is a CDN for HTTP/HTTPS content and does not support UDP, and a Route 53 latency-based record relies on DNS resolution rather than anycast backbone routing, providing weaker performance and slower failover than Global Accelerator. Only the NLB-plus-Global Accelerator design in Option A satisfies the UDP and on-premises constraints.

477.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Simple Email Service (Amazon SES) is a fully managed, highly scalable email service designed for transactional and marketing email such as order confirmations. Configuring the web instances to send through SES offloads complex email delivery concerns—deliverability, reputation, retries, and scaling—to AWS, reducing the time spent resolving delivery issues and minimizing operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because building a dedicated EC2 email tier means operating your own mail servers, which increases operational overhead and still leaves the company managing complex deliverability and reputation issues. Option D is incorrect for the same reason; adding an Auto Scaling group to self-managed mail servers scales the problem but does not eliminate the operational burden. Option C is incorrect because Amazon SNS is a pub/sub messaging service for notifications to subscribers and applications; it is not a full-featured email-sending service and cannot deliver formatted transactional or marketing email to customer inboxes the way SES does.

478.text:
Options C and D are correct because security groups are stateful and are the appropriate control for these requirements. Creating a security group for the web servers that allows inbound traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 on port 443 exposes the web tier to the internet over HTTPS as required. Creating a security group for the RDS DB instance that allows inbound traffic on port 3306 only from the web servers' security group restricts database access to exactly those web servers, which is more precise and maintainable than CIDR-based rules.

Option A is incorrect because security groups, being stateful, already prevent unsolicited inbound database connections, so a network ACL deny rule for outbound 3306 is unnecessary and could disrupt legitimate return traffic. Option B is incorrect because allowing the entire public subnet CIDR block is broader than necessary and grants access to any resource in that subnet rather than only the web servers. Option E is incorrect because security groups support only allow rules; they cannot contain explicit deny rules, so this configuration is invalid.

479.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon EFS is a fully managed, regional file system that is automatically highly available and durable across multiple Availability Zones, and it supports the EFS CSI driver for Amazon EKS Fargate, allowing the file system to be mounted and shared concurrently by multiple application containers. Registering it in a StorageClass object provides persistent shared storage with the least operational overhead because AWS manages availability, scaling, and durability.

Option A is incorrect because EBS Multi-Attach is limited to io1/io2 volumes within a single Availability Zone and is not supported by Fargate, which has no EC2 worker nodes to attach volumes to. Option C is incorrect because a single EBS volume is tied to one Availability Zone and cannot be shared across many Fargate containers, providing neither high availability nor multi-container sharing. Option D is incorrect because creating multiple EFS file systems and synchronizing them with a custom Lambda function adds substantial, unnecessary operational overhead, since a single EFS file system is already multi-AZ and shareable.

480.text:
Option B is correct because the write-through caching strategy updates the cache at the same time data is written to the database, so whenever a customer adds an item it is written to both the RDS database and ElastiCache simultaneously. This keeps the cache data continuously consistent with the database, satisfying the requirement that the cache always matches the database and that data is added or updated in the cache on every write.

Option A is incorrect because lazy loading populates the cache only when data is first read after a miss, so newly written items would not be in the cache until read, and the cache can serve stale data, violating the consistency requirement. Option C is incorrect because adding a TTL causes cache entries to expire after a set time, which means data can become temporarily stale before refresh and does not guarantee the cache always matches the database on writes. Option D is incorrect because AWS AppConfig manages application configuration data and is not an ElastiCache caching strategy.

481.text:
Option C is correct because Workload Discovery on AWS is a purpose-built solution that maintains an inventory of AWS resources and automatically generates detailed architecture diagrams showing the relationships and dependencies between workloads across multiple accounts and Regions. This directly meets the requirement to build and map workload relationship details in the most operationally efficient way, with little manual effort.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager Inventory collects software and configuration metadata from managed instances but does not automatically map cross-account, cross-Region relationships into architecture diagrams. Option B is incorrect because using Step Functions to gather details and then drawing diagrams manually is labor-intensive and not operationally efficient. Option D is incorrect because AWS X-Ray traces requests through distributed applications for performance analysis and debugging; it does not produce a resource inventory or full architecture relationship maps across all accounts and Regions.

482.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EFS is a fully managed, POSIX-compliant file system that can be shared concurrently across EC2 instances in multiple Availability Zones, and the EFS Standard storage class stores data redundantly across multiple Availability Zones for maximum durability and high availability. Applying a lifecycle management policy to transition infrequently accessed data to EFS Standard-Infrequent Access (Standard-IA) after 30 days reduces storage costs while preserving the shared, multi-AZ POSIX file system, making it the most cost-effective fit.

Options A and B are incorrect because Amazon S3 is object storage and is not POSIX-compliant, so it cannot serve as the shared file system layer the application requires. Option D is incorrect because EFS One Zone stores data in only a single Availability Zone, which does not provide the maximum durability or the multi-AZ high availability the requirements demand, even though it is cheaper. Only the multi-AZ EFS Standard with Standard-IA lifecycle (Option C) meets durability, sharing, POSIX, and cost goals together.

483.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon ElastiCache provides an in-memory caching layer (Redis or Memcached) that stores frequently requested, identical datasets in memory. The backend tier can check the cache first and only query RDS on a cache miss, dramatically reducing repeated read load on the database and improving response latency for the duplicate queries described.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon SNS is a pub/sub notification service and cannot store or serve query results to reduce database load. Option C is incorrect because while a read replica offloads read traffic, it does not cache identical datasets in memory; it still executes each query against the database engine, providing far less performance benefit for repeated identical reads than an in-memory cache. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose is designed for streaming ingestion and delivery of data to destinations like S3 or Redshift, not for caching or accelerating repetitive read queries against an RDS instance.

484.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Glue is a serverless ETL service that transforms data daily with no infrastructure to manage, and Amazon Redshift Serverless is a serverless MPP data warehouse that meets the massively parallel processing requirement without provisioning clusters. Redshift ML lets analysts create and train ML models directly with standard SQL commands, satisfying every requirement using serverless services.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon EMR requires managing a cluster and provisioned Redshift requires capacity management, so neither is fully serverless. Option B is incorrect because Aurora Serverless is a transactional (OLTP) relational engine that lacks the columnar MPP architecture required for a data warehouse, even though it scales automatically. Option D is incorrect because Athena is a serverless query service over S3, not an MPP data warehouse with loaded tables, and there is no Athena ML feature for building and training models with SQL.

485.text:
Option A is correct because AWS AppConfig is purpose-built to store, validate, and deploy application configuration with controlled rollouts, while AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built to securely store, encrypt, retrieve, and automatically rotate database credentials. Using these two managed services together delivers the required functionality with the least administrative overhead and follows AWS security best practices.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Lambda is a compute service, not a configuration store, so using it to manage configuration adds custom code and overhead; Parameter Store can hold credentials but does not provide native automatic rotation for RDS like Secrets Manager. Option C is incorrect because storing config and credentials as files in Amazon S3 requires building custom encryption, access control, and rotation logic, increasing overhead and risk. Option D is incorrect because Amazon RDS is a database, not a credential management service, and storing credentials there creates a circular dependency and provides no secure rotation mechanism.

486.text:
Option A is correct because creating AMIs and copying them to a secondary Region keeps almost no resources running during normal operations (only stored images), satisfying the requirement to use the fewest resources. On a disaster, automated deployment via AWS Lambda and custom scripts can launch the infrastructure well within the 4-hour RTO, making this a cost-efficient pilot-light/backup-and-restore style approach.

Option B also copies AMIs but uses AWS CloudFormation; while valid, the question marks Lambda automation as the intended answer for operational efficiency in this scenario. Option C is incorrect because keeping EC2 instances continuously active in a secondary Region is a warm/hot standby that wastes resources during normal operations, violating the fewest-resources requirement. Option D is incorrect because launching instances in a secondary Availability Zone does not provide Regional disaster recovery and still keeps resources running continuously, failing both the DR scope and the resource-minimization requirements.

487.text:
Option B is correct because the job is short (10 seconds), event-driven, and consumes only 1 GB of memory, which is an ideal fit for AWS Lambda. Lambda charges only for the milliseconds of execution, eliminates all server management, and an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule triggers it hourly. This is the most cost-optimized approach because you pay nothing between the brief executions.

Option A is incorrect because running the job as a Fargate task still incurs per-second billing for task startup and runtime and adds containerization effort, costing more than Lambda for a 10-second job. Option C is incorrect because installing a container in the existing AMI keeps the underlying EC2 instance running and adds significant operational complexity. Option D is incorrect because stopping and restarting the EC2 instance hourly introduces boot-time delays and management overhead, and you still pay for EBS storage and instance resources, making it far less cost-effective than serverless Lambda.

488.text:
Options C, E, and F are correct because together they align with the Well-Architected Framework for security, scalability, and resiliency. Option C refactors the application into separate web, application, and database tiers across two Availability Zones in private subnets with Auto Scaling for the web and application tiers, enabling scalability and isolation. Option E places Elastic Load Balancers in front of the web tier and uses security group references for layered access control. Option F uses a Multi-AZ RDS deployment in private subnets restricted to the application tier security group, providing database resiliency and security.

Options A and B are incorrect because they keep the application's existing single-server architecture and a single (non-Multi-AZ) RDS database, which provides no tier separation and no database failover, undermining resiliency. Option D is incorrect because it specifies a single RDS database with no Multi-AZ deployment, leaving the data tier without high availability and failing the resiliency requirement.

489.text:
Option A is correct because AWS DataSync is a managed data transfer service that natively supports S3 and EFS as locations and continuously copies files on a schedule with minimal setup. Setting the transfer mode to transfer only data that has changed ensures files are overwritten only when the source changes, meeting the incremental requirement with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because building and maintaining an AWS Lambda function with mounted EFS, S3 event wiring, and custom copy logic adds significant code and operational burden compared to a managed service. Option C is incorrect because setting the transfer mode to transfer all data copies and overwrites every file on each run rather than only changed files, wasting bandwidth and violating the change-only requirement. Option D is incorrect because running an EC2 instance with a custom synchronization script requires provisioning, patching, scaling, and maintaining the instance and script, which is the highest operational overhead of the options.

490.text:
Option C is correct because generating Amazon S3 presigned URLs lets users upload photos directly from their browsers into the S3 bucket, bypassing the application servers entirely. This leverages the virtually unlimited scalability and throughput of S3, so the application tier never becomes a bottleneck during traffic surges at large events, providing the most scalable solution.

Option A is incorrect because routing every upload through the application servers makes those servers a scaling bottleneck and consumes their compute and bandwidth, limiting scalability under heavy demand. Option B is incorrect because AWS Storage Gateway file gateway is designed for hybrid on-premises file storage integration, not for handling high-volume direct browser uploads at internet scale. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EFS is a shared file system for compute resources within a VPC and is not intended for direct, massively concurrent browser uploads, making it far less scalable than S3 with presigned URLs.

491.text:
Option B is correct because a NAT gateway placed in a public subnet allows instances in private subnets to initiate outbound connections to the internet (such as downloading security updates) while preventing the internet from initiating inbound connections. Configuring the private subnet's route table to send default traffic to the NAT gateway provides the required managed, highly available outbound access.

Option A is incorrect because attaching an internet gateway and routing the private subnet directly to it would make the subnet public and expose the instance to inbound internet traffic, which is not the secure outbound-only requirement. Option C is incorrect because a NAT instance placed in the same private subnet has no route to the internet itself, so it cannot provide outbound connectivity. Option D is incorrect because placing a NAT instance in the private subnet and routing to the internet gateway is misconfigured; the NAT must reside in a public subnet, and a private-subnet NAT instance cannot reach the internet.

492.text:
Option D is correct because attaching the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy to the EC2 instance role enables AWS Systems Manager Session Manager, which provides secure, browser- or CLI-based shell access without opening inbound SSH ports, managing SSH keys, or running a bastion host. It works for developers both remotely and in the office, leverages the existing NAT gateway for the SSM agent's outbound connectivity, and is the most cost-effective managed AWS solution.

Option A is incorrect because the ec2:CreateVpnConnection permission is unrelated to instance access and a bastion in the same private subnet has no public reachability. Option C is incorrect because a bastion host in the public subnet adds an always-running EC2 instance to manage and patch, requires open SSH ports and key management, increasing cost and attack surface. Option B is incorrect because Site-to-Site VPN only serves the corporate network and forcing developers to set up additional VPN connections for remote work is operationally complex and costly.

493.text:
Option D is correct because with all network settings in their default states, the default security group does not allow traffic between separate security groups, so the RDS instance is rejecting connections from the web tier. Adding an inbound rule to the database security group that allows traffic from the web tier's security group on the MySQL port establishes the required connectivity following least-privilege best practices.

Option A is incorrect because the default network ACL already allows all inbound and outbound traffic, so the ACL is not blocking the connection. Option B is incorrect because all subnets within a VPC are automatically routable through the local route, so no additional route is needed for intra-VPC communication. Option C is incorrect because moving the tiers into separate VPCs with VPC peering is an unnecessary and complex re-architecture; the tiers already share a VPC and only need a security group rule to communicate.

494.text:
Option C is correct because the Recycle Bin for Amazon EBS and AMIs lets you define retention rules that automatically retain deleted AMIs (and their associated snapshots) for a specified period, allowing quick and easy recovery of accidentally deleted images. This is a native, managed feature requiring only a rule configuration, giving it the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because creating EBS snapshots and copying them to a separate account requires manual orchestration and a rebuild process to recreate the AMI, adding overhead and not directly recovering the AMI. Option B is incorrect because periodically copying all AMIs to another account demands custom automation and cross-account management, increasing complexity and cost. Option D is incorrect because AMIs cannot simply be uploaded to and restored from an S3 bucket as objects; this approach is not how AMI recovery works and would require complex export/import processes, making it impractical compared to Recycle Bin retention rules.

495.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon DynamoDB uses eventually consistent reads by default, which can return stale data immediately after a write. Requesting strongly consistent reads forces DynamoDB to return the most up-to-date data reflecting all prior successful writes, directly resolving the problem of requests not returning the latest data, while latency remains acceptable.

Option A is incorrect because DynamoDB does not use user-managed read replicas; it is a fully managed service and adding read replicas is not a configurable option to improve consistency. Option B is incorrect because a global secondary index supports alternate query patterns but is itself only eventually consistent and would not guarantee the latest data. Option D is incorrect because eventually consistent reads are the default behavior already causing the stale-data problem, so requesting them would not fix the issue and would perpetuate the symptom the architect is trying to resolve.

496.text:
Option A is correct because deploying AWS Control Tower in the Organizations management account establishes a governed multi-account landing zone that automatically enables AWS CloudTrail across all existing and new accounts to audit API calls and logins. Enabling AWS Security Hub provides continuous monitoring against the AWS Foundational Security Best Practices standard with compliance findings, and Account Factory automates compliant account provisioning, meeting all requirements with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Control Tower must be set up from the Organizations management account, not a dedicated member account, so this deployment location is not supported. Options C and D are incorrect because AWS Managed Services (AMS) Accelerate with RFC-based self-service provisioning introduces significant operational process and cost and is more involved than the native, automated Control Tower approach; additionally Option C provisions GuardDuty, which provides threat detection but not FSBP compliance reporting.

497.text:
Option C is correct because a public NAT gateway must reside in a public subnet (one with a route to an internet gateway) so it can translate and forward outbound traffic to the internet. EC2 instances in private subnets then route their default traffic to that NAT gateway, enabling outbound internet connectivity while remaining unreachable from the internet, which precisely meets the requirement.

Option A is incorrect because placing a public NAT gateway in the same private subnet as the instances gives it no route to an internet gateway, so it cannot reach the internet. Option B is incorrect because private NAT gateways are designed for VPC-to-VPC or on-premises private communication, not for public internet access, so they cannot connect instances to the public internet. Option D is incorrect because a private NAT gateway placed in a public subnet still does not provide public internet access, since private NAT gateways do not route to the internet gateway.

498.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon RDS Proxy pools and shares database connections, preventing Lambda from overwhelming the PostgreSQL instance with too many connections during unpredictable invocation spikes, which maintains predictable database performance. Pointing the client driver at the RDS Proxy endpoint and deploying the Lambda functions inside the VPC allows them to reach the private RDS instance through the proxy.

Option A is incorrect because an RDS custom endpoint only directs traffic to a specific set of instances in a cluster and does not pool connections, so it cannot prevent connection exhaustion. Option C is incorrect because, in addition to using a custom endpoint that does not manage connections, deploying Lambda outside the VPC prevents it from reaching the private RDS instance. Option D is incorrect because, although it uses the proxy endpoint, placing the Lambda functions outside the VPC means they cannot connect to the private RDS for PostgreSQL instance, breaking connectivity.

499.text:
Option B is correct because the encrypted Aurora snapshot is protected by a customer managed KMS key, and to share it cross-account both the snapshot and the key must be shared. Adding the acquiring company's AWS account to the KMS key policy grants them the permission to decrypt, and then sharing the snapshot with their account allows them to copy and restore it securely, meeting the requirement.

Option A is incorrect because you cannot copy an encrypted snapshot to an unencrypted snapshot; encrypted snapshots remain encrypted, and removing encryption would also violate the confidential-data security requirement. Option C is incorrect because you cannot re-encrypt a snapshot with an AWS managed key for sharing, and you cannot grant cross-account access by adding an account to a KMS key alias; aliases are just friendly names, not permission grants. Option D is incorrect because Aurora snapshots cannot be downloaded as files and uploaded to S3; this manual export approach is not supported and bypasses secure native sharing.

500.text:
Option A is correct because S3 Lifecycle configuration natively supports expiring noncurrent (older) object versions automatically. By configuring a rule to retain the desired number of newer noncurrent versions and expire the rest, the company keeps only the two most recent versions and deletes the older ones automatically, reducing storage costs with the least operational overhead and no custom code.

Option B is incorrect because building and maintaining an AWS Lambda function to scan and delete old versions requires custom code, scheduling, and ongoing maintenance, adding operational overhead compared to native lifecycle rules. Option C is incorrect because S3 Batch Operations require generating manifests and running discrete jobs, which is a manual, repetitive process rather than an automated continuous policy. Option D is incorrect because deactivating versioning stops new versions from being created but does not remove existing noncurrent versions or enforce retaining only the two most recent, so it fails to reliably control costs or meet the retention requirement.

501.text:
Option C is correct because a VPC gateway endpoint for Amazon S3 routes traffic to S3 privately over the AWS network without traversing the NAT gateway or the internet. Gateway endpoints incur no hourly or data processing charges, so eliminating the NAT gateway data path removes the per-gigabyte data processing costs, making this the most cost-effective solution for high-volume same-Region S3 access.

Option A is incorrect because a dedicated EC2 NAT instance still routes S3 traffic through an instance you must manage and pay for, and still incurs data transfer costs without the savings of a gateway endpoint. Option B is incorrect because placing a NAT instance in the private subnet provides no path to S3 and is misconfigured for this routing. Option D is incorrect because provisioning a second NAT gateway adds more NAT data processing charges rather than reducing them, increasing rather than lowering data output costs.

502.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Macie is a managed data security service that uses machine learning and built-in managed data identifiers to discover sensitive data such as personally identifiable information and financial data in Amazon S3, including passport numbers and credit card numbers. Running a Macie data discovery job with the relevant managed identifiers directly meets the requirement to find this sensitive data.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Audit Manager helps assess and report on compliance frameworks like PCI DSS but does not scan S3 object contents to discover PII or financial data. Option B is incorrect because S3 Inventory only lists object metadata (keys, sizes, encryption status) and querying it with Athena cannot inspect object contents for sensitive data. Option D is incorrect because Amazon S3 Select retrieves subsets of data from individual objects using SQL expressions and cannot automatically discover or classify PII and financial information across a bucket.

503.text:
Options D, E, and F are correct because they form a fully managed AI pipeline. Amazon Transcribe (D) converts the customer service call audio recordings in any of the supported languages into text. Amazon Translate (E) then translates that text from any language into English. Amazon Comprehend (F) performs natural language processing to generate the sentiment analysis reports. All three are managed services requiring no ML model maintenance, satisfying the company's constraints.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Comprehend performs NLP tasks like sentiment analysis, not translation, and it does not process audio. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Lex builds conversational chatbots and voice interfaces, not sentiment analysis reports. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Polly is a text-to-speech service that converts text into audio, the opposite of the speech-to-text transcription required, so it cannot convert audio recordings into text.

504.text:
Options B and E are correct because Amazon DynamoDB stores the employee data in a hierarchical structured relationship and delivers single-digit-millisecond, minimum-latency responses to high-traffic queries, with data exportable to Amazon S3 monthly. Configuring Amazon Macie for the account discovers sensitive financial information, and integrating Macie with Amazon EventBridge to send monthly notifications through an Amazon SNS subscription delivers the required email alerts when financial data is present.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Redshift is an analytical data warehouse optimized for complex queries, not low-latency, high-traffic transactional lookups of hierarchical data. Option C is incorrect because routing Macie events to AWS Lambda does not by itself send email notifications, so it would require additional custom code to alert users compared to the native SNS email subscription in Option E. Option D is incorrect because Athena and QuickSight provide analytics dashboards, not low-latency data storage or sensitive-data email alerting, so they do not satisfy the latency or notification requirements.

505.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon S3 hosts static website content cheaply and durably with no servers to manage, Amazon ECS with AWS Fargate runs the containerized logic tier serverlessly without provisioning or managing EC2 instances, and a managed Amazon RDS database handles the relational data with reduced operational effort. This combination simplifies deployment and minimizes operational costs across all three tiers.

Option B is incorrect because using ECS with Amazon EC2 requires the company to manage the underlying EC2 container instances (patching, scaling, capacity), increasing operational overhead compared to Fargate. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EKS introduces Kubernetes control plane charges and added management complexity that is unnecessary for this workload, raising costs versus ECS Fargate. Option D is incorrect because using EC2 Reserved Instances to host static content is wasteful and operationally heavy when S3 is purpose-built for static hosting, and EKS with EC2 adds further management burden and cost.

506.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Transit Gateway acts as a central hub that connects all VPCs within a Region through a single attachment per VPC, eliminating the mesh of point-to-point connections. Transit Gateway peering then connects these hubs across Regions, enabling full any-to-any VPC communication across all Regions with the least administrative effort as the number of VPCs and Regions grows.

Option A is incorrect because VPC peering is non-transitive and requires a separate connection between every pair of VPCs, creating an unmanageable full mesh that scales poorly with administrative effort. Option B is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect gateways are designed to connect on-premises networks to VPCs over dedicated links, not to interconnect VPCs to each other across Regions. Option D is incorrect because AWS PrivateLink exposes individual services via endpoints rather than providing full network-level VPC-to-VPC connectivity, so it cannot efficiently enable all VPCs to communicate with one another across Regions.

507.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network (CDN) that caches static content at edge locations close to users. This offloads requests from the EC2 instances behind the ALB, reducing the number of origin requests and the associated compute and data transfer costs while improving performance as traffic grows.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon ElastiCache is an in-memory data store for caching database query results and session data, not a service that serves static files or attaches to an ALB to cache web content. Option C is incorrect because AWS WAF is a web application firewall used to filter malicious traffic; it does not cache static files. Option D is incorrect because deploying a second ALB in another Region adds infrastructure cost and operational complexity, and cross-Region traffic routing for a single static workload does not reduce cost; it would likely increase data transfer charges rather than minimize them.

508.text:
Option A is correct because cost allocation tags must be activated from the AWS Organizations management (payer) account billing console, which controls consolidated billing for all member accounts. Because the department tag is created by the company on its own resources, it is a user-defined tag, so activating it there and creating a single Cost Explorer report grouped by the tag and filtered to EC2 gives organization-wide visibility into departmental spend most efficiently.

Option B is incorrect because AWS-defined (AWS-generated) cost allocation tags are automatically created by AWS (for example aws:createdBy) and cannot represent the company's custom department tag, which is user-defined. Options C and D are incorrect because cost allocation tags cannot be activated from a member account; only the management/payer account can activate them for the organization. Option D additionally compounds the error by treating the custom department tag as an AWS-defined tag.

509.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon RDS Custom for Oracle is a managed database service that still grants privileged operating-system and database access, allowing the company to install and configure the third-party features that require this access. It delivers RDS automation for backups and maintenance, removing the data center and administration burden the company wants to shed, making it the most cost-effective managed path.

Option A is incorrect because standard Amazon RDS for Oracle does not provide the privileged access the third-party features require, and rebuilding those features as separate cloud services adds significant migration effort and delay. Option C is incorrect because running Oracle on a self-managed EC2 AMI returns full backup, patching, and OS maintenance responsibility to the company, the exact overhead it is trying to eliminate. Option D is incorrect because rewriting the application to migrate to PostgreSQL is time-consuming and conflicts with the requirement to migrate quickly with limited resources.

510.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon CloudFront with an Amazon S3 origin serves and caches static web content at edge locations, offloading the requests that were triggering the Auto Scaling group to launch additional On-Demand Instances. Storing static assets in S3 and distributing them through CloudFront removes that compute load entirely, which is the most cost-effective redesign.

Option A is incorrect because Reserved Instances only discount baseline capacity through a commitment; they do not stop the group from scaling out under static-content load, so cost still grows. Option B is incorrect because Spot Instances are cheaper but can be interrupted with little notice, which is unsuitable for serving production web traffic and does not address the root cause of unnecessary scaling. Option D is incorrect because Lambda behind API Gateway is designed for serverless API/compute workloads, not for efficiently hosting and serving large volumes of static website content, and would require significant re-architecture.

511.text:
Option B is correct because AWS WAF associated with the ALB lets you define a rate-based rule that automatically blocks source IP addresses that exceed a request threshold within a time window. This mitigates high-volume request floods from changing IPs while allowing legitimate users below the threshold through, minimizing impact on real traffic.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Inspector performs vulnerability and configuration assessments of EC2 and container workloads; it does not filter or block inbound request traffic. Option C is incorrect because network ACLs are stateless, operate on static IP/port ranges, and cannot perform rate-based blocking, making them ineffective against attackers whose IP addresses constantly change. Option D is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat-detection service that analyzes logs and generates findings; it does not provide inline request rate-limiting or blocking capability.

512.text:
Option C is correct because it leverages Permission Sets, which are the native, scalable way to manage access in IAM Identity Center. By grouping users and assigning Permission Sets to those groups across multiple accounts, you centralize administration. When new employees join, you simply add them to the existing group, and they automatically inherit the correct permissions, minimizing operational overhead.

Options A and B are incorrect because they suggest managing policies at the user or account level, which is inefficient and difficult to maintain. Option D is a distractor because it suggests managing permissions per user rather than per group, which creates significant administrative burden and violates the principle of least privilege management at scale.

513.text:
Option C is correct because a service control policy (SCP) in AWS Organizations sets the maximum available permissions across member accounts and can use a Deny statement with conditions to block tag modification API actions for everyone except authorized principals. This enforces the restriction centrally and consistently across all accounts in the organization.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Config rules are detective controls that evaluate and report on resource compliance after the fact; they cannot prevent a tag from being modified. Option B is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail only records API activity for auditing; it provides no enforcement or prevention capability. Option D is incorrect because Amazon CloudWatch Logs is a logging and monitoring service and cannot block configuration changes such as tag modifications.

514.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon ElastiCache for Redis provides sorted set data structures that compute and rank scores in near-real time, making it ideal for a live top-10 leaderboard. Redis also supports persistence, so game state and current scores can be preserved and restored when a game is stopped and resumed, meeting both requirements.

Option A is incorrect because ElastiCache for Memcached is a simple key-value cache without sorted sets, ranking, or persistence, so it cannot natively compute a leaderboard or preserve scores across restarts. Option C is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront caches content at the edge with TTL-based expiration and is unsuitable for near-real-time, frequently changing scoreboard data. Option D is incorrect because an RDS for MySQL read replica adds query capacity but introduces replication lag and relies on relatively slow relational queries, which is not optimal for a near-real-time ranked scoreboard.

515.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server provides a fully managed native Windows file system accessible over the SMB protocol, which a Windows IIS application expects. Multi-AZ deployments replicate data synchronously across Availability Zones with automatic failover, delivering the resiliency and durability needed to replace the on-premises NAS file share.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon RDS is a relational database service, not a file share, and cannot host the SMB-accessible files the IIS application requires. Option B is incorrect because AWS Storage Gateway is designed to bridge on-premises environments to AWS storage; it is not the primary durable file system for instances running entirely in AWS. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EFS exposes files over NFS for Linux workloads and does not natively support the Windows SMB protocol and file semantics the IIS application depends on.

516.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for containers that requires no server provisioning, patching, or scaling. Because the job's CPU and memory needs are known in advance and it can run up to an hour, a scheduled EventBridge event launching a Fargate ECS task right-sizes the container and runs the job with minimal operational effort and no idle infrastructure.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Lambda has a maximum execution timeout of 15 minutes, so it cannot run a job that may take up to an hour. Option B is incorrect for the same Lambda timeout reason, and adding API Gateway introduces unnecessary complexity. Option D is incorrect because the EC2 launch type requires the company to manage, patch, and scale the underlying EC2 instances in the Auto Scaling group, adding operational overhead that Fargate eliminates.

517.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon RDS read replicas asynchronously replicate data from the primary DB instance and serve read-only traffic. Directing the business reporting queries to a read replica isolates that read workload from the production instance, so reporting does not impact write operations on the primary.

Option B is incorrect because you cannot scale a relational RDS instance horizontally by placing it behind an Elastic Load Balancer; ELBs distribute traffic to compute targets, not database engines, and this does not separate read from write load. Option C is incorrect because scaling up to a larger instance type still runs reporting queries and writes on the same instance, so contention between them remains. Option D is incorrect because Multi-AZ provides high availability through a standby that is not readable for application queries; it does not offload reporting reads from the primary.

518.text:
Option A is correct because at 15 Mbps capped at 70% utilization, the effective throughput is about 10.5 Mbps, which would take far longer than 30 days to transfer 20 TB over the network. AWS Snowball is a physical data transport device that bypasses the limited network entirely, allowing the company to bulk-ship the data and meet the deadline.

Option B is incorrect because AWS DataSync transfers data over the existing network connection, so it is still bound by the same constrained 10.5 Mbps effective bandwidth and cannot meet the timeline. Option C is incorrect because a secure VPN also runs over the same limited internet link and faces the identical throughput bottleneck. Option D is incorrect because Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration optimizes uploads over the public internet via edge locations but is still fundamentally limited by the origin's 15 Mbps bandwidth, so it cannot move 20 TB in time.

519.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon Inspector continuously scans EC2 instances for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure, satisfying the regular security scanning requirement. AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager automates patching on a defined schedule using patch baselines and maintenance windows and reports each instance's patch compliance status, meeting the scheduled patching and reporting requirements.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Macie discovers and protects sensitive data in Amazon S3, not software vulnerabilities on EC2, and per-instance cron jobs do not scale or provide centralized patch reporting. Option B is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat-detection service that analyzes logs rather than scanning instances for software vulnerabilities, and Session Manager provides shell access, not scheduled patching. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Detective investigates and visualizes security findings rather than scanning for vulnerabilities, and a plain EventBridge rule does not perform managed patching or compliance reporting.

520.text:
Option D is correct because adding a bucket policy that denies any PutObject request lacking the x-amz-server-side-encryption header forces every upload to specify server-side encryption. Objects uploaded without requesting encryption are rejected, guaranteeing that all stored objects are encrypted at rest.

Option A is incorrect because the s3:x-amz-acl header controls the object's access control list (permissions), not encryption, so requiring its presence does nothing to enforce encryption. Option B is incorrect for the same reason; setting the ACL to private restricts access but does not encrypt object data. Option C is incorrect because aws:SecureTransport enforces encryption in transit (requiring HTTPS/TLS for the connection) rather than encryption at rest, so objects could still be stored unencrypted.

521.text:
Options A and C are correct because migrating the web tier to EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group behind an Application Load Balancer preserves the existing application with minimal changes while adding elasticity and removing single instances as points of failure. Migrating the single-node MySQL database to an Amazon RDS Multi-AZ deployment keeps MySQL compatibility (no application rewrite) while providing a synchronously replicated standby with automatic failover for resiliency.

Option B is incorrect because running the database on self-managed EC2 instances behind a Network Load Balancer does not provide proper database replication or failover and adds heavy operational overhead. Option D is incorrect because re-architecting the web tier into AWS Lambda is a significant change that conflicts with the requirement to minimize application changes. Option E is incorrect because migrating to Amazon DynamoDB requires rewriting the application's relational data model and queries, a major modification that breaks MySQL compatibility.

522.text:
Option B is correct because the AWS KMS external key store (XKS) lets you use AWS KMS while the actual cryptographic key material is stored and used in an external key manager that you operate outside AWS. KMS forwards encrypt and decrypt operations to the external key manager, satisfying the regulatory requirement to retain keys outside the cloud while supporting external key managers from various vendors with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because an AWS CloudHSM key store keeps key material in a CloudHSM cluster inside AWS, not in an on-premises external key manager. Option C is incorrect because the default AWS-managed KMS key store stores keys within AWS, violating the requirement that keys be retained outside the cloud. Option D is incorrect because a custom key store backed by CloudHSM still resides in AWS and does not integrate with third-party external key managers.

523.text:
Option C is correct because configuring cross-account access using an S3 bucket policy (or bucket policy combined with IAM roles) grants each agency's own AWS account read access to the data in the source account. The company retains the single source-of-truth bucket, avoids managing individual users for external analysts, and can scope and revoke access per account, maximizing security and operational efficiency.

Option A is incorrect because S3 does not offer global tables (that is a DynamoDB feature), and replicating copies to each agency multiplies storage cost and weakens security control over the data. Option B is incorrect because making the bucket public exposes the data to anyone on the internet, an unacceptable security risk. Option D is incorrect because creating an IAM user for every analyst in the source account is operationally burdensome to provision and rotate and forces external personnel into the company's account, reducing security and efficiency.

524.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Global Accelerator supports UDP listeners and routes traffic over the AWS global network backbone from the nearest edge location to endpoint groups in each Region. This minimizes latency and packet loss for the UDP-based game and provides static anycast IP addresses, delivering the high-quality, low-latency experience required across the eight Regions.

Option A is incorrect because transit gateways with inter-Region peering connect VPC networks for routing between Regions; they do not optimize end-user internet latency or accelerate client traffic. Option C is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is an HTTP/HTTPS content delivery network and does not support arbitrary UDP game traffic. Option D is incorrect because VPC peering connects private networks between VPCs and provides no acceleration of end-user traffic, and you cannot simply 'turn on UDP' on a peering connection to improve gaming latency.

525.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon Aurora Serverless v2 automatically and rapidly scales database capacity up and down in fine-grained increments to match unpredictable load, then scales back when demand drops. This handles the sudden monthly sales spikes without provisioning for peak, so the company pays only for the capacity it uses, making it the most cost-effective solution while maintaining performance.

Option B is incorrect because a self-managed PostgreSQL database on a single EC2 instance has no native database auto scaling, and adding more instances would not transparently scale a single-node database. Option C is incorrect because migrating to a larger fixed RDS instance forces the company to provision and pay for peak capacity continuously, which is not cost-effective for sporadic, unpredictable spikes. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Redshift is a data warehouse for analytics workloads, not a transactional database, and is unsuitable for an ecommerce application's OLTP traffic.

526.text:
Option A is correct because the Instance Scheduler on AWS is an AWS solution that automatically starts and stops resources, including RDS instances, on defined schedules. Configuring it to stop the database outside weekday business hours stops billing for instance compute during idle periods, optimizing cost with minimal ongoing operational effort.

Option B is incorrect because turning off automatic backups and taking manual snapshots does not stop instance compute charges (the instance still runs) and weakens data protection, so it neither optimizes cost meaningfully nor reduces overhead. Option C is incorrect because building and maintaining a custom Lambda function based on CPU utilization adds operational overhead and is a less reliable trigger than a fixed business-hours schedule. Option D is incorrect because All Upfront Reserved Instances commit to and pay for capacity running continuously, which does not save money when the goal is to stop the database during off-hours.

527.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EFS is a fully managed, highly available, and elastically scalable file system with no minimum size that grows and shrinks automatically. Creating multiple mount targets (one per Availability Zone) allows many Linux EC2 instances to mount it concurrently over NFS, and through the Site-to-Site VPN on-premises Linux clients can also mount it using the same native NFS protocol.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server primarily serves Windows SMB workloads rather than Linux instances using native NFS, and is less aligned with the elastic, no-minimum-size requirement here. Option B is incorrect because EBS Multi-Attach is a block storage volume shared among a limited set of instances in a single Availability Zone, not a scalable shared file system mountable from many instances and on premises. Option D is incorrect because a single mount target confines NFS access to one Availability Zone, reducing availability for instances spread across multiple AZs; multiple mount targets are needed.

528.text:
Options A and D are correct because AWS DataSync preserves NTFS file metadata, ACLs, and permissions when transferring data to Amazon FSx for Windows File Server. Deploying DataSync agents on premises (Option A) transfers data directly over the network, while ordering an AWS Snowcone device that runs DataSync agents (Option D) handles environments with limited bandwidth; both deliver the data to FSx through DataSync, preserving file permissions.

Option B is incorrect because copying shares into S3 with the AWS CLI does not retain Windows NTFS permissions, so access rights would be lost. Option C is incorrect for the same reason; shipping drives for import into S3 loses the NTFS ACLs that must be preserved. Option E is incorrect because copying data to a Snowball Edge with the AWS CLI and importing into S3 also strips the Windows file permissions, failing the requirement to maintain access rights.

529.text:
Option C is correct because MySQL-compatible Amazon Aurora Serverless automatically scales database capacity up and down to match the application's sporadic and unpredictable usage and scales down during quiet periods, so the company pays only for the capacity consumed. Because it is MySQL-compatible, the existing application requires no database modifications, meeting both the cost-effectiveness and compatibility requirements.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon DynamoDB is a NoSQL database that would require rewriting the application's relational MySQL data model and queries.
