The company then uses temporary credentials via AWS STS to assume that role, so no long-term credentials are shared or stored, and each customer retains full control over the granted permissions. This follows least privilege and is the most secure approach.

Option B is incorrect because building a custom token vending machine reinvents the assume-role mechanism that IAM roles and STS already provide securely, adding unnecessary complexity. Option C is incorrect because creating IAM users and storing their long-term access and secret keys is insecure; static credentials can be leaked and require rotation, which roles avoid. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Cognito is intended for application end-user authentication, not for granting another AWS account programmatic access, and storing a Cognito user password is likewise insecure compared to role assumption.

603.text:
Option B is correct because encryption at rest for Amazon EBS is enabled at the volume level. Creating the EBS volumes as encrypted and attaching them to the EC2 instances ensures that all data written to the volumes, along with snapshots and data in transit between the volume and the instance, is automatically encrypted using AWS KMS keys. This is the direct, supported mechanism for guaranteeing EBS encryption at rest.

Option A is incorrect because IAM roles grant permissions to make API calls; they do not configure or enforce encryption on EBS volumes, so attaching a role does not encrypt data. Option C is incorrect because EC2 instance tags are metadata labels with no inherent ability to enable encryption; a tag named Encrypt does nothing on its own. Option D is incorrect because a KMS key policy controls who can use a key, not whether EBS volumes are encrypted; while account-level default encryption can be enabled in EC2 settings, a KMS key policy by itself does not enforce that volumes are created encrypted, so explicitly creating encrypted volumes is the reliable solution.

604.text:
Option B is correct because cross-account access is achieved by establishing a trust relationship. By adding the development account as a principal in the trust policy of the role in the production account, the existing development account IAM users can assume that role and gain exactly the S3 permissions the role grants. This lets team members work across both accounts using their current identities while limiting production access to only the bucket permissions defined in the role, complying with least privilege.

Option A is incorrect because attaching the AdministratorAccess policy grants full, unrestricted access to all services and resources, which grossly violates the principle of least privilege. Option C is incorrect because turning off S3 Block Public Access exposes the bucket to the public internet, creating a serious security risk and not providing controlled cross-account team access. Option D is incorrect because creating duplicate IAM users with separate credentials in the production account increases credential sprawl and management overhead, contradicting the more secure and maintainable role-assumption approach.

605.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EBS Multi-Attach is supported only on Provisioned IOPS SSD volumes (io1 and io2). Multi-Attach lets a single EBS volume attach to up to 16 Nitro-based instances in the same Availability Zone simultaneously, allowing each instance to read and write to the volume concurrently. This delivers the shared-write, higher-availability design the company needs for clustered applications, and io2 also provides high durability and consistent low-latency performance.

Options A and D are incorrect because General Purpose SSD volumes (gp3 and gp2) do not support EBS Multi-Attach; only Provisioned IOPS SSD volumes can be attached to multiple instances at once. Option B is incorrect because Throughput Optimized HDD (st1) is a low-cost magnetic volume designed for large sequential streaming workloads and likewise does not support Multi-Attach. Choosing any of these would prevent the simultaneous multi-instance write capability that the requirement explicitly demands, so io2 is the only valid option.

606.text:
Option A is correct because converting an existing Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL Single-AZ instance to a Multi-AZ deployment is a simple modification that RDS performs in place. Multi-AZ maintains a synchronous standby replica in a separate Availability Zone and automatically fails over to it during an outage, eliminating the single point of failure and minimizing downtime. Because RDS keeps the same endpoint, no application code changes are required, satisfying every stated requirement with the least effort.

Option B is unnecessarily complex and disruptive; restoring from a snapshot creates a new endpoint and risks data loss for transactions made after the snapshot, when a direct modification achieves the same result. Option C is incorrect because read replicas serve read scaling, not automatic failover, and weighted Route 53 records would direct writes to a read-only replica, breaking the application. Option D is incorrect because RDS is a managed service that cannot be placed in an EC2 Auto Scaling group, making this approach technically infeasible.

607.text:
Option C is correct because enabling Amazon DynamoDB Streams captures item-level changes, and a trigger (an AWS Lambda function) can react to each new weather event and publish a message to a single Amazon SNS topic. All four internal teams subscribe to that topic and receive the alert via fan-out. This is fully managed, event-driven, and decoupled from the main application, so it adds the alerting feature with the least operational overhead and no impact on the existing application's performance.

Option A is incorrect because DynamoDB transactions provide atomic writes; they cannot send notifications. Option B is incorrect because it requires modifying the current application to publish to four separate topics, increasing coupling and operational overhead, and it risks affecting application performance. Option D is incorrect because a cron job that scans the table every minute consumes read capacity, adds latency, increases overhead, and degrades the current application's performance compared to the native, push-based Streams approach.

608.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon Transcribe converts speech in the audio files to text and natively supports PII redaction, which automatically identifies and masks personally identifiable information such as names, addresses, and credit card numbers within the transcript. Invoking an AWS Lambda function on S3 upload to start the transcription job, and writing the redacted output to a separate bucket, delivers an automated, managed pipeline that meets both the transcription and PII-removal requirements without custom pattern-matching logic.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon Kinesis Video Streams ingests live video/audio streams and does not transcribe stored files, and hand-rolled Lambda PII scanning is error-prone. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Textract extracts text from documents and images, not audio. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Connect is a contact center service for live customer interactions, not a batch transcription tool for stored recordings, and building a contact flow with custom PII scanning adds significant unnecessary complexity compared with Transcribe's built-in redaction.

609.text:
Options B, C, and F are correct because under the AWS shared responsibility model the customer manages tasks within the managed service boundary. The company creates the RDS DB instance and configures its maintenance window (B), installs and configures additional software on ECS for monitoring, patching, log management, and host intrusion detection since the customer controls the container workloads (C), and is responsible for encrypting data in transit, including over Direct Connect, because AWS does not encrypt customer traffic by default (F).

Options A, D, and E are incorrect because they fall to AWS as the service provider. AWS manages the RDS infrastructure layer, underlying operating system, and platform (A) and installs database engine patches for RDS during maintenance windows (D). AWS is also solely responsible for the physical security of its data centers and infrastructure (E). These responsibilities lie with AWS under the responsibility of the cloud, not the customer.

610.text:
Option B is correct because attaching an IAM role to the Lambda function and granting that role an IAM policy scoped to read access for the specific S3 bucket follows the principle of least privilege. The function assumes the role and obtains temporary, automatically rotated credentials through the AWS SDK, so no long-lived secrets are stored. This is the most secure pattern for granting an AWS service access to another AWS resource within the same account.

Option C is incorrect and insecure because embedding access and secret keys in code exposes long-lived static credentials that can be leaked and must be manually rotated. Option D is incorrect because granting read access to all S3 buckets in the account violates least privilege by over-provisioning permissions. Option A alone is insufficient and less secure than an identity-based role; a bucket policy grants access by principal but does not provide the Lambda execution identity, and the role-based approach is the AWS-recommended best practice for Lambda.

611.text:
Option A is correct because configuring a TLS listener and deploying the server certificate on the Network Load Balancer terminates TLS at the load balancer, ensuring that sensor data sent from users' devices is encrypted in transit over the network. The NLB natively supports TLS listeners, so adding the certificate establishes encrypted client connections without re-architecting the application, directly addressing the goal of securing data in transit.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Shield Advanced and AWS WAF protect against DDoS attacks and web exploits respectively; they do not encrypt traffic. Option C is incorrect because switching to an Application Load Balancer with AWS WAF adds application-layer filtering but does not by itself address encryption of data in transit, and WAF is not directly supported on an NLB. Option D is incorrect because encrypting EBS volumes with AWS KMS protects data at rest on the instances, not data moving over the network, so it does not satisfy the in-transit security requirement.

612.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Global Accelerator is a supported protected resource type in AWS Shield Advanced. Subscribing to Shield Advanced and adding the standard accelerator as a protected resource provides enhanced DDoS protection, near-real-time attack visibility, access to the Shield Response Team, and DDoS cost protection at the accelerator's static anycast edge, which is the entry point for the self-managed DNS solution.

Option B is incorrect because EC2 instances are not protected directly by Shield Advanced in this design; the accelerator is the public-facing entry point, and Shield Advanced protects the accelerator rather than the backend instances. Options C and D are incorrect because AWS WAF protects HTTP/HTTPS web applications fronted by CloudFront, an Application Load Balancer, or API Gateway; WAF cannot be associated with a Global Accelerator or directly with EC2 instances, and a rate-based rule is geared toward web request flooding, not the broad network-layer DDoS protection that Shield Advanced provides.

613.text:
Option B is correct because configuring provisioned concurrency for the Lambda function keeps a requested number of execution environments initialized and ready to respond. This eliminates cold-start latency caused by the function loading many libraries and establishing the RDS connection, so the first invocation responds as quickly as warm ones. It is a simple configuration change that lowers response latency for all users with minimal impact on the company's operations.

Option A is incorrect because connecting the frontend directly to the database bypasses the API Gateway and Lambda business logic, undermining security and architecture rather than tuning latency. Option C is incorrect because caching query results in Amazon S3 is not designed for low-latency request caching and would require significant application changes without resolving the cold-start initialization delay. Option D is incorrect because increasing database size does not address Lambda cold starts or library loading time, and Lambda connection limits are better handled by RDS Proxy than by oversizing the database.

614.text:
Option B is correct because an interface VPC endpoint, powered by AWS PrivateLink, creates a private entry point with an elastic network interface inside the VPC for the private API Gateway. Routing the CheckFunds private REST API through an interface endpoint keeps traffic between the BuyStock and CheckFunds APIs entirely within the AWS network instead of traversing the public internet, satisfying the requirement with no application code changes.

Option A is incorrect because adding an X-API-Key header concerns authorization and usage plans, not the network path the traffic takes. Option C is incorrect because gateway endpoints support only Amazon S3 and DynamoDB; API Gateway is not accessible through a gateway endpoint, so it cannot provide private connectivity here. Option D is incorrect because inserting an Amazon SQS queue between the two APIs would require rearchitecting the synchronous funds-check call into an asynchronous pattern, a major code change that does not address the underlying private connectivity requirement.

615.text:
Option B is correct because EC2 Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks pause instances in a wait state during the launch and terminate transitions, giving a custom script time to run and send inventory and configuration reports to the auditing system before the instance becomes fully in service or is fully removed. This guarantees reports are captured at both launch and termination events, achieving the goal reliably and efficiently within the Auto Scaling workflow.

Option A is incorrect because a scheduled Lambda function runs at fixed intervals and would miss the precise launch and termination moments, and remotely scripting all instances adds overhead. Option C is incorrect because user data scripts run only at first launch and cannot reliably execute during termination, so termination reporting would be missed. Option D is incorrect because the Auto Scaling group does not natively invoke arbitrary OS scripts on start and terminate without lifecycle hooks; relying on the instance OS alone does not guarantee the termination report is sent before the instance is gone.

616.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Batch on Amazon EC2 is purpose-built to run batch computing jobs and can provision compute environments using large EC2 instance types that match the 64 vCPU and 512 GiB on-premises server, ensuring the CPU-intensive job completes within 15 minutes. AWS Batch handles job queuing, scheduling, and instance provisioning automatically, providing the needed capacity with low operational overhead for a recurring hourly batch workload.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Lambda has a 15-minute maximum execution timeout and limited CPU/memory, leaving no margin for a job that already averages 15 minutes on a 64 vCPU server, risking timeout. Option B is workable but adds more cluster and task definition management overhead than AWS Batch for pure batch scheduling. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Lightsail offers simplified, limited compute that cannot match a 64 vCPU, 512 GiB workload, and is not designed for high-performance batch processing, making it unsuitable for this CPU-intensive requirement.

617.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon RDS Proxy is a fully managed database proxy that pools and shares established database connections. Serverless applications like Lambda can open many short-lived connections that overwhelm RDS for MySQL during random traffic spikes, causing connection rejection errors. RDS Proxy manages and reuses connections so the database is not exhausted, resolving the errors with minimal configuration and the least operational overhead because it is a managed service.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached caches query results to offload reads but does not manage or pool database connections, so connection exhaustion would persist. Option C is incorrect because moving to a larger instance class adds I/O capacity but does not directly solve connection pooling, and it adds cost and migration effort. Option D is incorrect because Multi-AZ provides high availability through a standby replica, not additional connection capacity, and having applications switch between instances does not address connection rejection during demand spikes.

618.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Lake Formation provides centralized, fine-grained data governance, including column-level access control, over data in the Amazon S3 data lake. Using a Lake Formation blueprint to ingest the Aurora MySQL data into the S3 data lake lets Lake Formation enforce column-level permissions so the marketing team sees only the permitted subset of columns. Querying through Amazon Athena as the QuickSight data source honors those Lake Formation permissions, meeting the requirement with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon EMR adds cluster management overhead and does not natively enforce Lake Formation column-level authorization. Option B is incorrect because IAM policies attached to QuickSight users cannot enforce column-level access on S3 objects, which are file-level. Option C is incorrect because AWS Glue Elastic Views is deprecated/unavailable and S3 bucket policies operate at the object level, not the column level, so neither can deliver the required column-level authorization.

619.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Step Functions is a serverless orchestration service designed to combine multiple AWS Lambda functions into responsive applications using state machine workflows. It natively supports manual approval steps through callback patterns with task tokens, and it can coordinate work across services running on Amazon EC2 instances, containers, and on-premises servers via activities and service integrations. This delivers the required order-processing orchestration with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Glue is an ETL service for data preparation and transformation, not a general workflow orchestrator with manual approval steps. Option C is incorrect because Amazon SQS provides message queuing and decoupling but lacks built-in workflow state management, branching, and manual approval orchestration, requiring significant custom code. Option D is incorrect because while Lambda and EventBridge can build event-driven applications, coordinating a multi-step workflow with manual approvals and human-in-the-loop logic would require substantial custom orchestration code, far more overhead than Step Functions provides natively.

620.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon RDS integrates with AWS Key Management Service to provide encryption at rest. By creating a KMS key and enabling encryption when launching or modifying the DB instance, RDS automatically encrypts the underlying storage, automated backups, read replicas, and snapshots using the specified key. This is the AWS-native, managed mechanism for satisfying the encrypt-data-at-rest requirement with no custom key handling.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Secrets Manager stores and rotates secrets such as database credentials; it does not supply encryption keys for RDS storage encryption, which must come from KMS. Options C and D are incorrect because certificates from AWS Certificate Manager or IAM are used to enable SSL/TLS, which protects data in transit between clients and the database, not data at rest. The requirement is specifically encryption at rest, so KMS-backed RDS encryption is the correct solution.

621.text:
Option A is correct because AWS WAF is a web application firewall that inspects incoming HTTP/HTTPS requests and can block common exploits, including SQL injection, using managed and custom rule groups. Deploying AWS WAF in front of the Application Load Balancer and associating the appropriate web ACLs filters malicious requests before they reach the EC2 instances and the Aurora database, directly remediating the SQL injection vulnerability.

Option B is incorrect because an ALB listener rule routes traffic based on conditions like path or host; it cannot inspect request payloads for SQL injection signatures, so it cannot reliably detect or block such attacks. Option C is incorrect because AWS Shield Advanced provides DDoS protection, not application-layer SQL injection filtering. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Inspector is an automated vulnerability assessment service that scans for software vulnerabilities and exposure; it reports findings but does not actively block live SQL injection attempts against the application.

622.text:
Options A and D are correct because AWS CloudTrail and AWS Config together provide the auditing and change-tracking the company needs. CloudTrail records API calls, capturing who launched oversized EC2 instances and who modified security group rules, along with when and from where, giving a complete audit trail of actions. AWS Config records configuration state over time and evaluates resources against rules, so it can track inventory and configuration changes and flag noncompliance, such as unauthorized security group modifications.

Option B is incorrect because data lifecycle policies automate EBS snapshot creation and retention, unrelated to auditing changes. Option C is incorrect because AWS Trusted Advisor offers best-practice recommendations across cost, security, and performance but does not provide detailed change auditing or a historical record of configuration changes. Option E is incorrect because using a CloudFormation template to restore previous configurations is a remediation action, not a mechanism to track and audit the inventory and configuration changes that the requirement asks for.

623.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Transfer Family provides a fully managed SFTP endpoint that stores and retrieves files directly in Amazon S3. It supports integrated Microsoft Active Directory authentication, allowing the customer to use existing on-premises AD credentials. Because the service speaks standard SFTP, the customer's existing SFTP client works unchanged, meeting the requirements with the least operational overhead and no application changes.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Database Migration Service migrates databases, not file downloads over SFTP, and does not provide an SFTP interface to S3. Option C is incorrect because AWS DataSync is a data transfer service for bulk synchronization between storage systems; it does not present an SFTP endpoint for a client to download files and is not authenticated via the customer's SFTP workflow. Option D is incorrect because building and managing a Windows EC2 instance with an SFTP server introduces significant operational overhead for patching, scaling, and availability compared with the managed Transfer Family service.

624.text:
Options A and C are correct because they address the two distinct requirements. Modifying the Single-AZ instance to a Multi-AZ deployment (A) provides high availability and automatic recovery by maintaining a synchronous standby in another Availability Zone that RDS fails over to automatically. Creating a read replica in a different Availability Zone and directing all reporting queries to it (C) offloads the resource-intensive report process from the primary, so transactions no longer slow down when reports run, improving report and transaction performance.

Option B is incorrect because restoring a snapshot to a new deployment creates a static, separate database that is not automatically maintained or failed over, providing neither continuous high availability nor offloaded live reporting. Option D is incorrect because RDS Custom targets workloads needing OS and database customization and does not inherently provide HA or report offloading here. Option E is incorrect because RDS Proxy manages connection pooling and cannot restrict reporting to a maintenance window, so it does not solve the performance contention.

625.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Glue is a serverless ETL service that handles the transformation of CSV data into a format compatible with Amazon Redshift with minimal management. It integrates natively with S3 and Redshift, requiring no infrastructure maintenance.

Option B is a common distractor because it requires managing EC2 instances, which increases operational overhead (patching, scaling, and monitoring). Option C is incorrect because the COTS application specifically requires data in Redshift or S3, not DynamoDB. Option D is incorrect because Amazon EMR requires managing cluster configurations and scaling, which is significantly more complex and operationally intensive than using the serverless AWS Glue service.

626.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Aurora Serverless for MySQL automatically starts, stops, and scales database capacity based on demand without requiring the administrator to select or manage a specific instance type. This fits the infrequent, unpredictable access pattern, avoids paying for idle capacity, and seamlessly scales as the user base grows in the future. It also provides high availability with minimal downtime, satisfying all stated requirements.

Option A is incorrect because provisioned Amazon Aurora MySQL requires choosing and managing an instance class, which the administrator explicitly wants to avoid. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Redshift Spectrum queries data in S3 for data warehousing analytics and is not a transactional MySQL-compatible relational database for this application. Option D is incorrect because Amazon RDS for MySQL also requires selecting a specific DB instance type and managing capacity, conflicting with the requirement to migrate without picking an instance type while anticipating future growth.

627.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Backup is a fully managed, centralized backup service. Creating a backup plan that performs nightly backups and copies them to another Region, then assigning the application's EC2 instances as resources, captures both the instance configuration (via AMI/image-based backups) and all attached EBS data volumes automatically. Adding EC2 instances as resources ensures the full instance, including its configuration and every data volume, is backed up and made recoverable cross-Region, which is the most operationally efficient approach.

Option A is incorrect because a custom Lambda function to schedule and copy snapshots reintroduces manual code and scheduling overhead that AWS Backup eliminates. Option C is incorrect because adding only the EBS volumes as resources backs up data but not the EC2 instance configuration, failing the requirement to back up the instance configuration. Option D is incorrect because it both relies on custom Lambda code and copies snapshots only to another Availability Zone, not another Region, so it does not meet the cross-Region recovery requirement.

628.text:
Option B is correct because deploying an Amazon CloudFront distribution with the existing S3 bucket as the origin caches the large dataset files at edge locations close to customers in North America and Europe, improving download performance. CloudFront data transfer is less expensive than transferring directly out of S3, reducing data transfer costs. Switching to CloudFront signed URLs preserves the per-purchase access control the company already relies on, satisfying both the cost and performance goals.

Option A is incorrect because S3 Transfer Acceleration optimizes uploads to S3 over long distances and adds a per-GB acceleration fee, so it does not reduce download costs. Option C is incorrect because Cross-Region Replication duplicates storage, increasing storage and replication costs, and adds operational complexity without edge caching. Option D is incorrect because re-architecting the web application to stream datasets and implement custom access control adds significant development effort and ongoing data transfer costs from S3, without the cost-saving edge caching that CloudFront provides.

629.text:
Option A is correct because launching all EC2 instances in the same Availability Zone and using a cluster placement group packs instances close together on the same high-bisection-bandwidth network segment. This delivers the low network latency and high throughput required by an in-memory database handling more than 100,000 transactions per minute. Keeping instances in one Availability Zone also avoids cross-AZ data transfer charges, providing the most cost-effective network design for a latency-sensitive workload.

Option B is incorrect because spreading instances across different Availability Zones with a partition placement group increases latency and incurs inter-AZ data transfer charges, conflicting with the low-latency and cost-minimization goals. Options C and D are incorrect because deploying Auto Scaling groups across multiple Availability Zones likewise introduces cross-AZ network latency and data transfer costs, and Auto Scaling addresses capacity scaling rather than the tightly coupled, high-throughput, low-latency networking that a cluster placement group provides.

630.text:
Option B is correct because referencing the ALB's security group as the source in the EC2 instances' security group creates a chained, identity-based rule that allows traffic only from instances associated with that ALB security group. Because security groups are stateful and reference each other by ID rather than by IP, this guarantees that the EC2 instances accept inbound traffic exclusively from the load balancer and reject all other sources inside or outside the private subnet, regardless of how IP ranges change.

Option A is incorrect because you cannot route internet traffic directly to private subnet instances, and adding such a route would expose the instances rather than restrict access. Option C is incorrect because moving the instances to a public subnet and attaching Elastic IP addresses makes them directly reachable from the internet, which is the opposite of the security requirement. Option D is incorrect because allowing any TCP traffic on any port to the ALB broadens exposure and does nothing to restrict which sources can reach the EC2 instances.

631.text:
Options A and B are correct because both pair a real-time streaming ingest service with an in-flight transformation step and land data in S3 that can be queried with SQL using Amazon Athena. In Option A, Kinesis Data Streams ingests, Kinesis Data Analytics transforms the streaming records, and Kinesis Data Firehose delivers to S3. In Option B, Amazon MSK streams the data and AWS Glue performs ETL and writes transformed output to S3. Athena then runs standard SQL directly over the S3 data with no servers to manage.

Option C is incorrect because AWS DMS is for database migration and replication, not for ingesting real-time streaming data from arbitrary sources. Options D and E are both incorrect because they end with the Amazon RDS query editor querying data in S3, which is not possible; RDS queries its own engine storage, not S3 objects. Athena is the correct tool for SQL over S3, making D and E invalid despite their otherwise plausible streaming components.

632.text:
Option C is correct because the slowdown occurs at a known, predictable time, the first of every month at midnight, when the batch job spikes CPU to 100%. A scheduled scaling policy lets the Auto Scaling group add capacity in advance based on the calendar, so additional instances are already running and warmed up before the batch begins. This proactively absorbs the surge and avoids the downtime that reactive scaling would incur.

Option A is incorrect because CloudFront caches and accelerates content delivery but does nothing to add backend compute capacity for a CPU-bound batch calculation. Option B is incorrect because simple scaling based on CPU is reactive; it only reacts after utilization is already at 100%, leaving a lag during which the application is disrupted. Option D is incorrect because ElastiCache offloads read-heavy database access and does not relieve a CPU-intensive financial computation running on the instances, so it would not prevent the periodic spike.

633.text:
Option B is correct because a fully serverless API Gateway plus Lambda backend that calls Amazon SES is the most cost-effective design for a low-volume workload of fewer than 100 requests per month. You pay only per request and per millisecond of Lambda execution, with no idle infrastructure, and SES sends the contact email cheaply. This eliminates any always-on compute charges while providing the dynamic server-side processing the form needs.

Option A is incorrect because running the form on ECS requires continuously running container infrastructure, incurring costs even when idle, which is wasteful for a handful of monthly visits. Option C is incorrect because Lightsail provisions an always-on instance and WorkMail charges per mailbox, adding fixed monthly costs that are excessive for this tiny workload. Option D is incorrect because a t2.micro EC2 instance running a LAMP stack runs continuously and must be patched and managed, producing both higher cost and more operational overhead than the serverless approach.

634.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon FSx for NetApp ONTAP provides multiprotocol access, exposing the same data over both NFS for the Linux simulation application and SMB for the Windows visualization application simultaneously. This eliminates the duplicated, synchronized file systems and the inefficient resource usage, and it requires no code changes because each application keeps using its native protocol against a single shared dataset on EC2.

Option A is incorrect because migrating to Lambda would require rewriting the applications and S3 is object storage, not an NFS or SMB file system, so it would mandate code changes. Option B is incorrect because FSx File Gateway is intended for on-premises caching of FSx for Windows file shares, not for providing simultaneous NFS and SMB access to cloud applications. Option C is incorrect because SQS is a message queue, not shared file storage, and the applications need a file system rather than a messaging layer, which would require significant code changes.

635.text:
Option B is correct because an Amazon Aurora global database replicates to the secondary Region with typical latency under one second using dedicated, low-overhead storage-level replication, keeping the DR database current with the least latency. A warm standby keeps a scaled-down but fully functional copy of the infrastructure already running, which can scale up quickly, delivering the lowest recovery time objective because services are live rather than needing to be provisioned at failover.

Option A is incorrect because a pilot light keeps only core elements (such as the database) running while application tier resources are switched off, so they must be started and scaled at failover, increasing RTO compared to warm standby. Options C and D are incorrect because an RDS Multi-AZ instance provides high availability within a single Region, not cross-Region disaster recovery; it does not natively replicate to a different Region with the low latency and global reach that Aurora global databases provide.

636.text:
Option B is correct because an API Gateway HTTPS endpoint invoking a Lambda function and storing results in DynamoDB is a fully managed, highly available, serverless architecture that scales automatically with badge scan volume. API Gateway natively terminates HTTPS, Lambda processes each message without managing servers, and DynamoDB durably stores the access records across multiple Availability Zones, making the data readily available for the security team to query and analyze.

Option A is incorrect because a single EC2 instance is a single point of failure and is not highly available without additional load balancing and scaling, increasing operational overhead. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Route 53 is a DNS service and cannot directly route HTTPS messages to a Lambda function; it lacks an HTTP request handling capability. Option D is incorrect because a Site-to-Site VPN and S3 gateway endpoint are designed for private network connectivity, not for receiving HTTPS messages from distributed badge readers, and add unnecessary network complexity.

637.text:
Option C is correct because creating a read replica of the primary RDS database offloads the read-only analytical queries to a separate instance, relieving the primary of the additional read load and restoring application performance. This requires minimal changes since business analysts simply point their queries at the read replica endpoint while the existing application continues using the primary, and RDS manages the asynchronous replication automatically.

Option A is incorrect because exporting data to DynamoDB changes the data model to NoSQL and would require rewriting the analysts' SQL queries, which is a significant change. Option B is incorrect because ElastiCache is an in-memory cache for frequently accessed key lookups, not a general-purpose engine for running ad hoc relational SQL queries from analysts. Option D is incorrect because copying data into Amazon Redshift introduces a separate data warehouse with ongoing ETL and data synchronization to maintain, which is more complex than simply adding a read replica for the existing workload.

638.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Organizations with a service control policy lets you centrally enforce a guardrail across all the development accounts in an organizational unit with the least development effort. An SCP using a condition on ec2:InstanceType can deny launching disallowed instance types organization-wide, applying to all current and future users in those accounts without writing any custom code or maintaining provisioning workflows.

Option A is incorrect because building and maintaining Systems Manager templates for an approved creation process is a workaround that does not prevent users from launching instances through other means, and it requires ongoing development. Option C is incorrect because an EventBridge rule plus Lambda is a reactive, custom-coded approach that stops instances only after creation, leaving a cost window and adding maintenance overhead. Option D is incorrect because Service Catalog requires building and curating products and does not by itself prevent users from launching EC2 instances directly, making it more effort than a simple preventive SCP.

639.text:
Option B is correct because writing orders to an SQS queue decouples ingestion from processing, allowing the application to durably buffer incoming orders during traffic spikes so none are lost. EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group behind an ALB then poll the queue and write to the Aurora database, scaling out based on queue depth to drain the backlog quickly and reliably. This pattern absorbs bursts and guarantees each order is processed exactly when capacity allows.

Option A is incorrect because increasing instance size does not durably buffer orders, and SNS is a pub/sub notification service whose database endpoints cannot subscribe to a topic. Option C is incorrect for the same reason: a database endpoint cannot be an SNS subscriber, and SNS does not persist messages for reliable later processing like a queue does. Option D is incorrect because conditionally queueing only at CPU thresholds risks dropping orders before the threshold, and scheduled scaling does not respond to actual queue backlog, harming reliability.

640.text:
Option D is correct because configuring SAML 2.0-based federation lets the company's existing Active Directory serve as the identity provider, so users authenticate with their existing corporate credentials and assume IAM roles to access AWS resources. Mapping AD groups to IAM roles with appropriate policies means users do not maintain a second identity, and their on-premises access is preserved. This scales cleanly to 1,500 users without creating individual AWS identities.

Option A is incorrect because creating an IAM user for each of the 1,500 users forces every user to maintain a separate AWS identity and credentials, which is exactly what the company wants to avoid and is operationally burdensome. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Cognito is designed for application end-user identity (web and mobile apps), not for federating corporate workforce access to the AWS Management Console and resources. Option C is incorrect because cross-account roles address access between AWS accounts, not federation of on-premises Active Directory identities into AWS.

641.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Cost Anomaly Detection uses machine learning to continuously monitor spending patterns and automatically identify unusual or unexpected cost spikes. When an anomaly is detected, it sends alerts to the responsible stakeholders through configured notifications, directly meeting the requirement to detect unusual spending and notify the team without requiring manual threshold tuning.

Option A is incorrect because a zero spend budget only alerts when any cost is incurred at all, which is meant to catch unintended spending in accounts expected to be free, not to detect anomalies on a workload that legitimately incurs ongoing EC2 costs. Option C is incorrect because the AWS Pricing Calculator only produces upfront cost estimates and does not monitor actual spending or send alerts. Option D is incorrect because Amazon CloudWatch is built for operational metrics and does not natively monitor billing for anomalous spend; Cost Anomaly Detection is the purpose-built service for this requirement.

642.text:
Option C is correct because integrating AWS Secrets Manager with Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL provides fully managed master user password rotation. RDS can manage the master credential directly in Secrets Manager, which supports built-in automatic rotation on a defined schedule such as every 30 days, securely storing and encrypting the password. This delivers the requirement with the least operational overhead because AWS handles the rotation logic without custom code.

Option A is incorrect because writing and maintaining a custom Lambda function triggered by EventBridge requires building, testing, and operating the rotation logic yourself, which adds significant operational overhead. Option B is incorrect because the modify-db-instance CLI command is a manual, one-time action with no built-in scheduling or automation for recurring 30-day rotation. Option D is incorrect because Systems Manager Parameter Store does not provide native automatic secret rotation; rotation would still require custom automation, unlike Secrets Manager's managed rotation capability designed specifically for RDS credentials.

643.text:
Option D is correct because a tag policy in AWS Organizations lets you define standardized tag keys and a list of approved values, such as allowed application names, and enforce compliance centrally across all accounts in the organization. Combined with enforcement, it prevents resources from being created or modified with non-compliant tag values, which precisely matches the requirement that resources only be created when the application name tag holds an approved value.

Option A is incorrect because an IAM group with a conditional Allow policy would have to be replicated and maintained per account and cannot enumerate an approved-value list centrally the way an Organizations tag policy can. Option B is incorrect because a cross-account Deny policy on resources that have the application name tag would block legitimate tagged resources rather than enforce approved values. Option C is incorrect because AWS Resource Groups only organizes and views resources by tags; it provides no enforcement to validate or restrict tag values at creation time.

644.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon RDS Proxy is a fully managed, highly available database proxy that pools and shares established connections to the RDS database. By placing RDS Proxy with a target group in front of the database and pointing the applications at the proxy endpoint, the application can scale dramatically during weekends and peak times without exhausting database connections, improving scalability and resiliency with the least operational overhead since AWS manages the proxy.

Option A is incorrect because DynamoDB is a separate NoSQL service, not a connection pooler for RDS, and switching to its endpoint would require rearchitecting the data layer. Option C is incorrect because running a custom proxy on EC2 means you must build, patch, scale, and operate that intermediary yourself, which is high operational overhead. Option D is incorrect because Lambda does not natively provide persistent connection pooling for a relational database; building pooling into Lambda is complex and is precisely the problem RDS Proxy was created to solve.

645.text:
Option D is correct because the performance problem occurs at the same predictable time each day, at the start of peak hours, before reactive scaling can respond. A scheduled scaling policy launches additional instances in advance of the known peak window so capacity is already provisioned and warm when demand arrives, eliminating the slow start while the application catches up. This directly addresses the timing of the issue.

Option A is incorrect because an Application Load Balancer only distributes traffic across existing instances and does not add capacity; the problem is insufficient capacity at peak onset, not uneven distribution. Options B and C are incorrect because dynamic scaling based on memory or CPU utilization is reactive: instances only launch after the metric crosses a threshold, then need time to boot and warm up, which is exactly the lag causing the slow start. Scheduled scaling proactively avoids that delay for a predictable peak.

646.text:
Options A and E are correct because the application is behind an Application Load Balancer, which requires a public ACM certificate to encrypt traffic in transit, and requesting both the apex domain example.com and a wildcard *.example.com certificate covers the root plus all country subdomains like country1.example.com. Option E completes the process by validating domain ownership through DNS validation, adding the required CNAME records to the DNS provider, which is the recommended method because it enables automatic certificate renewal.

Option B is incorrect because a private ACM certificate is issued by AWS Private CA for internal use and is not trusted by public clients reaching a public-facing ALB. Option C is incorrect because it requests a private certificate and omits coverage for the subdomains via a wildcard, failing the public encryption requirement. Option D is incorrect because it describes starting with email validation and then switching, which is unnecessary and convoluted; DNS validation alone, as in Option E, is the clean and renewable approach.

647.text:
Option C is correct because creating a read replica of the source RDS for PostgreSQL DB instance lets you redirect read-only query traffic to the replica, offloading work from the primary instance and improving overall application performance. RDS read replicas use asynchronous replication and provide a dedicated endpoint, allowing the read-heavy queries that are causing slowness to scale independently of the primary's write workload.

Option A is incorrect because the Multi-AZ standby replica cannot serve read traffic; it exists solely for failover high availability and remains passive until a failover occurs. Option B is incorrect because Transfer Acceleration is an Amazon S3 feature for speeding up uploads over long distances and has no applicability to RDS query performance. Option D is incorrect because Kinesis Data Firehose is a streaming data delivery service and does not increase database query concurrency or accelerate relational query processing; a read replica is the correct mechanism for scaling reads.

648.text:
Option C is correct because S3 Storage Lens provides organization-wide visibility into storage usage and activity, and it includes a built-in metric for incomplete multipart upload object counts and the bytes they consume. By enabling Storage Lens across the AWS Organizations accounts and Regions, the company gets centralized cost-compliance reporting on incomplete multipart uploads with the least operational overhead, since it aggregates the data automatically without custom code.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Config tracks resource configuration and compliance state but does not report counts of incomplete multipart uploads inside S3 buckets. Option B is incorrect because a service control policy is a preventive permission guardrail and cannot generate reports or metrics about storage contents. Option D is incorrect because an S3 Multi-Region Access Point provides a single global endpoint for routing requests across buckets in multiple Regions; it is a data access mechanism and does not produce reporting on incomplete multipart uploads.

649.text:
Option C is correct because a Provisioned IOPS SSD (io1/io2) Amazon EBS volume is designed to deliver durable, persistent block storage with consistent, low-latency I/O performance at a specified IOPS level. This makes it the right choice for a business-critical application that demands both durability and predictable low-latency performance, since the data persists independently of the instance lifecycle and the provisioned IOPS guarantee steady throughput.

Option A is incorrect because an instance store volume is ephemeral; its data is lost when the instance stops or terminates, so it is not durable for a business-critical workload. Option B is incorrect because ElastiCache for Memcached is an in-memory cache, not durable persistent storage, and data is volatile. Option D is incorrect because Throughput Optimized HDD (st1) is optimized for large, sequential throughput workloads like big data and log processing, not for the consistent low-latency, IOPS-sensitive performance that a Provisioned IOPS SSD volume provides.

650.text:
Options B and E are correct because Amazon EFS provides a fully managed, elastic NFS file system that multiple AWS resources can mount and access concurrently using the NFS protocol, satisfying the requirement for shared NFS access. AWS DataSync, with an agent installed on premises, performs an automated, secure, incremental transfer of the 200 GB from the NFS source to EFS without interrupting existing services, and it is cost-effective and reliable for one-time and ongoing migrations.

Option A is incorrect because FSx for Lustre is a high-performance file system for HPC and machine learning workloads, which is overkill and not the standard NFS multi-access solution needed here. Option C is incorrect because Amazon S3 is object storage and does not natively present an NFS file system to multiple resources. Option D is incorrect because manually using an OS copy command is error-prone, lacks resumability and validation, and risks service interruption compared to the managed, non-disruptive DataSync transfer.

651.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon CloudWatch Container Insights is purpose-built to collect, aggregate, and summarize metrics and logs from containerized environments such as Amazon EKS. It automatically gathers performance metrics at the cluster, node, pod, and container levels and ingests logs into CloudWatch Logs, giving a centralized view of the microservices in the CloudWatch console with minimal setup, exactly matching the monitoring requirement.

Option A is incorrect because the basic CloudWatch agent collects host and custom metrics but does not provide the automatic, summarized container and microservice-level metric and log aggregation that Container Insights delivers. Option B is incorrect because AWS App Mesh is a service mesh for managing service-to-service communication and traffic routing, not a centralized metrics and log aggregation solution, and it has no dedicated console for that purpose. Option C is incorrect because CloudTrail records API activity and management events, not application performance metrics and logs from microservices, so it cannot meet the monitoring requirement.

652.text:
Option D is correct because an AWS Storage Gateway volume gateway configured with cached volumes presents iSCSI block storage to the on-premises servers while storing the primary data durably in Amazon S3 and keeping a local cache of frequently accessed data. This provides low-latency access to hot data, reduces dependence on on-premises storage servers, and requires minimal infrastructure changes because applications continue to use the same iSCSI interface.

Option A is incorrect because an S3 File Gateway presents NFS or SMB file shares, not the iSCSI block protocol the existing servers use, which would require changes to how the data is accessed. Option B is incorrect because Amazon EBS is block storage attached to EC2 instances in AWS and does not provide an on-premises iSCSI target to offload the local servers. Option C is incorrect because stored volumes keep the entire primary dataset on premises and only back up to S3, so they do not reduce the on-premises storage footprint the way cached volumes do.

653.text:
Option A is correct because a Network Load Balancer operates at Layer 4 and is designed to handle TCP traffic at extreme scale, capable of processing millions of requests per second with ultra-low latency. It supports listening on arbitrary, nonstandard TCP ports and can be made publicly accessible, matching the existing hardware appliance's performance characteristics for a TCP-based application with the required throughput.

Option B is incorrect because an Application Load Balancer operates at Layer 7 for HTTP and HTTPS traffic and cannot load balance arbitrary TCP on a nonstandard port, nor is it optimized for raw TCP at this scale. Option C is incorrect because CloudFront is a content delivery network for HTTP/HTTPS web content and does not listen on arbitrary TCP ports for a generic TCP application. Option D is incorrect because API Gateway is built for HTTP and REST/WebSocket APIs, not raw TCP on a custom port, and the Lambda approach does not match the sustained 3 million TCP requests per second requirement.

654.text:
Option C is correct because launching On-Demand Instances with hibernation turned on and configuring EC2 Auto Scaling warm pools directly addresses slow startup and memory loading. A warm pool keeps pre-initialized instances in a stopped or hibernated state, so when scaling occurs the instances resume from a saved memory (RAM) state on disk rather than performing a cold boot and reloading application memory. This eliminates the lengthy launch and warm-up period, making instances fully productive almost immediately while controlling cost because warm pool instances are not billed at full running rates.

Option A is incorrect because plain Auto Scaling still launches instances from a cold state, so the long load time and memory warm-up persist for each new instance. Option B is incorrect because Spot Instances can be interrupted with little notice and do nothing to reduce application startup or memory-load time; they also risk unexpected termination. Option D is incorrect because Capacity Reservations only guarantee that capacity is available in an Availability Zone; they do not pre-warm the operating system or load application memory, so the slow launch behavior remains unchanged.

655.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) issues free public TLS certificates that integrate natively with CloudFront, and DNS validation enables fully automatic certificate renewal. Once the validation CNAME record is in place in Route 53 (or another DNS provider), ACM renews the certificate automatically before expiry with no manual action, giving the most operationally efficient way to enforce TLS on the distribution.

Option A is incorrect because a CloudFront security policy only defines the TLS protocol version and cipher suites a viewer can negotiate; it cannot create or manage certificates. Option B is incorrect because origin access control (OAC) is used to restrict access to an S3 or other origin so content is served only through CloudFront; it has nothing to do with certificate issuance. Option D is incorrect because, although ACM can issue the certificate, email validation requires a person to click a confirmation link for each renewal, which is a manual step that fails automation and is less operationally efficient than DNS validation.

656.text:
Option D is correct because Lambda SnapStart is purpose-built to reduce Java startup latency at no additional cost. SnapStart takes a snapshot of the initialized execution environment (memory and disk state) after the init phase, then restores from that cached snapshot when functions scale up. This dramatically lowers cold-start and outlier latencies for Java 11 functions without the per-hour charge of keeping environments pre-provisioned, matching the requirement to reduce cold starts most cost-effectively when there are no strict latency demands.

Option A is incorrect because provisioned concurrency keeps environments initialized and ready, but it incurs an ongoing hourly charge whether or not the functions are invoked, making it more expensive than SnapStart. Option B is incorrect because increasing the timeout only changes how long a function may run before being stopped; it has no effect on startup latency or cold starts. Option C is incorrect because adding memory raises cost and can slightly improve CPU-bound execution, but it does not meaningfully address JVM initialization cold-start latency and is not the targeted, cost-effective solution.

657.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Database Migration Service (AWS DMS) provides a low-downtime migration path from on-premises MySQL into Amazon Aurora, and Aurora MySQL-Compatible Edition is wire-compatible with MySQL so existing applications continue to work without code changes. Aurora Auto Scaling automatically adds and removes Aurora Replicas in response to load, satisfying the requirement to scale automatically during periods of increased demand.

Option A is incorrect because, while RDS for MySQL maintains compatibility, elastic storage scaling only grows the storage volume; it does not automatically scale compute capacity to handle increased transactional demand. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Redshift is a columnar data warehouse for analytics, not a transactional OLTP MySQL-compatible engine, so it would break application compatibility. Option D is incorrect because Amazon DynamoDB is a NoSQL key-value store that is not compatible with MySQL applications; migrating to it would require rewriting the data model and application queries.

658.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon EKS Connector lets you register external Kubernetes clusters, including self-managed on-premises clusters, with the Amazon EKS console. After registration, all connected clusters and their workloads appear in a single, central EKS console view alongside native EKS clusters, providing unified visibility with minimal setup and the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because CloudWatch Container Insights focuses on collecting performance metrics and logs rather than providing a central console to view and manage clusters and workloads, and configuring it across on-premises clusters adds overhead. Option C is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager is geared toward managing instances and operational tasks, not presenting a consolidated Kubernetes cluster and workload view. Option D is incorrect because EKS Anywhere is software for running Kubernetes on your own on-premises infrastructure; it is not a central dashboard for viewing multiple disparate clusters, and using native kubectl against each cluster does not provide a single consolidated location.

659.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon Macie is the AWS managed data security service that uses machine learning and pattern matching to automatically discover and classify sensitive data, including personally identifiable information (PII), in Amazon S3. Because Macie is a Regional service, you configure it in each Region (us-east-1 and us-west-2) and create discovery jobs against the S3 buckets, giving accurate PII detection with the least operational overhead since no custom logic is required.

Option B is incorrect because AWS Security Hub aggregates security findings and AWS Config evaluates resource configurations; neither inspects S3 object contents to discover PII. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Inspector performs vulnerability and network-reachability assessments on compute resources such as EC2 and container images, not content-level PII discovery in S3. Option D is incorrect because Amazon GuardDuty is a threat-detection service that analyzes logs and activity for malicious behavior; it does not classify or discover PII within S3 objects.

660.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Backup vault lock in compliance mode enforces write-once-read-many (WORM) protection that cannot be disabled or shortened by any user, including the account root user, for the duration of the retention period. This guarantees backups of EC2 and multiple S3 buckets cannot be altered or deleted before retention expires, which is exactly what the regulatory immutability requirement demands. AWS Backup centrally manages both EC2 and S3 backups through a single backup plan.

Option A is incorrect because vault lock in governance mode allows users with sufficient IAM permissions to remove the lock or delete recovery points, so it cannot guarantee immutability for regulatory compliance. Option B is incorrect because Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager automates EBS snapshots but does not provide immutable, tamper-proof retention or cover S3 buckets. Option C is incorrect because S3 File Gateway with lifecycle rules manages storage transitions and expiration but does not enforce immutable, non-deletable retention across EC2 and S3 the way a compliance-mode vault lock does.

661.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon VPC supports adding secondary IPv4 CIDR blocks to an existing VPC. By associating an additional CIDR block and creating new subnets from it, you immediately expand the pool of available IP addresses within the same VPC and can launch future workloads in the new subnets. This stays within one VPC, requires no peering, gateways, or routing complexity, and therefore has the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because creating a second VPC with a peering connection introduces additional routing, security group references, and cross-VPC management overhead that is unnecessary when the existing VPC can simply be extended. Option C is incorrect because adding a Transit Gateway to connect a second VPC adds a managed networking component and routing configuration, which is far more overhead than expanding the current VPC. Option D is incorrect because building a self-managed Site-to-Site VPN on EC2 between two VPCs is the most complex and operationally burdensome approach, requiring instance management and VPN maintenance.

662.text:
Option B is correct because a NAT gateway is a fully managed, highly available service that lets instances in private subnets initiate outbound connections to the internet (to reach the third-party catalog and pricing service) while preventing the internet from initiating inbound connections to those instances. By placing the NAT gateway in a public subnet and pointing the private subnet route table's default route to it, the MySQL instances retrieve external data securely without exposure, and AWS handles patching, scaling, and availability, so operational overhead does not increase.

Option A is incorrect because a NAT instance is a self-managed EC2 instance that you must patch, scale, and make highly available, which increases operational overhead compared with the managed NAT gateway. Option C is incorrect because routing private subnet traffic directly to an internet gateway would assign the instances public reachability and undermine security. Option D is incorrect because a virtual private gateway is used for VPN and Direct Connect connectivity to on-premises networks, not for outbound access to third-party internet endpoints.

663.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Storage Gateway's Tape Gateway exposes an iSCSI-based virtual tape library (VTL) interface that on-premises backup applications recognize as physical tape infrastructure. This lets the company eliminate physical tapes while preserving its existing backup applications and workflows unchanged, with virtual tapes stored durably and cost-effectively in Amazon S3 and archived to S3 Glacier classes, reducing backup costs and infrastructure complexity.

Option A is incorrect because, although Storage Gateway is the right service family, the NFS file interface (File Gateway) presents file shares rather than a tape library, so backup applications expecting tape devices would require reconfiguration and would not preserve existing tape workflows. Options B and C are incorrect because Amazon EFS is a managed NFS file system that does not emulate tape devices; EFS does not support an iSCSI interface at all, and neither variant preserves the company's tape-based backup applications and workflows.

664.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Global Accelerator improves performance for TCP and UDP traffic by routing users over the AWS global network from the nearest edge location to the optimal Regional endpoint, lowering latency and jitter for the gaming application. Global Accelerator integrates directly with Network Load Balancers across multiple Regions and lets you configure listener ports for both TCP and UDP, providing static anycast IP addresses and fast regional failover as the user base grows.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is optimized for cacheable HTTP/HTTPS content and does not accelerate raw TCP/UDP multiplayer game traffic; adjusting Cache-Control is irrelevant to dynamic gaming sessions. Option B is incorrect because Application Load Balancers operate at Layer 7 for HTTP/HTTPS and do not support UDP, so they cannot serve the UDP gaming traffic. Option D is incorrect because API Gateway is for HTTP/REST and WebSocket APIs and does not handle multiplayer TCP/UDP game traffic, and caching does not reduce real-time game latency.

665.text:
Option B is correct because migrating to Amazon RDS provides a fully managed relational database that reduces operational overhead by handling patching, backups, and maintenance, while configuring encryption at rest protects the sensitive transactional data. RDS encryption uses AWS KMS to encrypt the underlying storage, automated backups, snapshots, and read replicas, directly meeting both the increased-security and reduced-operational-overhead requirements.

Option A is incorrect because running databases on raw Amazon EC2 leaves the company responsible for installation, patching, backups, and high availability, which increases rather than reduces operational overhead even when a KMS key is used. Option C is incorrect because Amazon S3 with Macie is an object storage and data-discovery solution, not a transactional database engine, so it does not fit the relational transactional workload. Option D is incorrect because CloudWatch Logs provides monitoring and log storage, not data security or encryption for the database, so it does not satisfy the security requirement.

666.text:
Option D is correct because deploying the web and application tiers into a second Region and using an Amazon Aurora global database provides the most fault tolerance for global expansion. An Aurora global database uses dedicated storage-based replication to deploy the database in both the primary and secondary Regions with typically sub-second replication lag, and the secondary can be promoted to primary quickly during a Regional outage. Combined with Route 53 health checks and a failover routing policy, the architecture survives the loss of an entire Region with minimal downtime.

Option A is incorrect because extending Auto Scaling groups to a second Region's Availability Zones is not how AWS deploys cross-Region compute; Auto Scaling groups are Regional, so this does not provide a true second-Region stack. Option B is incorrect because a single cross-Region Aurora Replica offers less robust failover and replication management than a purpose-built global database. Option C is incorrect because using AWS DMS for ongoing cross-Region replication adds operational complexity and weaker durability and failover guarantees than an Aurora global database.

667.text:
Option A is correct because the AWS Cost Explorer API provides programmatic access to detailed usage and cost data and supports cost forecasting, letting the company retrieve current-year costs and forecast the next 12 months and feed them directly into its operations dashboard. Using pagination handles large result sets cleanly, and because it is a direct API call with no infrastructure to manage, it meets the requirements with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because downloadable .csv report files require manual export and import handling and do not provide the seamless programmatic, automated access the dashboard needs. Option C is incorrect because AWS Budgets actions are designed to trigger remediation or notifications on budget thresholds, not to deliver full usage cost and forecast data, and sending data over FTP adds insecure, custom plumbing. Option D is incorrect because AWS Budgets reports delivered over SMTP (email) are intended for human-readable notifications, not for programmatic ingestion of cost and forecast data into a dashboard.

668.text:
Option D is correct because creating an AMI, referencing it in a launch template, and placing it in an Auto Scaling group lets the stateless application scale out and in automatically based on demand, eliminating the single-instance bottleneck that causes 5xx errors at busy times. Using a Spot Fleet for the launch template minimizes compute cost, which suits a highly resilient, stateless workload that can tolerate instance replacement, and the attached Application Load Balancer distributes traffic across the dynamic fleet, delivering seamless, cost-effective scaling.

Option A is incorrect because launching a single additional On-Demand Instance behind an ALB provides only fixed capacity for two instances and does not scale seamlessly with variable load. Option B is incorrect for the same fixed-capacity reason, and Route 53 weighted routing distributes by ratio rather than reacting to demand. Option C is incorrect because stopping the instance to change its type causes downtime and only scales vertically to a hard limit, which does not provide seamless, continuous scaling for traffic spikes.

669.text:
Option D is correct because IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA) is the AWS-recommended mechanism to grant fine-grained, least-privilege IAM permissions to individual EKS pods. By mapping a dedicated Kubernetes service account to a specific IAM role for each workload, the UI pods can be scoped to DynamoDB access and the data services pods to S3 access, so each set of pods receives only the permissions it needs through the OIDC provider and temporary credentials.

Option A is incorrect because attaching both policies to the shared EC2 instance profile grants every pod on that node access to both services, and Kubernetes RBAC controls Kubernetes API actions, not AWS service access. Option B is incorrect because you cannot attach IAM policies directly to pods; IAM permissions must be delivered through an IAM role. Option C is incorrect because using broad AmazonS3FullAccess and AmazonDynamoDBFullAccess policies violates least privilege and, as written, also maps the wrong service to each workload rather than restricting access correctly.

670.text:
Option B is correct because an S3 gateway endpoint provides private connectivity to Amazon S3 directly from the VPC route table at no additional charge. Traffic to S3 is routed through the gateway endpoint and stays on the AWS network rather than traversing the internet, satisfying the security policy, and because gateway endpoints incur no hourly or data processing fees, this is the most cost-effective solution.

Option A is incorrect because an S3 interface endpoint (powered by PrivateLink) also keeps traffic private but charges hourly per endpoint and per gigabyte of data processed, making it less cost-effective than the free gateway endpoint for S3. Option C is incorrect because routing through the NAT gateway's Elastic IP sends traffic across the internet, which violates the security policy, and a bucket policy alone does not change the network path. Option D is incorrect because adding a second NAT gateway still routes traffic over the internet and increases cost, failing both the security and cost requirements.

671.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon S3 File Gateway presents S3 objects as a local NFS or SMB file share and caches frequently accessed data on premises, giving the rendering application low-latency file access while the data is durably and cost-effectively stored in S3. This lets the company keep its existing file-based application performance without re-architecting, and it is more cost-effective than provisioning a separate high-performance file system.

Option A is incorrect because Mountpoint for Amazon S3 is optimized for high-throughput read-heavy access patterns from within AWS and does not provide the local caching that an on-premises latency-sensitive application needs, so performance would suffer. Option C is incorrect because copying data into FSx for Windows File Server and using an FSx File Gateway adds the cost and management of a full Windows file system and duplicates storage, which is more expensive than an S3 File Gateway. Option D is incorrect because building a custom on-premises file server using the S3 API adds significant development and operational effort and would not reliably deliver low-latency caching.

672.text:
Option D is correct because DynamoDB global tables provide a fully managed, active-active, multi-Region replication solution that keeps user profiles, achievements, and transactions continuously available and resilient even if an entire Region fails. Pairing global tables with provisioned capacity mode and auto scaling lets the company match capacity to predictable gaming workloads while automatically adjusting throughput, which is more cost-effective at sustained, steady traffic than paying on-demand per-request pricing.

Option A is incorrect because, although it uses global tables, on-demand capacity mode is generally more expensive for predictable, sustained workloads than provisioned capacity with auto scaling, so it is not the most cost-effective choice. Option B is incorrect because DAX is an in-Region cache that does not provide cross-Region resilience, and manually configuring cross-Region replication is operationally burdensome and error-prone. Option C is incorrect because building cross-Region replication manually with DynamoDB Streams reinvents what global tables provide natively, adding complexity and risk while still incurring on-demand pricing.

673.text:
Option D is correct because provisioning two SQS queues decouples order collection from order fulfillment, ensuring no data is lost during scaling events because messages persist durably in the queue until processed. Scaling each Auto Scaling group on the number of messages in its queue (for example, using the ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric via a target tracking policy) lets the fast collection tier and the slower fulfillment tier scale independently and accurately based on actual backlog, which directly handles unpredictable peak traffic.

Option A is incorrect because fixing minimum capacity at peak values wastes money during off-peak times and CPU utilization alone does not reflect the queue backlog that drives fulfillment work. Option B is incorrect because CPU-based alarms that create new Auto Scaling groups on demand are operationally unworkable and do not protect against data loss. Option C is incorrect because, although it correctly uses two SQS queues, scaling merely on generic queue notifications rather than the actual number of messages does not provide precise, demand-driven scaling for each tier.

674.text:
Option D is correct because the processing tier already pulls work from an SQS queue, so an EC2 Auto Scaling target tracking policy based on the ApproximateNumberOfMessages metric scales the processing instances precisely in proportion to the backlog of queued orders. This reacts automatically to the variable, unpredictable peaks, adds capacity when the queue fills and CPU saturates, and removes it when the backlog clears, directly improving order-processing performance.

Option A is incorrect because scheduled scaling assumes known, time-based traffic patterns, but the peaks here are variable and unpredictable, so it cannot reliably add capacity at the right time. Option B is incorrect because ElastiCache for Redis accelerates DynamoDB reads, but the bottleneck is the processing tier saturating at 100% CPU with a growing queue, not database read latency. Option C is incorrect because CloudFront caches static web-tier responses and does not relieve the backend processing tier that is overloaded, nor does HTTP latency reflect the SQS backlog driving the work.

675.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Batch is a fully managed service that dynamically provisions and scales the compute needed to run large numbers of batch image-processing jobs, removing the need to manage the underlying infrastructure. Orchestrating the workflow with AWS Step Functions automates the multi-step process and reduces manual tasks, while storing the large processed files in Amazon S3 provides durable, cost-effective, virtually unlimited storage, meeting the requirements with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because, although ECS with SQS can process images, managing the ECS cluster and EC2 Spot capacity and storing large files in EFS adds infrastructure management and higher storage cost compared with the serverless Batch and S3 approach. Option C is incorrect because Lambda has execution duration and resource limits that are poorly suited to large file processing, and mixing in self-managed EC2 Spot Instances and FSx increases operational overhead. Option D is incorrect because deploying and managing a fixed group of EC2 instances and EBS volumes requires significant manual infrastructure management and does not scale automatically.

676.text:
Option A is correct because adding an Amazon CloudFront distribution in front of the S3 bucket caches images at edge locations close to users worldwide, dramatically improving download performance with minimal configuration. Enabling S3 Transfer Acceleration routes uploads through the nearest CloudFront edge location and over the optimized AWS backbone to the bucket, improving upload performance for globally distributed users. Both are simple managed features that require the least implementation effort.

Option B is incorrect because migrating the static site to EC2 instances across multiple Regions behind load balancers with Global Accelerator is a major re-architecture with high implementation and operational effort. Option C is incorrect because manually creating multiple Regional buckets, configuring replication rules, and redirecting downloads by location adds substantial complexity compared with simply using CloudFront and Transfer Acceleration. Option D is incorrect because AWS Global Accelerator does not front Amazon S3 buckets for static website content the way described; CloudFront with Transfer Acceleration is the appropriate and far simpler solution for S3-hosted content.

677.text:
Option A is correct because the workload has two distinct, predictable patterns. The instances that run constantly Monday through Saturday are a steady, always-on load best covered by Standard Reserved Instances, which provide the deepest discount for committed, continuous usage. The Sunday workload runs on a fixed recurring 12-hour schedule and cannot tolerate interruptions, which matches Scheduled Reserved Instances that reserve capacity for a defined recurring time window, making this the most cost-effective combination.

Option B is incorrect because Convertible Reserved Instances offer a smaller discount than Standard Reserved Instances for the constant Monday-through-Saturday workload, so using Convertible for the Sunday portion and only Standard for the rest is not optimal here. Options C and D are incorrect because Spot Instances can be interrupted with little notice, which violates the explicit requirement that the Sunday workload cannot tolerate interruptions; the recurring fixed-schedule, interruption-intolerant Sunday usage is better matched by Scheduled Reserved Instances.
